qa.question: What is the purpose of the predicate `play_228`?
qa.answer: The predicate `play_228` is used to skip to the first uppercase letter in a string and then copy that letter to the output.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the predicate `play_228` is used in the solution to the build task `build_95`. The predicate `play_228` is defined recursively and it calls the predicate `play_52`, which is used to skip to the first uppercase letter in a string. The predicate `play_228` then calls the predicate `copy` to copy the uppercase letter to the output.
qa.student: The predicate `play_228` is used to convert letters to uppercase in the input and output them. It mainly implements the uppercasing operation needed in the build task.
qa.feedback: You are right that `play_228` is related to uppercase letters, but you are missing its main conceptual role. The caption states that `play_228` is a recursive definition corresponding to “skip to the first uppercase letter and then copy the letter to the output.” So its key purpose is not just to uppercase characters; it recursively skips over input until it reaches an already uppercase letter and then outputs that letter. The actual uppercasing operation is handled elsewhere (e.g., by `mk_uppercase` in `play_136_1`), while `play_228` focuses on navigating to and copying the first uppercase letter.

qa.question: Which metarule represents a chain of reasoning where one rule leads to another?
qa.answer: The chain metarule.
qa.explanation: The chain metarule has the form P(A, B) :- Q(A, C), R(C, B). This means that in order to prove P(A, B), we must first prove Q(A, C) and then prove R(C, B). This is a chain of reasoning because the proof of one rule (Q(A, C)) leads to the proof of another rule (R(C, B)).
qa.student: The chain metarule.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

The source entity context vector and the target entity context vector are concatenated together to be sent to the prediction layer. The prediction layer predicts 1 if the source to target blame tie exists otherwise 0.

qa.question: Which model performs best on the KNOWN data?

The combined model has the highest dev F1 (81.75) and a competitive test F1 (68.67), so it is considered the best-performing model on KNOWN data. You should interpret “best” in context of the full set of reported metrics, not just the single largest test value.

qa.question: Which model performed better on the dev/test sets?

qa.feedback: You are misreading the table counts. The cells “413/406” and “31/19” are not totals for CMB vs. CTX; they are joint outcomes (how often each model is correct or wrong on the same examples). To see which model is better, you need to compare how many times each model is correct overall: CTX is correct in 31+413 dev examples and 19+406 test examples, while CMB is correct in 98+413 dev and 127+406 test examples. CMB has more correct predictions than CTX on both sets, so CMB is the better-performing model.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the LSTM layer in the context model?
qa.answer: The LSTM layer is used to encode the sentences.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the LSTM layer takes in words/entity embedding as input and outputs hidden vectors. These hidden vectors are then pooled together to represent the context of the entity.
qa.student: The LSTM layer processes the sequence of word and entity embeddings in the sentence so that each token is encoded with information from its surrounding context.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that the LSTM encodes the sequence context, but you stop short of explaining its specific role in this model. From the figure and caption, you also need to mention that the hidden vectors at the positions of the source and target entities are taken from the LSTM outputs, pooled into separate context vectors for each entity, and then concatenated and fed to the prediction layer. In other words, the LSTM is not just encoding the sentence; it is providing the entity-position hidden states that become the entity context representations used for blame prediction.

1904.11251v1-Table2-1.png.caption: Novel, F1 and Accuracy scores of our proposed model and other state-of-the-art methods on ImageNet dataset. All values are reported as percentage (%).

1904.11251v1-Figure2-1.png.caption: Objects and sentence generation results on held-out COCO. The detected objects are predicted by MIL model in [7], and the output sentences are generated by 1) Ground Truth (GT): one ground truth sentence, 2) LRCN and 3) our LSTM-P.

1904.11251v1-Figure3-1.png.caption: Objects and sentence generation results on ImageNet. GT denotes the ground truth object. The detected objects are predicted by the standard CNN architecture [23], and the output sentences are generated by 1) LRCN and 2) our LSTM-P.

1904.11251v1-Table1-1.png.caption: Per-object F1, averaged F1, SPICE, METEOR, and CIDEr scores of our proposed model and other state-of-the-art methods on held-out COCO dataset for novel object captioning. All values are reported as percentage (%).

1904.11251v1-Figure1-1.png.caption: An overview of our Long Short-Term Memory with Pointing (LSTM-P) for novel object captioning (better viewed in color). The image representation extracted by CNN is firstly injected into LSTM at the initial time for triggering the standard word-by-word sentence generation. The output of LSTM is the probability distribution over all the words in the vocabulary at each decoding time. Meanwhile, the object learners pre-trained on object recognition data are utilized to detect the objects within the input image. Such predicted score distribution over objects are further injected into a copying layer along with the current hidden state of LSTM, producing the probability distribution of being copied over the recognized objects. To dynamically accommodate word generation via LSTM and word copying from learnt objects, a pointing mechanism is specially devised to elegantly point when to copy the object depending on contextual information (i.e., current input word and LSTM hidden state). The whole LSTM-P is trained by minimizing two objectives in an end-to-end manner: (1) the widely-adopted sequential loss that enforces the syntactic coherence of output sentence, and (2) the sentence-level coverage loss that encourages the maximum coverage of all objects found in the image, which is independent of the position in the sentence.

1904.11251v1-Figure4-1.png.caption: Sentence generation results with visualized weights learnt in pointer mechanism of our LSTM-P at each decoding step on held-out COCO dataset. The bar plot at each decoding step corresponds to the weights for generating a word via LSTM or copying a word from recognized objects when the corresponding word was generated.

1904.11251v1-Figure5-1.png.caption: The effect of the tradeoff parameter λ in our LSTM-P over (a) F1average (%) and (b) METEOR (%) on held-out COCO.

qa.question: Which of the three models generated the most accurate sentence for the image of the woman on the tennis court?
qa.answer: The Ground Truth (GT) model generated the most accurate sentence for the image of the woman on the tennis court.
qa.explanation: The GT model correctly identified the woman as a tennis player and described her as walking on a tennis court holding a tennis racket. The LRCN model incorrectly identified the woman as a young boy holding a baseball bat, and the LSTM-P model correctly identified the woman as a tennis player but did not mention that she was walking.
qa.student: The LRCN model generated the most accurate sentence for the image of the woman on the tennis court.
qa.feedback: You misread which caption best matches the image. The image clearly shows a tennis player with a racket on a tennis court. The LRCN sentence talks about “a young boy holding a baseball bat on a court,” which is wrong in both the person (boy vs. woman/tennis player) and the object (baseball bat vs. tennis racket). The LSTM-P caption, “a tennis player holding a racket on a court,” correctly describes the scene, so LSTM-P is the most accurate of the three.

qa.question: Which model performed better at generating sentences that describe the images?
qa.answer: LSTM-P performed better at generating sentences that describe the images.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that LSTM-P generated more accurate and detailed sentences than LRCN for all three images. For example, LSTM-P correctly identified the man in the first image as sitting on a lawnmower, while LRCN incorrectly stated that he was walking down a road.
qa.student: LSTM-P performed better.
qa.feedback: You only state which model did better but don’t explain how you know from the figure. To fully answer, you need to mention that LSTM-P’s generated sentences more accurately match the ground truth descriptions: for each image, its sentence correctly includes the main object (like “lawnmower,” “orangutan,” “abacus”) and gives a more appropriate description than LRCN’s sentence.

qa.question: Which model performed the best overall on the held-out COCO dataset for novel object captioning?
qa.answer: LSTM-P
qa.explanation: The figure shows that LSTM-P achieved the highest average F1 score (60.9%), as well as the highest SPICE (23.4%), METEOR (23.4%), and CIDEr (88.3%) scores.
qa.student: The KGA-CGM model performed the best overall because it has the highest F1 score on several individual objects like couch and microwave.
qa.feedback: You are focusing on a few per-object F1 scores instead of how “overall” performance is defined in the table. Overall here should be judged by the aggregate metrics: F1_average, SPICE, METEOR, and CIDEr. LSTM-P has the highest F1_average (60.9) and also the highest or tied-highest values in SPICE, METEOR, and CIDEr among all models. Even if another model is best on some individual objects, the one with the best combined aggregate metrics is considered the best overall, so the correct answer is LSTM-P, not KGA-CGM.

qa.question: What is the role of the pointing mechanism in the LSTM-P model?
qa.answer: The pointing mechanism decides whether to copy a word from the recognized objects or to generate a word from the LSTM based on the contextual information (i.e., current input word and LSTM hidden state).
qa.explanation: The pointing mechanism takes the current input word and the LSTM hidden state as input and outputs a probability distribution over the recognized objects. This distribution is then used to decide whether to copy a word from the objects or to generate a word from the LSTM.
qa.student: The pointing mechanism decides which object in the image to mention next by picking the object label with the highest score from the object learners and feeding that as the next word in the sentence.
qa.feedback: You are right that the pointing mechanism is related to using detected objects as words, but you misread its exact role. It does not simply pick the single highest‑scoring object label. Instead, it computes a probability pt that controls how much to rely on the LSTM’s normal word distribution versus the copying layer’s object distribution. These two distributions are then combined (scaled by 1−pt and pt) to form the final word probabilities. So its main function is to decide when to copy from recognized objects versus when to generate words from the standard vocabulary, not to choose one specific object directly.

qa.question: How does the LSTM-P model use its pointer mechanism to generate sentences based on the images it is provided?
qa.answer: The LSTM-P model uses its pointer mechanism to generate sentences by either generating a word via LSTM or copying a word from recognized objects in the image. The weights for these two options are visualized in the bar plot at each decoding step.
qa.explanation: The figure shows two examples of how the LSTM-P model generates sentences. In the first example, the model generates the sentence "A tennis player is hitting a ball with a racket." The bar plot at each decoding step shows the weights for generating a word via LSTM (purple bars) and copying a word from recognized objects (blue bars). For example, at the first decoding step, the weight for generating the word "a" via LSTM is 1.0, while the weight for copying the word "tennis" from the recognized object is 0.93. This means that the model is more likely to generate the word "a" via LSTM than to copy the word "tennis" from the recognized object.
qa.student: The LSTM-P model uses its pointer mechanism to decide at each decoding step whether to generate a word from the LSTM or to copy a word from the detected objects in the image. The bar plots show that for some words, like “tennis” or “truck,” the model assigns higher weight to copying from objects, while for other words it relies more on the LSTM.
qa.feedback: You correctly describe that the pointer mechanism trades off between generating a word from the LSTM and copying a word from recognized objects, and that the bars visualize these weights. However, you do not explain how this behavior varies across the sentence and why that matters. The figure shows that content words tied to visual entities (e.g., “tennis,” “racket,” “truck,” “street,” “bus”) get high copy weights, while function or generic words (“a,” “with,” “driving,” “down,” “next,” “to”) rely mainly on the LSTM. This pattern illustrates that the model grounds object-related nouns in the image via copying, and uses the language model part to fluently connect them into a grammatical sentence. Including this distinction would make your explanation complete.

qa.question: What is the effect of the tradeoff parameter λ on F1_average and METEOR?
qa.answer: The effect of the tradeoff parameter λ on F1_average and METEOR is that it is non-monotonic. As λ increases, both F1_average and METEOR initially increase, then decrease, and then increase again.
qa.explanation: The figure shows two plots, one for F1_average and one for METEOR. The x-axis of each plot is the tradeoff parameter λ, and the y-axis is the corresponding F1_average or METEOR score. The plots show that the relationship between λ and F1_average and METEOR is not simple.
qa.student: As λ increases, both F1_average and METEOR consistently improve. This means using a larger λ always gives better performance on both metrics.
qa.feedback: You are assuming performance increases monotonically with λ, but the plots show a peak and then a decline. For F1_average, scores rise as λ increases from 0 to about 0.3–0.4, then steadily drop as λ grows further. METEOR similarly increases up to around λ ≈ 0.2, then generally decreases, with some small fluctuations at higher λ. So larger λ is not always better; there is an intermediate λ that yields the best performance on both metrics.

qa.question: What is the sentiment transfer task?
qa.answer: The sentiment transfer task is the process of changing the sentiment of a sentence from negative to positive or positive to negative, while preserving the factual content of the sentence.
qa.explanation: The table shows examples of the sentiment transfer task, where the original sentence is either negative or positive, and the human annotated sentence has the opposite sentiment.
qa.student: The sentiment transfer task is about translating sentences from one language to another while keeping the same emotional tone.
qa.feedback: You are confusing sentiment transfer with machine translation. In this figure all sentences are in the same language (English) and differ in polarity: negative vs. positive. The sentiment transfer task keeps the original content (like place, food, service details) but changes the sentiment polarity, for example converting a negative Yelp review into a positive one, or vice versa, rather than translating between languages.

The highest accuracy is 97.7% in the “Sentiment + Length↑” row, not in “Sentiment + Keywords.” Carefully match each accuracy value with its corresponding row label to avoid mixing up which attribute set gives that performance.

qa.question: What is the effect of adding sentiment and keywords to the original text?
qa.answer: Adding sentiment and keywords to the original text can change the overall sentiment of the text.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the original text can be either positive or negative, depending on the sentiment and keywords that are added. For example, the first row of the table shows that the original text "i love this place" can be changed to "i love this place too" or "i was very disappointed with this place" by adding sentiment and keywords.
qa.student: Adding sentiment and keywords to the original text mainly just appends the bolded keyword while keeping the rest of the sentence the same, so the meaning doesn’t really change; it only highlights the keyword without affecting sentiment or style much.
qa.feedback: You are right that the bolded keyword is explicitly inserted, but you are misunderstanding how much the meaning and sentiment change. When sentiment and keywords are added, the model often rewrites the sentence so that its polarity flips (negative→positive or positive→negative) and the overall content becomes consistent with the new sentiment. It is not just highlighting a word; the surrounding phrasing is altered (e.g., “very disappointed with this place” becomes variations of “I love this place” when controlling for positive sentiment). Pay attention to how whole clauses are replaced or rephrased to match the new sentiment and incorporate the keyword naturally, not just appended.

qa.question: What is the relationship between the encoder, decoder, and the error surface of the predictor in the figure?
qa.answer: The encoder takes an input sentence and maps it to a continuous representation in the latent space. The decoder then takes this representation and generates a new sentence. The error surface of the predictor shows how well the predictor is able to predict the sentiment, length, and content of the generated sentence. The goal is to find a representation in the latent space that minimizes the error of the predictor.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the encoder, decoder, and the error surface of the predictor. The encoder and decoder are connected by a dashed line, which represents the latent space. The error surface of the predictor is shown as a 3D plot, with the axes representing the sentiment, length, and content of the generated sentence. The black dots represent the original sentence and the target sentence, and the arrows show how the representation is revised in the latent space to minimize the error of the predictor.
qa.student: The encoder takes an input sentence and maps it to a continuous representation in the latent space. The decoder then takes this representation and generates a new sentence. The error surface of the predictor shows how well the predictor is able to predict the sentiment, length, and content of the generated sentence. The goal is to find a representation in the latent space that minimizes the error of the predictor.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: How do the different models compare in terms of their ability to preserve the original meaning of the sentence?
qa.answer: The "Ours (balanced)" model appears to preserve the original meaning of the sentence the best, while the "Ours (content-strengthen)" and "Ours (style-strengthen)" models sometimes introduce extraneous information or change the meaning of the sentence slightly.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the "Ours (balanced)" model produces translations that are most similar to the original sentences, while the other models sometimes produce translations that are more different from the original sentences.
qa.student: BackTranslation preserves the original meaning best in almost all examples, while all of the “Ours” variants change the meaning a lot when they alter the gender, so they are worse at content preservation.
qa.feedback: You are misreading how much meaning actually changes in the examples. BackTranslation often changes content noticeably (e.g., “if you do come for breakfast get a croissant” becomes “if you are looking for lunch, has a stems”, which shifts both meal time and dish). In contrast, several “Ours” outputs, especially content-strengthen and balanced, keep the main meaning very close (e.g., “the only thing worth mentioning is their dessert” is almost unchanged; “we went here for my fiancé’s birthday” becomes “went here for my wife’s birthday,” where the event and sentiment are preserved). You should compare each output to the “Original” line and see that the proposed models frequently preserve core meaning as well or better than BackTranslation rather than “changing the meaning a lot” in most cases.

qa.question: Which model is the most effective at transferring sentiment from negative to positive?
qa.answer: Our (style-strengthen) model is the most effective at transferring sentiment from negative to positive.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the Our (style-strengthen) model produces the most human-like sentences when transferring sentiment from negative to positive. For example, the original sentence "ridiculous ! ! i had no trouble getting it on with zero bubbles." is transferred to "i have no trouble putting bubbles on it." by the Our (style-strengthen) model, which is very close to the human-annotated sentence "i have no trouble putting bubbles on it.".
qa.student: The “Ours (style-strengthen)” model is the most effective.
qa.feedback: You only named one model without explaining why it is more effective based on the examples. To fully answer, you need to compare how well each model changes the sentiment while keeping the original content, using the shown sentences (CrossAligned, MultiDecoder, DeleteAndRetrieve, BackTranslation, and the three “Ours” variants). Point out that in several negative-to-positive cases, multiple models successfully flip sentiment, and you must justify which performs best overall rather than just stating a model name.

qa.question: Which model performs best in terms of BLEU score on the Yelp dataset?
qa.answer: Our (style-strengthen) model performs best in terms of BLEU score on the Yelp dataset.
qa.explanation: The BLEU score is a measure of how well a machine-generated text matches a human-generated text. The higher the BLEU score, the better the match. The table shows that Our (style-strengthen) model has the highest BLEU score on the Yelp dataset, which is 61.5.
qa.student: The model “Ours (style-strengthen)” performs best on Yelp in terms of BLEU score.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up model families and not focusing on the BLEU column itself. BLEU↑ means higher values are better, regardless of whether a model is labeled “Ours” or comes from prior work. On the Yelp table, the highest BLEU among the non-Human, non-Original systems is from the “StyleEmbedding (Fu et al. 2018)” model (31.9), not any of the “Ours” variants. You need to compare all numbers in the BLEU column and then pick the numerically largest one.

qa.question: What is the relationship between the LSC and the frequency of the words in the two corpora?
qa.answer: The LSC is negatively correlated with the frequency of the words in the two corpora. This means that words with a higher LSC tend to have a lower frequency in the two corpora.
qa.explanation: The LSC is a measure of how much the frequency of a word has changed between two corpora. A negative LSC indicates that the frequency of the word has decreased in the second corpus compared to the first corpus. The table shows that the words with the highest LSC (e.g., "Abend", "Museum") have the lowest frequencies in both corpora. Conversely, the words with the lowest LSC (e.g., "Vorwort", "Donnerwetter") have the highest frequencies in both corpora.
qa.student: Words with higher LSC values tend to have higher frequencies in both corpora. For example, “Vorwort” and “Donnerwetter” have relatively high LSC and also relatively high frequencies in Ca and Cb, while words with lower LSC values like “Museum” and “Abend” have lower frequencies.
qa.feedback: You are misreading the relationship between LSC and frequency. LSC here ranges from about –1.6 to –3.8, where “higher” LSC means a value closer to zero (e.g., –1.58 is higher than –3.79). When you compare these values with the frequencies, there is no consistent pattern that higher LSC corresponds to higher frequency in either Ca or Cb. Some items with relatively high LSC (e.g., “Vorwort”, freq_Ca = 85) are actually less frequent than items with lower LSC (e.g., “Abend”, freq_Ca = 4144). Since high LSC indicates high semantic change, you should conclude that frequency and LSC do not show a clear monotonic relationship in this table, rather than that higher LSC means higher frequency.

qa.student: The word with the highest frequency in the COOK corpus is “Salz,” with a frequency of 5806.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified “Salz” as having the highest frequency in the COOK (Cb) column. However, your answer omits how you determined this from the table. To fully answer the question, you should mention that you compared the freq. Cb values for all listed lexemes and found that Salz has the largest number (5806), which shows it is the most frequent word in the COOK corpus.

qa.question: Which alignment methods are compatible with all semantic representations?
qa.answer: Only the CI (centroid initialization) and OP (orthogonal Procrustes) alignment methods are compatible with all semantic representations.
qa.explanation: The table in the figure shows which alignment methods are compatible with which semantic representations. The CI and OP columns have an "X" in every row, indicating that they are compatible with all semantic representations.
qa.student: The CI and SRV alignment methods are compatible with all semantic representations, since they appear in multiple rows across the table.
qa.feedback: You are confusing “appearing in multiple rows” with “compatible with all representations.” To be compatible with all semantic representations, an alignment method’s column would need an x in every row of the semantic representation section. In the table, no alignment column (CI, SRV, OP, VI, or WI) has x’s for every representation (raw count, PPMI, SVD, RI, SGNS, SCAN). CI is only marked for raw count; SRV only for PPMI; OP, VI, and WI only for SVD or SGNS. Therefore, there is actually no alignment method that is compatible with all semantic representations.

qa.question: Which representation and dataset combination achieved the highest best ρ score?
qa.answer: SGNS and DUREL

qa.feedback: You are misunderstanding how to compare the ρ scores. You must look for the numerically largest best ρ value, not just whether it is positive. In the table, SCAN has very low best scores (0.327 on DURel and 0.082 on SURel). The highest best ρ overall is 0.866 for the SGNS representation on the DURel dataset, which is larger than all other best values in both datasets.

qa.question: How does time-shuffling and downampling affect the value of ρ for SGNS+OP+CD on the DU Rel and SU Rel datasets?
qa.answer: Time-shuffling decreases the value of ρ for both datasets, while downampling further decreases the value of ρ for both datasets.
qa.explanation: The table shows the value of ρ for SGNS+OP+CD on the DU Rel and SU Rel datasets before and after time-shuffling and downampling. For the DU Rel dataset, the value of ρ decreases from 0.816 to 0.180 after time-shuffling and to 0.372 after downampling. For the SU Rel dataset, the value of ρ decreases from 0.767 to 0.763 after time-shuffling and to 0.576 after downampling.
qa.student: For DU Rel, time-shuffling slightly increases ρ from 0.816 to 0.880, and downsampling brings it to 0.372. For SU Rel, shuffling keeps ρ almost the same, going from 0.767 to 0.763, and downsampling reduces it further to 0.576.
qa.feedback: You correctly noted that shuffling has little effect on SU Rel and that downsampling reduces ρ on both datasets, but you misread the DU Rel value after shuffling. In the table, DU Rel goes from 0.816 (ORG) down to 0.180 (SHF), not up to 0.880. Since ρ is a correlation measure, lower values mean weaker correlation, so time-shuffling strongly decreases ρ on DU Rel, and downsampling then raises it somewhat to 0.372, but it is still well below the original 0.816. Be careful to copy the numbers accurately from the table.

qa.question: Which batch selection algorithm resulted in the highest average embedding magnitude in the selected batch?
qa.answer: k-means++
qa.explanation: The plot on the right shows that the average embedding magnitude for k-means++ is consistently higher than the other algorithms.
qa.student: The k-means++ batch selection algorithm resulted in the highest average embedding magnitude.

The figure shows that k-means++ consistently selects batches with higher average embedding magnitude than k-DPP, Rand, and especially FF k-center across most of the labeling process, while FF k-center tends to pick the lowest‑magnitude points. Including this comparison would show a fuller understanding of what “highest” means in the context of the plot.

qa.question: Which of the two algorithms (k-DPP or k-means++) achieves higher accuracy on the MNIST dataset with an MLP model?
qa.answer: k-DPP achieves higher accuracy than k-means++ on the MNIST dataset with an MLP model.
qa.explanation: The plots on the left show the accuracy of the two algorithms for different batch sizes. For both batch sizes, the k-DPP curve is above the k-means++ curve, indicating that k-DPP achieves higher accuracy.
qa.student: k-means++ achieves higher accuracy than k-DPP on MNIST with the MLP model, because its curve is above the k-DPP curve as more labels are queried.
qa.feedback: You are misinterpreting the accuracy curves. On both batch sizes (100 and 1000), the k-DPP (gray) and k-means++ (blue) accuracy lines essentially overlap; neither method consistently lies above the other. That means they achieve almost identical accuracy for any number of queried labels. Your reasoning treats a visually indistinguishable overlap as if one method were clearly better, which is a conceptual mistake about how to read such plots.

The gray k-DPP curve is consistently above the blue k-means++ curve at the same number of labels, so k-DPP requires more time, not k-means++.

The figure shows two algorithms, k-DPP and k-means++, and for CIFAR10 with an MLP and batch size 1000, k-means++ achieves slightly higher accuracy than k-DPP across the queried labels. You need to clearly state that k-means++ is more accurate than k-DPP in this setting.

qa.question: How does the batch size affect the accuracy of the model?
qa.answer: The batch size affects the accuracy of the model in a non-monotonic way. For small batch sizes (e.g., 10), the accuracy is lower than for larger batch sizes (e.g., 1000). However, for very large batch sizes (e.g., 10000), the accuracy decreases again.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the accuracy of the model for different batch sizes. For small batch sizes, the model is not able to learn effectively because it does not have enough data to train on. For large batch sizes, the model may overfit to the training data and not generalize well to new data.
qa.student: Larger batch sizes clearly give higher accuracy than smaller batch sizes, because the rightmost plot (batch size 10,000) reaches the highest accuracy, while the smallest batch size (100) stays noticeably lower.
qa.feedback: You are assuming that higher batch size automatically leads to better accuracy, but the curves do not support that. All three batch sizes eventually reach roughly the same top accuracy (around 0.9+), so larger batches do not give a higher final accuracy. The main effect of batch size here is on how quickly that accuracy is reached in terms of labels queried (x‑axis): with batch size 10,000 the model jumps to high accuracy very early, while batch size 100 improves more gradually. You need to distinguish between “final level of accuracy” (similar across plots) and “speed of reaching it” (different across batch sizes).

qa.question: How does the batch size affect the accuracy of the model?
qa.answer: The batch size does not seem to have a significant impact on the accuracy of the model.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the accuracy of the model is similar for all three batch sizes. This suggests that the batch size does not have a significant impact on the accuracy of the model.
qa.student: Larger batch sizes always give higher accuracy. The plot shows that with a batch size of 10,000, the model reaches the highest accuracy for the same number of labels queried compared to batch sizes 100 and 1000.
qa.feedback: You are right that batch size changes the accuracy curves, but you misread how. Accuracy is on the y-axis (higher means better), and for most of the x-range the curves for batch size 100 and 1000 are as high as or slightly higher than those for 10,000, not lower. The batch size 10,000 plot only shows three points and does not clearly dominate; at comparable numbers of labels queried (e.g., around 10,000–15,000), the accuracy is similar or sometimes worse than with smaller batch sizes. So it is not true that larger batch sizes always give higher accuracy in this figure.

qa.question: Which algorithm performed the best overall, according to the figure?
qa.answer: Rand
qa.explanation: The column-wise averages at the bottom of each plot show the aggregate performance of each algorithm, with lower values indicating better performance. The Rand algorithm has the lowest average values across all three batch sizes, indicating that it performed the best overall.
qa.student: The Conf algorithm performed the best overall.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified Conf as strong, but you did not justify this using the information the figure emphasizes. To fully answer, you should refer to the column-wise averages at the bottom of each matrix (where lower is better) across all three batch sizes. Those averages show that Conf has the lowest overall penalty compared with the other algorithms at each batch size, which is why it is the best performer. Without citing this evidence from the figure, your answer is incomplete.

qa.question: Which algorithm performed the best overall across all neural network models?
qa.answer: Coreset
qa.explanation: The figure shows the pairwise penalty matrices of the algorithms, grouped by different neural network models. The column-wise averages at the bottom show the aggregate performance of each algorithm. Coreset has the lowest average score across all neural network models, indicating that it performed the best overall.
qa.student: The best overall algorithm is the one with the darkest colors (highest values) across the matrices, which is Coreset, because it tends to beat the other methods most often in all three neural network models.
qa.feedback: You correctly looked at the heatmaps, but you reversed what “better” means in this figure. The caption states that the column-wise averages at the bottom indicate aggregate performance and that lower is better, not higher. The main matrices also count how many times algorithm i beats j, so larger entries mean i wins more often. To find the best overall method, you should focus on which algorithm has the smallest column-wise averages across MLP, ResNet, and VGG, not the largest or the darkest cells. You mixed up the direction of the performance measure, which led you to pick the wrong algorithm.

qa.question: Which algorithm has the best performance for a batch size of 1000?
qa.answer: ALBL
qa.explanation: The CDF of ALBL is the highest for a batch size of 1000, which indicates that it has the best performance.
qa.student: For a batch size of 1000, BADGE has the best performance.

For batch size 1000, you need to compare all curves: higher CDF values at the same normalized error indicate better performance. Several algorithms (e.g., ALBL and BADGE, and also Rand at higher errors) are very close and often overlap, so you should describe that multiple methods perform similarly well instead of singling out only one without justification.

qa.question: Which acquisition function consistently outperforms the others across all three neural network models?
qa.answer: ALBL
qa.explanation: The ALBL curve is consistently higher than the other curves in all three plots, indicating that it has a higher cumulative frequency of lower normalized errors.
qa.student: The random (Rand) acquisition function consistently outperforms the others across all three neural network models.
qa.feedback: You are misunderstanding what “higher CDF indicates better performance” means in these plots. For each normalized error value on the x-axis, the best method is the one with the highest curve (largest cumulative frequency) at that x-value. The Rand curve (purple) is often below the others, meaning fewer runs achieve low error compared with other methods, so it is generally worse, not better. If you compare across all three models, the BADGE and ALBL curves are typically on top over most of the low-error region, so those methods are the ones that consistently outperform the others.

qa.question: How does the batch size affect the learning curves of the different active learning algorithms?
qa.answer: The batch size has a significant impact on the learning curves of the different active learning algorithms. For example, with a batch size of 100, ALBL and Conf perform similarly, while Coreset and BADGE perform slightly worse. However, with a batch size of 10000, ALBL and Conf perform significantly better than the other algorithms. This suggests that the optimal batch size for each algorithm may be different.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the learning curves of different active learning algorithms for different batch sizes. The x-axis shows the number of labels queried, while the y-axis shows the accuracy.
qa.student: As the batch size increases from 100 to 10,000, the learning curves show that all active learning algorithms become clearly better separated: with large batches (10,000) some methods like BADGE and Coreset achieve much higher accuracy than random, while others lag behind.
qa.feedback: You are right that the figure compares different batch sizes for the same set of algorithms, but you misread how separation between methods changes. Accuracy is on the y‑axis (higher means better) and #labels queried on the x‑axis. At batch size 10,000 (right panel), all curves are almost exactly on top of each other and follow the same straight line, so there is essentially no difference between methods. At batch size 1,000 (middle) the curves are still very similar with only tiny gaps, and at batch size 100 (left) there is slightly more visible but still small separation. So, larger batches do not make algorithms “clearly better separated”; instead, they make their performance more similar, with the largest batch size showing virtually no difference across methods.

qa.question: Which active learning strategy appears to be the most efficient for this dataset and model, based on the learning curves?
qa.answer: ALBL and Coreset.

The ALBL and Coreset curves rise more steeply than the others, indicating that they achieve higher accuracy with fewer labeled examples.
qa.student: The ALBL strategy appears to be the most efficient, since its curve is at the top.
qa.feedback: You correctly point out that ALBL is very strong, but you leave out important details needed for a full answer. The curves show that ALBL, BADGE, and sometimes Coreset all reach similarly high accuracies very quickly across the different batch sizes, while random and simple uncertainty methods tend to lag slightly behind, especially at smaller batch sizes. To fully answer the question, you should mention that ALBL (and often BADGE) consistently achieves high accuracy with fewer queried labels than most other strategies, and note that differences become smaller as the number of labels grows large.

qa.question: How does the performance of different active learning strategies compare on OpenML #156 with an MLP model?
qa.answer: The figure shows that ALBL and BADGE perform the best, while Margin and Random perform the worst.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the learning curves for different active learning strategies. The learning curves show the accuracy of the model as a function of the number of labeled examples. ALBL and BADGE reach a higher accuracy with fewer labeled examples than the other strategies.
qa.student: All of the active learning strategies perform about the same on OpenML #156 with an MLP. Their curves overlap, so strategy choice doesn’t really matter; accuracy just increases similarly as more labels are queried.
qa.feedback: You are right that all methods improve as more labels are queried and that the differences are not huge, but you are overlooking consistent performance ordering. Higher curves mean better accuracy. Across batch sizes, BADGE and Coreset tend to underperform, especially at smaller label budgets, while ALBL, Conf, Entropy, Marg, and even Rand generally achieve higher accuracies. In the largest batch size plot, most strategies (except Coreset) converge to nearly the same high accuracy, but in the smaller-batch plots, uncertainty-based methods clearly dominate early and mid stages. You should describe which methods are better or worse at different label counts rather than concluding they are all equivalent.

qa.question: Which algorithm tends to select batches that are both more diverse and higher-magnitude than a k-DPP?
qa.answer: k-MEANS++

qa.student: The algorithm that tends to select batches that are both more diverse and higher-magnitude than a k-DPP is k-means++.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified k-means++ as the algorithm, but your answer is incomplete because you did not explain what “more diverse and higher-magnitude” refers to in the context of the plots. You should mention that k-means++ has higher log determinants of the Gram matrices (greater diversity) and higher average ℓ₂ norms of gradient embeddings (greater predictive uncertainty) than k-DPP in the shown experiments.

qa.question: Which acquisition function performs the best in terms of normalized error?
qa.answer: BADGE
qa.explanation: The cumulative distribution function (CDF) of BADGE is shifted to the left compared to the other acquisition functions, which indicates that it has a lower normalized error.
qa.student: The random (Rand) acquisition function performs the best because its curve is closest to the vertical line at normalized error 1, meaning it most often achieves that target error.
qa.feedback: You are misunderstanding what “best” means on this CDF plot. For normalized error on the x-axis, lower values are better. In a CDF, for any given error value, a curve that is higher (larger cumulative frequency) means the method more often achieves errors at or below that value. So the best acquisition function is the one whose curve is furthest to the left and highest for low error values, not the one closest to 1. From the plot, methods like BADGE and ALBL dominate by having higher cumulative frequencies at lower normalized errors compared to Rand.

qa.question: How do the learning curves for k-MEANS++ and k-DPP sampling compare in terms of accuracy?
qa.answer: The learning curves for k-MEANS++ and k-DPP sampling nearly perfectly overlap, indicating that they achieve similar accuracy.
qa.explanation: The left and center plots show the learning curves for k-MEANS++ and k-DPP sampling, respectively. The curves are nearly identical, which suggests that the two sampling approaches achieve similar accuracy.
qa.student: The k-MEANS++ sampling method clearly achieves higher accuracy than k-DPP across the learning curves, since the blue k-MEANS++ line is consistently above the gray k-DPP line for most numbers of labels queried.
qa.feedback: You correctly refer to the two methods and the learning curves, but you misread their relative performance. In both left and center plots, the gray (k-DPP) and blue (k-MEANS++) curves are almost perfectly overlapping in accuracy for all numbers of labels queried. One is not consistently above the other; their accuracies are essentially the same throughout. Higher values on the y-axis mean better accuracy, and here those values match very closely for both methods.

qa.question: How does the performance of different active learning methods vary with the batch size?

qa.explanation: The figure shows the learning curves for different active learning methods on the MNIST dataset with different batch sizes. The learning curves show how the accuracy of the model changes as the number of labeled examples increases.
qa.student: All methods improve in accuracy as more labels are queried, and their curves look very similar across different batch sizes, with only small differences between methods.
qa.feedback: You correctly noted that all methods improve and that their curves are similar, but you did not describe how changing the batch size affects their relative performance. For small batch size (100), methods like BADGE, Coreset, and ALBL give a noticeable accuracy gain over random and simple uncertainty methods early on. At batch size 1000, the advantage shrinks but better methods still stay slightly ahead. At the largest batch size (10000), nearly all methods, including random, converge to almost the same accuracy very quickly, so active learning offers little benefit. Be sure to compare how the gap between strategies changes as batch size increases, not just that they all improve.

In the plots, higher curves mean better performance. While MLP improves fast, its accuracy curve stays below those of ResNet and VGG for most of the label range. VGG (and very closely ResNet) actually achieves the highest accuracy, so saying MLP is best reflects a misunderstanding of what metric to use when comparing model performance.

1906.05478v3-Figure19-1.png.caption: Comparisons of architectures with (red curves) and without (blue curves) a net bias for the experimental design described in Section 5. The networks are trained using i.i.d. Gaussian noise but evaluated on noise drawn i.i.d. from a uniform distribution with mean 0. The performance is quantified by the PSNR of the denoised image as a function of the input PSNR of the noisy image. All the architectures with bias perform poorly out of their training range, whereas the bias-free versions all achieve excellent generalization across noise levels, i.e. they are able to generalize across the two different noise distributions. (a) Deep Convolutional Neural Network, DnCNN (Zhang et al., 2017). (b) Recurrent architecture inspired by DURR (Zhang et al., 2018a). (c) Multiscale architecture inspired by the UNet (Ronneberger et al., 2015). (d) Architecture with multiple skip connections inspired by the DenseNet (Huang et al., 2017).

1906.05478v3-Figure18-1.png.caption: Comparison of the performance of a CNN and a BF-CNN with the same architecture for the experimental design described in Section 5. The networks are trained using i.i.d. Gaussian noise but evaluated on noise drawn i.i.d. from a uniform distribution with mean 0. The performance is quantified by the PSNR of the denoised image as a function of the input PSNR of the noisy image. All the architectures with bias perform poorly out of their training range, whereas the bias-free versions all achieve excellent generalization across noise levels, i.e. they are able to generalize across the two different noise distributions. The CNN used for this example is DnCNN (Zhang et al., 2017); using alternative architectures yields similar results (see Figures 19).

1906.05478v3-Figure1-1.png.caption: First-order analysis of the residual of a denoising convolutional neural network as a function of noise level. The plots show the norms of the residual and the net bias averaged over 100 20× 20 natural-image patches for networks trained over different training ranges. The range of noises used for training is highlighted in blue. (a) When the network is trained over the full range of noise levels (σ ∈ [0, 100]) the net bias is small, growing slightly as the noise increases. (b-c) When the network is trained over the a smaller range (σ ∈ [0, 55] and σ ∈ [0, 30]), the net bias grows explosively for noise levels beyond the training range. This coincides with a dramatic drop in performance, reflected in the difference between the magnitudes of the residual and the true noise. The CNN used for this example is DnCNN (Zhang et al., 2017); using alternative architectures yields similar results as shown in Figure 8.

1906.05478v3-Figure14-1.png.caption: Visualization of the linear weighting functions (rows of Ay) of a BF-DnCNN for three example pixels of a noisy input image (left). The next image is the denoised output. The three images on the right show the linear weighting functions corresponding to each of the indicated pixels (red squares). All weighting functions sum to one, and thus compute a local average (although some weights are negative, indicated in red). Their shapes vary substantially, and are adapted to the underlying image content. Each row corresponds to a noisy input with increasing σ and the filters adapt by averaging over a larger region.

qa.question: Which architecture is the most robust to changes in the noise distribution?
qa.answer: The bias-free versions of all the architectures are more robust to changes in the noise distribution than their counterparts with bias.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the performance of the architectures with bias drops significantly when the noise distribution changes from Gaussian to uniform, while the performance of the bias-free architectures remains relatively stable.
qa.student: The architecture in panel (b), the recurrent DURR-inspired model, is the most robust to changes in the noise distribution.
qa.feedback: You misread which curves correspond to robustness. Higher output PSNR means better robustness. Across all input PSNR values, the blue curves (bias-free) stay consistently high and close together in every subfigure, while the red curves (with bias) drop sharply outside the training range. No single architecture stands out as clearly better than the others in terms of generalization across the noise distributions; all four bias-free versions show similarly strong robustness, so you should not single out (b) as the most robust.

qa.question: How does the performance of the BF-CNN compare to the DnCNN when the input PSNR is high?
qa.answer: The BF-CNN performs similarly to the DnCNN when the input PSNR is high.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the two lines are close together when the input PSNR is high, which indicates that the two networks are performing similarly.
qa.student: When the input PSNR is high, the BF-CNN still performs well.
qa.feedback: You only say that the BF-CNN “performs well” and do not specify how it compares to DnCNN at high input PSNR. From the plots, when the input PSNR is high (toward the right side of the x‑axis), the BF-CNN (blue) maintains high output PSNR and clearly outperforms the biased DnCNN (red), which degrades outside its training range. You need to state this relative performance, not just that BF-CNN works well.

qa.question: What happens to the net bias when the network is trained over a smaller range of noise levels?
qa.answer: The net bias grows explosively for noise levels beyond the training range.
qa.explanation: This can be seen in figures (b) and (c), where the blue line (representing the net bias) increases sharply for noise levels above 55 and 30, respectively.
qa.student: When the network is trained over a smaller range of noise levels, the net bias stays small and roughly constant for all noise levels, just like in the full-range training case.
qa.feedback: You are assuming the behavior outside the training range is the same as inside it. In panels (b) and (c), once the noise level goes beyond the highlighted training range, the blue “bias” curve increases sharply rather than staying small. This means the net bias grows explosively for unseen (higher) noise levels when the network was trained only on a smaller noise range, unlike the relatively small bias seen when training on the full range.

qa.question: How do the linear weighting functions of a BF-DnCNN change with increasing noise levels?
qa.answer: The linear weighting functions of a BF-DnCNN become more diffuse and average over a larger region with increasing noise levels.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the linear weighting functions for a noisy input image with a higher σ value (e.g., 55) are more spread out than those for a noisy input image with a lower σ value (e.g., 5). This suggests that the BF-DnCNN adapts to higher noise levels by averaging over a larger region, which helps to reduce the noise while preserving the important features of the image.
qa.student: As the noise level σ increases, the linear weighting functions of the BF-DnCNN become more concentrated around each pixel, putting almost all the weight on the center and using a smaller spatial region for averaging.
qa.feedback: You correctly focus on how the weighting functions change with noise, but you have the trend backwards. The figure and caption state that “with increasing σ the filters adapt by averaging over a larger region.” In the visualizations, higher σ clearly corresponds to weights that spread out more spatially instead of concentrating more at the center. So the weighting functions become broader and cover a larger neighborhood as the noise level increases, rather than shrinking to a smaller region.

Occlude(A,B) = 0 in occlusion head means mask B should be placed on top of mask A. Mask R-CNN proposes three instance masks listed with decreasing confidence. The heuristic of [17] occludes all subsequent instances after the “person”, while our method retains them in the final output by querying the occlusion head.

The baseline fusion process [17] is unable to properly assign these as shown in the 2nd and 4th images of the rightmost column whereas our method is able to handle the occlusion relationship present as shown in the 1st and 3rd images of the rightmost column. qa.question: Why does the OCFusion method retain more information in the final output than the fusion by confidence method?
qa.answer: The OCFusion method queries the occlusion head to determine whether one mask should be placed on top of another, while the fusion by confidence method simply occludes all subsequent instances after the "person."
qa.explanation: The figure shows how the two methods differ in their handling of occlusions. The fusion by confidence method simply occludes all subsequent instances after the "person," while the OCFusion method queries the occlusion head to determine whether one mask should be placed on top of another. This allows the OCFusion method to retain more information in the final output.
qa.student: OCFusion retains more information because it does not just rely on confidence scores to order the masks. Instead, it queries an occlusion head to decide whether one mask should be placed on top of another, which prevents lower-confidence objects from being completely hidden.
qa.feedback: You correctly mention that OCFusion uses an occlusion head instead of only confidence scores, but you leave out the key detail of how this preserves specific instances. In the example, the confidence-based fusion hides all later masks once the “person” mask is placed, so objects like the glove and ball are lost. OCFusion explicitly checks pairwise occlusion relations (Occlude(A,B) values) so it can decide when a later mask should appear on top (e.g., the blue object over the red person) and when it should remain behind. By modeling which object actually occludes which, rather than just ordering by confidence, OCFusion keeps multiple overlapping instances visible in the final segmentation.

qa.question: Which method performs better on the Cityscapes val dataset, according to the PQ metric?

qa.student: Both methods perform equally well on the Cityscapes val dataset according to the PQ metric, since their scores are very close.
qa.feedback: You are right that the scores are close, but “equally well” is not correct conceptually. PQ is a metric where higher values mean better performance, and even small differences matter. In the table, OCFusion has a PQ of 59.3, while the Baseline has 58.6, so OCFusion performs better according to PQ. Your mistake is treating a small numerical difference as if it implies no difference in performance.

The question asks which method performs best, and performance here is indicated by the highest PQ (and associated PQTh and PQSt). You should mention that OCFusion* with ResNeXt-101 (with multi-scale testing) has the highest PQ value (46.3), as well as the highest PQTh (53.5) and PQSt (35.4), compared to all other methods listed with multi-scale testing.

qa.question: What is the role of the occlusion head in the thing branch of the network?
qa.answer: The occlusion head is responsible for determining whether two mask proposals overlap significantly, and if so, it outputs a binary value indicating whether one mask occludes the other.
qa.explanation: The occlusion head takes as input two mask proposals, Mi and Mj, and outputs a binary value, Occlude(Mi,Mj). This value is 1 if Mi occludes Mj, and 0 otherwise. The occlusion head helps the network to better understand the spatial relationships between objects in the scene.
qa.student: The occlusion head refines the instance masks by making them more accurate around the object boundaries.
qa.feedback: You are confusing mask refinement with occlusion reasoning. The figure and caption state that Mask R-CNN already generates the instance mask proposals. The occlusion head does not improve their boundaries; instead, it takes pairs of overlapping masks Mi and Mj and predicts a binary value Occlude(Mi, Mj) indicating which one occludes the other. In other words, its role is to infer occlusion relationships between instances with appreciable overlap, not to sharpen or adjust the masks themselves.

qa.question: What is the difference between the ground truth and the Kirillov et al. and OCFusion (ours) results?
qa.answer: The ground truth is a more accurate representation of the scene, with more detail and less noise. The Kirillov et al. and OCFusion results are more blurry and have less detail.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the input image, the ground truth, and the results of two different methods for depth estimation. The ground truth is the most accurate representation of the scene, and the other two methods are compared to it.
qa.student: The ground truth shows the ideal segmentation of all objects and people. Kirillov et al. and OCFusion (ours) both miss some of the table areas and confuse some people with the background. Compared to the ground truth, both methods also over-segment the plates into many small parts, while the ground truth keeps them as one region.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that the ground truth is the ideal reference and that both methods make segmentation mistakes, but you misread specific differences. In the figure, plates are not over-segmented into many small parts by either Kirillov et al. or OCFusion; they are mostly single colored regions similar to the ground truth. The main visual difference is that Kirillov et al. leaves more holes and missing parts in people and objects (e.g., gaps in arms or heads and missing table regions), while OCFusion fills these in and produces more continuous, complete regions that more closely match the ground truth shapes and extents.

qa.question: Which of the following images is the output of the Spatial Ranking Module?
qa.answer: The third image from the right.
qa.explanation: The caption states that the figure shows a comparison against the Spatial Ranking Module, and the third image from the right is labeled "Spatial Ranking Module."
qa.student: The Spatial Ranking Module output is shown in the second image from the right in each row.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified that the Spatial Ranking Module corresponds to the second image from the right. However, you only described its position and did not clarify that this is the column labeled “Spatial Ranking Module” beneath the examples, nor did you distinguish it from the other outputs (fusion by confidence and OCFusion). When answering, explicitly mention that it is the column labeled “Spatial Ranking Module,” separate from the “fusion by confidence” and “OCFusion (ours)” columns, so the reader knows exactly which images you mean.

qa.question: How does the proposed OCFusion method compare to UPSNet in terms of accuracy?
qa.answer: The proposed OCFusion method appears to be more accurate than UPSNet, as it produces segmentations that are closer to the ground truth.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the input image, the ground truth segmentation, and the segmentations produced by the two methods. The OCFusion segmentations are visually closer to the ground truth than the UPSNet segmentations, which suggests that OCFusion is more accurate.
qa.student: OCFusion has similar accuracy to UPSNet because both segment the main objects correctly. The small visual differences between their outputs are mostly stylistic and don’t indicate a real accuracy improvement of OCFusion over UPSNet.
qa.feedback: You correctly notice that both methods capture the main scene structure, but you misunderstand what the qualitative comparison is meant to show. The differences are not just “stylistic”: OCFusion more closely matches the ground truth, especially around object boundaries and finer details (for example, shapes of people, boats, and thin structures). Those corrections indicate higher segmentation accuracy, not just a different visual style. When comparing methods, you should look for where labels are corrected or refined relative to ground truth, because those discrepancies reflect real accuracy gains.

qa.question: What is the difference between the ground truth masks and the Mask R-CNN predictions in (a) and (b)?
qa.answer: In (a), the ground truth masks show the individual objects in the scene, while the Mask R-CNN predictions show the objects as a single, merged object. In (b), the ground truth mask shows the dining table as a single object, while the Mask R-CNN prediction shows the dining table as a large, occluding object that covers the entire image.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the original images, the ground truth masks, and the Mask R-CNN predictions. The ground truth masks show the individual objects in the scene, while the Mask R-CNN predictions show the objects as a single, merged object. This difference is likely due to the fact that the Mask R-CNN model is not able to properly handle the occlusion relationships present in the scene.
qa.student: In (a), the ground truth masks show the individual objects in the scene, while the Mask R-CNN predictions show the objects as a single, merged object. In (b), the ground truth mask shows the dining table as a single object, while the Mask R-CNN prediction shows the dining table as a large, occluding object that covers the entire image.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: Which hyperparameter value results in the highest performance?
qa.answer: The hyperparameter value of 0.05 results in the highest performance.
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of the PQ hyperparameter on the COCO dataset. The performance is measured by the average precision (AP). The AP is highest for the hyperparameter value of 0.05.
qa.student: The highest performance is achieved with τ = 0.4 and ρ = 0.05, where PQ is 41.27.
qa.feedback: You misread the table. All reported PQ values are around 41, but the maximum is 41.27, which appears twice: at (τ, ρ) = (0.4, 0.05) and also at (0.5, 0.05). Saying that only τ = 0.4, ρ = 0.05 gives the best performance is factually wrong because τ = 0.5, ρ = 0.05 achieves exactly the same (highest) PQ.

qa.question: What is the relationship between τ and PQ?
qa.answer: There is a positive relationship between τ and PQ. As τ increases, PQ also increases.
qa.explanation: The table shows that for all values of ρ, the PQ value increases as τ increases.
qa.student: As τ increases, PQ decreases.
qa.feedback: You’ve only mentioned a general trend and left out important details. The table shows PQ values for several (τ, ρ) pairs at three different τ settings (0.05, 0.10, 0.20). PQ does not simply decrease with τ in all cases; it stays roughly similar or changes only slightly across τ values for each ρ, and sometimes is even highest at an intermediate τ (e.g., τ = 0.10). To fully answer, you need to mention that the relationship is weak and depends on ρ, rather than stating only a one-directional trend.

qa.question: What is the effect of enabling inter-class and intra-class occlusion on the PQ, PQQth, and PQQst metrics?
qa.answer: Enabling inter-class and intra-class occlusion increases the PQ, PQQth, and PQQst metrics.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the PQ, PQQth, and PQQst metrics are all higher when both inter-class and intra-class occlusion are enabled (the last row of the table) than when neither is enabled (the first row of the table).
qa.student: Enabling inter-class and intra-class occlusion hurts performance: PQ, PQQth, and PQQst all decrease because adding more occlusion makes the segmentation task harder and the model less accurate.
qa.feedback: You are assuming that adding occlusion always makes performance worse, but the table shows the opposite for most metrics. Higher values of PQ and PQQth mean better performance. When only inter-class occlusion is enabled, PQ and PQQth increase compared to the baseline, and when both inter- and intra-class occlusion are enabled, they increase even more. PQQst stays the same in all cases, so it is not hurt either. Your conceptual mistake is treating occlusion modeling as pure noise, instead of recognizing that these occlusion mechanisms actually improve the model’s capability and thus the metrics.

qa.question: Which method is faster, Baseline or OCFusion?
qa.answer: Baseline is faster than OCFusion.
qa.explanation: The table shows the runtime (ms) overhead per image for each method. The Baseline method has a lower runtime than the OCFusion method for both the COCO and Cityscapes datasets.
qa.student: OCFusion is faster than Baseline because its runtime overhead is lower on both COCO and Cityscapes.
qa.feedback: You misread the runtimes. Higher numbers in milliseconds mean slower performance. In the table, Baseline takes 153 ms (COCO) and 378 ms (Cityscapes), while OCFusion takes 156 ms and 396 ms. Since OCFusion’s times are larger, it is actually slower than Baseline, not faster.

qa.question: What is the effect of enabling intraclass capability?
qa.answer: Enabling intraclass capability results in more accurate and detailed segmentation of objects in the image.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the car on the left side of the image is segmented more accurately and in greater detail when intraclass capability is enabled (right side of the image). This is because intraclass capability allows the model to learn more about the different types of objects that can appear in an image, and to better distinguish between them.
qa.student: Enabling intraclass capability improves the segmentation of the cars in the scene. The car region becomes more consistent and accurate compared to when intraclass capability is disabled.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that intraclass capability improves the car segmentation, but your answer is too general and misses what specifically changes. From the right image, you should describe that with intraclass capability enabled, multiple adjacent cars that were previously merged into one large blob are better separated into distinct instances of the same class. In other words, the effect is not just greater accuracy, but clearer boundaries and differentiation between objects within the same class.

qa.question: What is the effect of using the OCFusion method with ResNet-101 backbone on the PQ metric?
qa.answer: The OCFusion method with ResNet-101 backbone improves the PQ metric by 3.2 points.
qa.explanation: The table shows the results of the baseline and OCFusion methods with different backbones. The relative improvement for the PQ metric is shown in the last row.
qa.student: Using the OCFusion method with a ResNet-101 backbone slightly decreases the PQ metric compared to the baseline, so it doesn’t really help overall performance.
qa.feedback: You are misinterpreting the direction of the PQ change. In this table, higher PQ means better performance. For ResNet-101, PQ increases from 41.0 (Baseline) to 43.0 (OCFusion), which is a relative improvement of +2.0 points, not a decrease. You correctly focused on the ResNet-101 rows, but your conclusion about the effect is reversed: OCFusion improves overall PQ performance.

qa.question: What are the two dimensions that the valence and arousal circumplex uses to represent emotions?
qa.answer: Valence and arousal.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the valence and arousal circumplex is a two-dimensional model of affect. The x-axis represents valence, which ranges from negative to positive, and the y-axis represents arousal, which ranges from calming to exciting.
qa.student: The two dimensions are positive vs. negative emotions and exciting vs. calming emotions.
qa.feedback: You are describing the ends of the scales rather than naming the underlying dimensions. In this model, the horizontal axis is called valence (ranging from negative to positive), and the vertical axis is called arousal (ranging from calming/low arousal to exciting/high arousal). The question asks for these conceptual dimensions, not just their polar adjectives.

qa.question: What is the difference between the static separable (2D) convolution and the spatio-temporal (3D) separable convolution?
qa.answer: The static separable (2D) convolution operates on a single static image, while the spatio-temporal (3D) separable convolution operates on a sequence of images over time.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the static separable (2D) convolution takes a single image as input and produces a single output. In contrast, the spatio-temporal (3D) separable convolution takes a sequence of images as input and produces a sequence of outputs.
qa.student: The static separable (2D) convolution operates only on single images using 2D spatial filters (the blue and red factors). The spatio-temporal (3D) separable convolution does the same thing but just stacks those 2D filters across frames, without adding any new type of factor, so it still only models spatial information and not time.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that the 2D convolution works on single static images with spatial filters, while the 3D version works on sequences. However, it is factually wrong to say the 3D separable convolution “still only models spatial information and not time” or that it has no new type of factor. In the figure, the spatio-temporal (3D) convolution adds explicit temporal factors (shown in green) that operate along the time dimension, in addition to the pretrained spatial factors (blue and red). This means the 3D separable convolution jointly models spatial and temporal information, unlike the purely spatial 2D convolution.

qa.question: Which network architecture achieved the highest Top-1 accuracy on the 20BN-Jester Dataset?
qa.answer: HO-CP ConvNet-S
qa.explanation: The table shows the Top-1 accuracy for each network architecture. HO-CP ConvNet-S has the highest Top-1 accuracy of 85.4%.
qa.student: The HO-CP ConvNet (Ours) architecture achieved the highest Top-1 accuracy.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on Top-1 accuracy, but you chose the wrong variant. There are two HO-CP architectures listed, and you did not distinguish between them. The table shows that HO-CP ConvNet-S (Ours) has the highest Top-1 accuracy (85.4%), which is higher than both 3D-ConvNet and HO-CP ConvNet (Ours). Be sure to read all rows carefully and name the exact model variant with the best value.

qa.question: What is the difference between the baseline and the proposed method in the 3D convolutional network architecture?
qa.answer: The only difference between the baseline and the proposed method is the 3D convolutional block used (3D Conv B): for the baseline, a regular 3D conv is used, and for the proposed method, the HO-CP conv-S is used.
qa.explanation: The caption states that the only difference between the baseline and the proposed method is the 3D convolutional block used. The figure shows the overall architecture of the network, which is the same for both the baseline and the proposed method.
qa.student: The difference is that the proposed method adds HO-CP conv-S blocks on top of the existing 3D convolutional layers in the baseline, making the network deeper and more complex, while the baseline just uses the original 3D Conv B blocks.
qa.feedback: You correctly focus on the 3D convolutional block, but you misunderstand how the proposed method differs from the baseline. The architecture depth and sequence of layers are actually the same for both models; nothing is “added on top.” The only change is which type of 3D Conv B block is used: the baseline uses a regular 3D convolution, while the proposed method replaces that block with the HO-CP conv-S variant. You should think of it as swapping the convolution type inside the same architecture, not making the network deeper.
