qa.question: Why is sentence S2 more coherent with sentence S1 than sentence S4?
qa.answer: Sentence S2 is more coherent with sentence S1 than sentence S4 because they share the same entity "dad."
qa.explanation: The figure shows that sentence S1 mentions "dad" and sentence S2 also mentions "dad." This shared entity creates a connection between the two sentences, making them more coherent.
qa.student: Because S2 mentions “dad” like S1 does.
qa.feedback: You only mentioned shared mention of “dad” and left out the full explanation. S2 is more coherent with S1 not just because they share the same entity “dad,” but also because S2 describes an activity with the dad (taking the speaker to a park) that logically continues the idea of “we always had a good time when getting together.” You need to state that this continuation of the relationship and activity makes S2 fit better after S1 than S4 does.

qa.question: Which model modification has the greatest impact on the accuracy of the S-Graph model?
qa.answer: Removing 50% of entities has the greatest impact on the accuracy of the S-Graph model.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the accuracy of the S-Graph model decreases from 58.06 to 57.57 when 50% of entities are removed. This is a larger decrease than any other modification made to the model.
qa.student: Sharing parameters between entity and sentence nodes has the greatest impact on the S-Graph model’s accuracy, because it leads to the largest drop in performance compared with the original model.
qa.feedback: You are interpreting “impact” only as a negative effect and also confusing S-Graph with SE-Graph. In the table, S-Graph accuracy is only reported for the original, shuffle edges, remove 50% entities, and remove 10% entities settings; there is no S-Graph result for “share parameters,” so you cannot claim it affects S-Graph accuracy. Among the actual S-Graph variants, shuffling edges causes the largest accuracy change (a drop from 58.06 to 57.06), which is smaller than the entity-removal variants. This suggests that, within S-Graph, removing entities has more effect than edge shuffling, but you must base your conclusion only on the rows where S-Graph accuracy is given.

qa.question: Which model performed the best on the SIND dataset for predicting the first sentence?
qa.answer: The SE-Graph model performed the best on the SIND dataset for predicting the first sentence, with an accuracy of 78.12%.
qa.explanation: The table shows the accuracy of different models on the SIND dataset for predicting the first and last sentences. The SE-Graph model has the highest accuracy for predicting the first sentence.
qa.student: The S-Graph model performed the best on the SIND dataset for predicting the first sentence.
qa.feedback: You correctly looked at the SIND “head” column (first sentence prediction), but you misread which value is highest. S-Graph has 77.07, while SE-Graph has 78.12, which is larger and thus better because higher ratios mean better prediction accuracy here. So SE-Graph, not S-Graph, is the best model for predicting the first sentence on SIND.

qa.question: What is the role of the Sentence Encoder in the ATTOrderNet architecture?
qa.answer: The Sentence Encoder is responsible for converting the input sentences into a sequence of hidden representations, which are then fed into the Self-Attention layer.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the Sentence Encoder takes the input sentences S1, S3, S2, and S4 as input and outputs the hidden representations k0, k1, k2, and k3. These representations are then used by the Self-Attention layer to attend to different parts of the input sentences and generate the output sequence h0, h1, h2, h3, and h4.
qa.student: The Sentence Encoder converts each input sentence (S1, S3, S2, S4) into a vector representation before it is processed by the self-attention layers.
qa.feedback: You correctly state that the Sentence Encoder turns each sentence into a vector, but you leave out how these encoded vectors are used in the rest of the architecture. In ATTOrderNet, the Sentence Encoder produces the initial sentence representations (the k vectors) that are then fed both into the stack of self-attention layers and, via connections, to the Pointer Network. These encoded sentences provide the base features on which self-attention refines contextual representations and from which the Pointer Network ultimately predicts the sentence order.

qa.question: What does the green dashed arrow represent in the figure?
qa.answer: The green dashed arrow represents the influence of the sentence context on the generation of the entity graph at time t.
qa.explanation: The green dashed arrow points from the sentence context g^t-1 at time t-1 to the entity graph at time t. This indicates that the sentence context from the previous time step is used to inform the generation of the entity graph at the current time step.
qa.student: The green dashed arrow shows the temporal recurrence of a node’s own hidden state from the previous time step to the current time step, meaning the node just passes its state forward through time.
qa.feedback: You are right that the arrow links information across time steps, but it does not represent a node simply passing its own state forward. The green dashed arrow connects a global node at time t−1 to another node at time t, indicating propagation of global or sentence-level context into the node’s update, not self-recurrence. In this GRN, each time step’s node state is updated using both local graph neighbors (solid arrows) and this separate global signal (dashed arrow), so you should distinguish global-to-node message passing from standard recurrent self-connections.

qa.question: How does the sentence-entity graph in (b) differ from the fully-connected graph in (a)?
qa.answer: The sentence-entity graph in (b) only connects entities that are related by a syntactic role in a sentence, while the fully-connected graph in (a) connects all entities to each other.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the sentence-entity graph in (b) has fewer edges than the fully-connected graph in (a). This is because the sentence-entity graph only connects entities that are related by a syntactic role in a sentence, while the fully-connected graph connects all entities to each other.
qa.student: The sentence-entity graph in (b) only connects entities that are related by a syntactic role in a sentence, while the fully-connected graph in (a) connects all entities to each other.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: What is the relationship between the recurrent step and accuracy?
qa.answer: The accuracy generally increases as the recurrent step increases.
qa.explanation: The figure shows a plot of accuracy vs. recurrent step. The accuracy generally increases as the recurrent step increases, with a slight dip at step 4. This suggests that increasing the recurrent step can improve the accuracy of the model.
qa.student: Accuracy decreases steadily as the recurrent step increases from 0 to 5.
qa.feedback: You misread the trend in the plot. The y-axis shows accuracy, where higher values mean better performance. As the recurrent step increases from 0 to 3, accuracy clearly increases (the line goes up), peaking around step 3. After that, accuracy dips slightly at step 4 and then rises a bit again at step 5, but it never goes below the accuracy at step 0. It does not decrease steadily with more recurrent steps.

From the NIPS Abstract column, SE-Graph achieves 57.27 Accuracy, which is higher than all other models listed. Always report both which model is best and the corresponding score when the question asks about performance.

qa.question: What is the relationship between the divergence of a CNN on a small test set and the divergence on a large test set?
qa.answer: The divergence of a CNN on a small test set is positively correlated with the divergence on a large test set.
qa.explanation: The figure shows a scatter plot of the divergence of a CNN on a small test set versus the divergence on a large test set. The points on the plot are clustered around a line with a positive slope, indicating that the two measures of divergence are positively correlated.
qa.student: The divergence on the small test set accurately matches the divergence on the large test set, meaning the small test set gives an unbiased estimate of the CNN’s true divergence.
qa.feedback: You are assuming “accurate” here to mean unbiased, but the figure and caption emphasize bias despite correlation. The scatter plot shows that higher divergence on the small test set corresponds to higher divergence on the large test set (a strong positive correlation), but not that the values coincide or are unbiased. Correlated means the rankings and trends align, while biased means the small test set systematically over- or underestimates the divergence relative to the large test set. You should distinguish between these two ideas instead of treating correlation as evidence of no bias.

qa.question: Which set of data shows the most overfitting?
qa.answer: The training set.
qa.explanation: The CNN divergence for the training set is much lower than the CNN divergence for the two test sets. This indicates that the model is performing much better on the training set than on the test sets, which is a sign of overfitting.
qa.student: Test set 1 shows the most overfitting because its CNN divergence is the highest over the iterations compared with the other sets.
qa.feedback: You are right that higher CNN divergence values on the test sets compared with the training set indicate overfitting, but the factual mistake is picking only Test set 1. In the plot, both Test set 1 (blue) and Test set 2 (green) stay well above the Training set (red) across almost all iterations, and they are very close to each other. This means the model is overfitting similarly on both test sets, not predominantly on Test set 1.

qa.question: Which model performed the best according to the test set CNN divergence metric?
qa.answer: WGAN-GP
qa.explanation: The table shows the test set CNN divergence for different models. The WGAN-GP model has the lowest value of 12.97, indicating it performed the best.
qa.student: The WGAN-GP model performed the best because it has the lowest test set CNN divergence value of 12.97.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified WGAN-GP as the best-performing model among the generative models listed, since a lower CNN divergence value indicates better performance and 12.97 is the lowest. However, you did not mention how this compares to the “Training set” baseline with divergence 14.62. The caption explicitly notes that WGAN-GP attains a lower divergence than memorization of the training set, which strengthens the conclusion that it performs best overall. Including this comparison would make your answer more complete.

qa.question: Which metric is the most sensitive to diversity, and which is the least sensitive?
qa.answer: The CNN Div. metric is the most sensitive to diversity, and the Inception Score is the least sensitive.
qa.explanation: The "n to Win" column indicates how many training set images need to be memorized to score better than a well-trained GAN model. A larger n means the metric is more sensitive to diversity. The CNN Div. metric requires over 1 million images to be memorized, while the Inception Score only requires 32 images.
qa.student: The most sensitive metric to diversity is the Inception score because it only needs 32 memorized images to beat a GAN, and the least sensitive is CNN Diversity since it requires more than 1 million images.
qa.feedback: You’ve reversed what “more sensitive to diversity” means in the table. The caption says that a larger n (number of memorized training images needed to beat the GAN) indicates higher sensitivity to diversity. So the metric that needs the largest n is actually the most sensitive. Here, CNN Diversity (n > 1M) is the most sensitive, while Inception score (n = 32) is the least sensitive. You interpreted small n as indicating higher sensitivity, which is the opposite of the definition given.

qa.question: Which distribution is preferred by common GAN benchmarks?
qa.answer: The distribution preferred by common GAN benchmarks is p̂train, which is the red distribution in the figure.
qa.explanation: The caption states that common GAN benchmarks prefer training set memorization, which is represented by p̂train in the figure.
qa.student: The distribution preferred by common GAN benchmarks is p̂train, which is the red distribution in the figure.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: Based on the table, which evaluation metric assigns better scores to GANs that generalize beyond the training set?
qa.answer: DCNN
qa.explanation: The table shows that the Inception Score and FID assign higher scores to memorization of the training set, while DCNN assigns higher scores to GANs.
qa.student: The Inception Score assigns better scores to GANs that generalize beyond the training set.
qa.feedback: You misread which values correspond to “better” scores. For Inception Score, higher values are better; the memorization model has a higher score (11.3) than the GAN (6.49), so this metric favors memorization, not generalization. For FID and DCNN, lower values are better. The DCNN test divergence is lower for the GAN (12.9) than for memorization (14.7), so DCNN is the metric that assigns better scores to GANs that generalize beyond the training set.

qa.question: Which retrieval method performed better, text-retrieval or image-retrieval?
qa.answer: Image-retrieval performed better than text-retrieval.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the image-retrieval model achieved higher R@1, R@5, R@10, and Mean r scores than the text-retrieval model.
qa.student: Text-retrieval performed better.
qa.feedback: You only stated that text-retrieval was better without checking all the metrics. In the table, higher values of R@1, R@5, and R@10 indicate better retrieval, while a lower mean rank (Mean r) is better. Image-retrieval has higher recall at all three cutoffs (39.6 vs 36.2, 72.3 vs 69.0, 83.7 vs 81.7), but a worse mean rank (11.0 vs 7.7). Since you were asked which overall performed better, you need to mention that image-retrieval generally performs better on recall, while text-retrieval is better on mean rank, instead of choosing just one without this comparison.

qa.question: Which model achieved the highest CIDEr score when trained with cross-entropy loss?
qa.answer: Up-Down
qa.explanation: The table shows the CIDEr scores for each model under both cross-entropy loss and CIDEr optimization training. Under cross-entropy loss, the Up-Down model has the highest CIDEr score of 113.5.
qa.student: The model “Ours: SG+RWS+WR” achieved the highest CIDEr score under cross-entropy loss because adding more recall components always increases performance.
qa.feedback: You are assuming that adding more components automatically yields the best performance, instead of actually comparing the CIDEr values. Under cross-entropy loss, the CIDEr scores (C column) are: Up-Down 113.5, Ours:SG 115.3, Ours:SG+RWS 116.9, and Ours:SG+RWS+WR 116.9. The highest score is shared by both Ours:SG+RWS and Ours:SG+RWS+WR, so you cannot claim that SG+RWS+WR alone achieves the highest CIDEr. Your reasoning needs to be based on the specific metric values, not just on the model’s complexity.

qa.question: Which model performed the best in terms of CIDEr score?
qa.answer: stackcap+SG+RWS+WR
qa.explanation: The table shows the CIDEr score for each model. The highest score is 126.4, which is achieved by the stackcap+SG+RWS+WR model.
qa.student: The best CIDEr score was achieved by the att2all+SG+RWS+WR model, with a CIDEr score of 125.0.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified a strong model, but it is not the best in terms of CIDEr. In this table, higher C (CIDEr) values mean better performance. The highest CIDEr score is 126.4, obtained by the “stackcap+SG+RWS+WR” model, which is larger than 125.0 for att2all+SG+RWS+WR. Carefully scan the entire C column to find the maximum value.

qa.question: How does the plot in the figure help to understand the recalled words?
qa.answer: The plot in the figure shows the weights of the recalled words in the attention mechanism. The words with higher weights are more likely to be used in the generated caption.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the words "street" and "sitting" have the highest weights, which is consistent with the generated caption "a yellow fire hydrant sitting on the site of a street."
qa.student: The plot shows the attention weights for the recalled words, so it helps us see which words the model focuses on most when generating the caption.
qa.feedback: You correctly mention that the plot shows attention weights, but you do not explain how this specifically helps interpret the recalled words. The x-axis lists the recalled words and the lines correspond to target words like “street” and “sitting.” By looking at where each line peaks, you can see which recalled words contribute most to predicting each target word (for example, higher weights on “street” near that word). You need to state that the plot links recalled words to particular generated words, revealing which recalled words are most influential in forming each part of the caption.

qa.question: What is the role of the "switch" component in the proposed recall mechanism?
qa.answer: The "switch" component combines the word probabilities computed by the semantic guide (SG) and the recalled-word slot (RWS) into a final word probability.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the switch component takes as input the word probabilities from the SG and RWS, as well as a switching parameter s_t. It then uses these inputs to compute the final word probability Pt.
qa.student: The switch decides whether to use the semantic guide or the recalled-word slot by picking whichever one has the higher word probability at time t. It basically turns one source on and the other off, so only a single source contributes to the final word probability.
qa.feedback: You correctly identify that the switch controls how the semantic guide and recalled-word slot contribute, but you misunderstand how it does this. The diagram shows a soft switch, not a hard winner-take-all choice. The switch outputs a weight s_t for the semantic guide and 1 − s_t for the recalled-word slot, then combines their word probabilities into the final P_t. So both sources can contribute simultaneously, with their influence smoothly balanced rather than one being completely turned off.

qa.question: Which method achieved the best CIDEr score when K=5?
qa.answer: C
qa.explanation: The table shows the CIDEr scores for different methods and values of K. When K=5, the CIDEr score for C is 116.7, which is the highest score among all methods.

qa.feedback: You misread the table. All rows in this table are under the same method (Cross-Entropy Loss); only K changes. For K=5, the CIDEr score (column “C”) is 116.7, not for K=1. At K=1 the CIDEr score is 115.8. So the best CIDEr score when K=5 is simply the Cross-Entropy Loss method with K=5, with a value of 116.7.

qa.student: The λ value of 0.5.

When a question asks “which λ value resulted in the highest CIDEr score,” you should give both the λ and the corresponding score to fully use the information in the table.

qa.question: What is the role of the Recall Unit in the proposed method?
qa.answer: The Recall Unit is a text-retrieval module that retrieves relevant words from a corpus based on the input image. These retrieved words are then used to improve the performance of the captioning model.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the Recall Unit takes the input image as input and outputs a set of recalled words. These words are then fed into the RW-Attention module, which uses them to attend to relevant parts of the image. The attended image features are then used by the RNN to generate the caption.
qa.student: The Recall Unit is responsible for generating the final sentence description from the image features.
qa.feedback: You are confusing what generates the caption with what provides extra lexical cues. The RNN is what actually produces the sentence, guided by visual features and attention. The Recall Unit instead acts as a text-retrieval module: it pulls related words from the external corpus (like “airplane,” “runway,” “airport”) that are then fed through RW-Attention into the RNN. Its role is to recall potentially useful words to guide and enhance caption generation, not to directly output the final caption.

qa.question:
Which of the following statements is supported by the figure?

A. The AUC increases with the size of the training data.
B. The AUC increases with the lexical heterogeneity of the expressions used to express a dimension.
C. The AUC is not affected by the size of the training data.
D. The AUC is not affected by the lexical homogeneity of the expressions used to express a dimension.
qa.answer: A. The AUC increases with the size of the training data.
qa.explanation: The left panel of the figure shows that the AUC increases as the number of positive samples increases. This indicates that the AUC increases with the size of the training data.
qa.student: The figure supports statements A and B: the AUC increases with the size of the training data and with the lexical heterogeneity of the expressions used to express a dimension.
qa.feedback: You are correct about A: the left plot shows AUC on the y-axis and number of positive samples on the x-axis, and the upward trend means higher AUC with more training data. The factual mistake is in your interpretation of B. The right plot uses “embedding similarity” on the x-axis as a measure of lexical homogeneity: higher similarity means more homogeneous (not more heterogeneous) expressions. Since AUC increases as embedding similarity increases, the figure supports that AUC increases with lexical homogeneity, not heterogeneity.

qa.question: What are the features that are used to measure trust?
qa.answer:
The features used to measure trust are:
- LIWC (posemo, affect)
- VADER (positive)
- Empath (friends, help, trust)
- Style (empathy words)
qa.explanation: The table shows the features that are used to measure each dimension. The trust dimension is measured by the features listed above.
qa.student: Trust is measured mainly using LIWC categories related to positivity and affect, like posemo and affect.
qa.feedback: You are right that LIWC posemo and affect are part of the trust features, but you are conceptually treating trust as if it were based only on LIWC affective categories. The table shows that trust is a separate dimension with multiple types of signals: LIWC (posemo, affect), Vader (positive), Empath categories (friends, help, trust), and Style (empathy words). You need to recognize that trust is modeled by combining sentiment, lexical categories about friends/help/trust, and stylistic empathy cues, not just general positive affect.

qa.question: Which social dimension of relationships is most closely associated with the keywords "trustworthy, honest, reliable, dependability, loyalty, faith"?
qa.answer: Trust.
qa.explanation: The table in the figure lists the ten social dimensions of relationships and their corresponding keywords. The keywords "trustworthy, honest, reliable, dependability, loyalty, faith" are listed under the social dimension of "Trust".
qa.student: Trust.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: What relationship types are easier to predict than others?
qa.answer: Knowledge and conflict relationship types are easier to predict than others.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the AUC of LSTM models trained on the Reddit data and tested on the other datasets. The AUC is a measure of how well the model can distinguish between positive and negative examples. The higher the AUC, the better the model is at making predictions. The figure shows that the AUC for knowledge and conflict is higher than for the other relationship types.
qa.student: Conflict and identity relationships are easier to predict than trust or romance, because they have the highest AUC values across datasets.
qa.feedback: You misread which bars are highest on the AUC axis. Higher AUC means the relationship type is easier to predict. In the left plot, support, fun, and knowledge consistently have the highest AUC values (around 0.9–0.98), not conflict or identity, whose bars are lower (around 0.8 or below). Re‑checking the bar heights against the y‑axis will help you correctly identify which relationship types are easier to predict.

qa.question: Which of the social dimensions shows the most significant decline after the first financial concerns are raised?
qa.answer: Status
qa.explanation: The plot for "Status" shows a sharp decline after the first financial concerns are raised, indicated by the vertical dashed line.
qa.student: Status giving shows the most significant decline after the first financial concerns are raised.
qa.feedback: You correctly picked one dimension that declines, but you did not compare it to the other dimensions as the question asks. From the plots, status, knowledge, and power all drop sharply after the first concerns, with knowledge and power also showing strong declines similar to status. To answer fully, you need to note that several dimensions (status, knowledge transfer, and power-based exchanges) all experience major declines rather than naming only one without justification or comparison.

qa.question: Which of the ten dimensions has the strongest association with income?
qa.answer: Knowledge
qa.explanation: The table shows the standardized beta coefficients (β) for each of the ten dimensions, as well as for population density. The beta coefficient for Knowledge is 1.140, which is the largest beta coefficient for any of the dimensions in the income column. This indicates that Knowledge has the strongest association with income.
qa.student: The dimension with the strongest association with income is Similarity, because it has the largest negative beta value.
qa.feedback: You are focusing only on the sign and magnitude of the negative coefficient for Similarity and inferring “strongest” from that. To compare associations, you should look at the absolute size of the standardized β coefficients for all dimensions predicting income. Knowledge has β = 1.140, which in absolute value is larger than the β for Similarity (−0.597) and any other dimension. This means variation in Knowledge is most strongly related to income in this regression, regardless of it being positive.

qa.question: Which social dimension is most predictive of education, income, and suicide rate in the US?
qa.answer: Knowledge is most predictive of education and income, while support is most predictive of suicide rate.
qa.explanation: The figure shows three scatter plots, each with a different outcome variable (education, income, and suicide rate) on the y-axis and knowledge or support on the x-axis. The plots are annotated with a few representative US states. The R2 values indicate the strength of the linear relationship between each outcome variable and its most predictive social dimension.
qa.student: Education is most predicted by the support dimension, income is most predicted by knowledge, and suicide rate is most predicted by knowledge as well.
qa.feedback: You misread which social dimension is associated with each outcome on the x-axes. In the first and second plots, the horizontal axis is labeled “knowledge,” so knowledge is the most predictive dimension for both education and income. In the third plot, the horizontal axis is labeled “support,” so support is the most predictive dimension for suicide rate, not knowledge. Higher values on each x-axis correspond to higher levels of that specific social dimension, so it is important to match the correct label to each outcome.

qa.question: How does the Tinghy game represent the relationship between two people?
qa.answer: The Tinghy game represents the relationship between two people by allowing the player to select blocks that represent different dimensions of their relationship, such as similarity, trust, romance, and conflict. These blocks are then added to a "friendship wall" that visually depicts the relationship.
qa.explanation: The figure shows a screenshot of the Tinghy game, with two profiles and a set of blocks representing different relationship dimensions. The player can select blocks to add to the friendship wall, which visually represents the relationship between the two people.
qa.student: The Tinghy game represents the relationship between two people by showing them as two profile pictures (the player and a Twitter friend) and asking the player to describe the relationship.
qa.feedback: You correctly noticed that the game uses the two profile pictures to indicate which people the relationship is about, but you left out how the relationship itself is encoded. The core representation is the set of colored blocks added to the “friendship wall” in the middle. The player selects 1–3 relationship dimensions from the menu on the left (such as similarity, trust, romance, etc.), and each choice adds a corresponding block to the tower. This block structure is what actually represents the relationship between the two people in the game.

qa.question: What type of social expression is conveyed in the highlighted sentence?
qa.answer: The highlighted sentence conveys a combination of social support and similarity.
qa.explanation: The speaker is expressing support for the listener by offering advice and acknowledging their shared experience as introverts.
qa.student: The highlighted sentence mainly conveys similarity because the speaker says “as a fellow introvert,” showing they are alike. It doesn’t really express social support, since it’s more about shared identity than helping or comforting the listener.
qa.feedback: You correctly notice the similarity aspect (“as a fellow introvert”), but you misunderstand what counts as social support. Social support is not only practical help; it also includes emotional comfort, empathy, and reassurance. The phrase “you have my sympathy” is explicitly supportive and comforting. So this sentence jointly expresses both similarity (shared introversion) and social support (offering sympathy), not just similarity.

Each dataset (reddit, enron, movies, twitter) has its own vertical axis, and for this question you only need to compare the conflict bar heights within that shared interpretation of “higher bar = more occurrences.” The reddit axis being logarithmic in parts of the paper would not change which bar is tallest in this figure. qa.question: Which method appears to learn the fastest in each plot?
qa.answer: In plot (a), PTF-A3C appears to learn the fastest. In plot (b), PTF-PPO appears to learn the fastest. In plot (c), Deep-CAPS appears to learn the fastest.
qa.explanation: The plots show the average discounted reward as a function of the number of training episodes. The method that learns the fastest is the one that reaches the highest reward in the fewest number of episodes.
qa.student: In all three plots, the baseline methods (A3C, PPO, and Deep-CAPS) learn the fastest because their curves rise more sharply at the beginning compared to the PTF-based methods.
qa.feedback: You correctly referred to the learning speed as how quickly the average return increases with training episodes, but you misread which curves rise faster. In each subplot, higher values on the y-axis mean better average return, and curves that reach high returns with fewer episodes are learning faster. The plots show that the PTF variants (PTF-A3C, PTF-PPO, and PTF-A3C in the third plot) all climb to high returns earlier than the corresponding baselines (A3C, PPO, and Deep-CAPS). So it is actually the PTF-based methods, not the baselines, that learn the fastest in each plot.

qa.question: Which method has the highest average discounted reward?
qa.answer: PTF-A3C
qa.explanation: The figure shows the average discounted rewards of various methods when learning task g on grid world W. PTF-A3C has the highest average discounted reward, as its line is the highest on the y-axis.
qa.student: PTF-A3C has the highest average discounted reward.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified that PTF-A3C reaches the highest average discounted reward, but you did not mention that this is true across all three comparisons in the figure (vs A3C, vs PPO through PTF-PPO, and vs Deep-CAPS). To fully answer the question, you should state that among all the methods shown, PTF-A3C consistently achieves the highest average return over training episodes.

qa.question: What is the learning rate for the policy network?
qa.explanation: The learning rate for the policy network is shown in the table as 3e-4.
qa.student: The learning rate for the policy network is 1e-3.
qa.feedback: You correctly looked at the hyperparameter table, but you mixed up the two different learning rates. The table lists a learning rate of 3e-4 for the policy network and 1e-3 for the option network. Your error comes from conceptually assuming both networks share the same learning rate or misattributing the value to the wrong network. Always match the specific network name (policy vs. option) to its corresponding value.

qa.question: What is the learning rate used in the PPO algorithm?
qa.answer: The learning rate used in the PPO algorithm is 3e-4.
qa.explanation: The table in the figure shows the hyperparameters used in the PPO algorithm, and the learning rate is listed as 3e-4.
qa.student: The learning rate used in the PPO algorithm is 3e-4.

qa.question: What is the learning rate used in the CAPS algorithm?
qa.answer: The learning rate used in the CAPS algorithm is 3e-4.
qa.explanation: The table in the figure lists the hyperparameters used in the CAPS algorithm, and the learning rate is one of them.
qa.student: The learning rate used in the CAPS algorithm is 1e-3.

qa.question: What is the role of the discount factor in the A3C algorithm?
qa.answer: The discount factor controls how much weight is given to future rewards in the agent's decision-making process.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the discount factor is set to 0.99, which means that future rewards are discounted by 1% for each time step. This encourages the agent to focus on short-term rewards, but still take into account the potential for long-term rewards.
qa.student: The discount factor γ is set to 0.99 in the A3C algorithm.
qa.feedback: You only stated the numeric value from the table but did not describe its role. The question asks what the discount factor does conceptually in A3C. You need to explain that γ controls how much future rewards are weighted relative to immediate rewards when computing returns, with values close to 1 (like 0.99) placing strong emphasis on long-term rewards.

qa.question: Which of the methods is the most effective in terms of average discounted rewards?
qa.answer: PTF-A3C is the most effective method in terms of average discounted rewards.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that PTF-A3C achieves the highest average discounted rewards across all three subplots.
qa.student: PPO is the most effective method because its curve rises quickly and reaches the highest average discounted reward among all the methods.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up the methods and their performance. In all three panels, the PTF-based methods (PTF-A3C and PTF-PPO, shown in blue) reach higher average returns than their non-PTF counterparts (A3C, PPO, Deep-CAPS). The curves for PTF-A3C and PTF-PPO consistently lie above the others at the same training episodes, meaning they achieve better average discounted rewards. PPO alone is not the best; it is outperformed by PTF-PPO, and overall the PTF variants are the most effective.

qa.question: Which algorithm performed better in the grid world W with imperfect source policies?
qa.answer: Deep-CAPS
qa.explanation: The plot shows the average return for both algorithms over the course of training. The Deep-CAPS line is consistently higher than the PTF-A3C line, indicating that Deep-CAPS achieved a higher average return.
qa.student: Deep-CAPS performed better than PTF-A3C because its red curve stays higher across the training episodes, indicating a higher average return.
qa.feedback: You correctly referred to the two algorithms and to average return, but you misread which curve is higher. On this plot, higher on the y-axis means better average return. The blue PTF-A3C curve climbs above the red Deep-CAPS curve early and stays consistently higher throughout training, reaching close to 4 while Deep-CAPS stays around 2–3. So, factually, PTF-A3C, not Deep-CAPS, performed better in grid world W with imperfect source policies.

qa.answer: Pinball and Reacher.
qa.explanation: The figure shows two different environments: Pinball and Reacher. In Pinball, the goal is to control the ball to hit the targets. In Reacher, the goal is to control the arm to reach the target. Both environments require continuous control of the agent's actions.
qa.student: One of the evaluation environments with continuous control is Pinball.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified Pinball as one of the continuous control environments, but you left out the second one shown in the figure. The image and caption indicate there are two environments: (a) Pinball and (b) Reacher. To fully answer the question, you need to name both.

qa.question: What are the three main modules of the policy transfer framework?
qa.answer: The three main modules of the policy transfer framework are the agent module, the option module, and the policy transfer function (PTF).
qa.explanation: The figure shows the three modules and their interactions with each other and the environment. The agent module takes in states and outputs actions. The option module takes in states and outputs options. The PTF takes in options and outputs new options.
qa.student: The three main modules of the policy transfer framework are the Agent, the Option, and the Environment.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up components of the overall RL setup with the specific modules of the policy transfer framework. The environment is not a module of the framework; it is the external system the agent interacts with. The three main modules shown are: (a) the overall Policy Transfer Framework (PTF) box that connects everything, (b) the Agent Module (containing actor and critic with their losses), and (c) the Option Module (containing termination, option, and source policies with their transfer losses). You should distinguish between the framework’s internal modules and the surrounding environment.

To answer this question fully, you need to look across all three subplots and identify which curve is highest at 2 million episodes among A3C, PTF-A3C, PPO, PTF-PPO, and Deep-CAPS. qa.question: How does the performance of PTF-A3C compare to the performance of A3C?
qa.answer: PTF-A3C performs slightly better than A3C.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the average return for both algorithms over the course of training. The PTF-A3C curve is consistently higher than the A3C curve, indicating that PTF-A3C achieves a higher average return.
qa.student: PTF-A3C performs worse than A3C overall, because its curve is more variable and often lies below the A3C curve, indicating lower average return across training.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up variability with worse performance. The vertical axis is average return, so higher values mean better performance. Although PTF-A3C (blue) has some variance, its mean curve lies consistently above the A3C (green) curve over most of the training episodes, especially after the initial phase, reaching higher final returns. So despite similar noise levels, PTF-A3C actually performs better, not worse.

qa.question: Which algorithm resulted in the highest switch frequency?
qa.answer: PTF-PPO
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the switch frequency for PTF-PPO is higher than for PTF-A3C in almost all episodes.
qa.student: The PTF-A3C algorithm resulted in the highest switch frequency because its bars reach up to about 50 switches, which is higher than what is seen for PTF-PPO.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on comparing the heights of the bars (switch numbers) between the two plots, but you misread which has the higher values. In the left plot (PTF-A3C), the maximum switch number is just under 50, while in the right plot (PTF-PPO), the tallest bars reach close to 70. Since higher bars mean a higher switch frequency, PTF-PPO actually has the higher switch frequency, especially in the earlier episodes.

2002.09286v1-Figure4-1.png.caption: This block diagram shows the full pipeline for the proposed model. Inside the solid black box are the operations which are trained when using a fixed transform. The dashed boxes contain the additional operations trained in our setup.

2002.09286v1-Figure2-1.png.caption: On the left we show the connectivity pattern enforced by a DFT matrix. It is a dense linear layer since the DFT matrix application can be represented by a single dense matrix multiply. On the right we show the sparser connectivity pattern and bit-reversal enforced by the DIT-FFT algorithm. The FFT connectivity structure makes all matrices in the FFT factorization trainable except for the bit-reversal permutation matrix.

2002.09286v1-Figure3-1.png.caption: The left plot contains the analysis window. It has fairly regular high frequency patterns. The right plot shows the synthesis window. Interestingly, it is two peaked. Both windows have changed considerably from their intializations.

2002.09286v1-Figure5-1.png.caption: The masking network is composed of three layers: two linear layers, and a unidirectional gated recurrent unit layer. This network is causal for real-time speech enhancement. The real and imaginary components of the transformed input are stacked before being fed through the network. Similarly, the output is interpreted as the real mask stacked on top of the imaginary mask.

2002.09286v1-Table1-1.png.caption: Comparison of speech enhancement performance on the VCTK test set using several perceptual metrics. We compare performance between several front-end setups. For the trainable window/FFT attributes we use Xwhen this attribute was trainable and× when it was not. In this results table we include the loss as defined in the training section. For the loss, lower is better. Finally, the best score for each metric is displayed in bold.

2002.09286v1-Figure1-1.png.caption: The DIT-FFT algorithm computes the Fourier transform as a series of sparse matrix multiplies. This figure demonstrates the structure involved in these matrices. The solid diagonal lines indicate the positions of the non-zero elements in the matrices, and the grey squares represent 2× 2 DFT matrices.

qa.question: What is the role of the RNN in the proposed model?
qa.answer: The RNN is used to predict the mask.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the RNN takes the output of the FFT as input and outputs a prediction. This prediction is then used to create the mask.
qa.student: The RNN is used in the model to process the features after the FFT and help improve the final output waveform.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that the RNN operates after the FFT, but you leave out its specific function. The diagram labels it as a “Mask Prediction RNN,” indicating that its role is to predict a time–frequency mask that is then applied (multiplication node) to the transformed mixture signal. This mask suppresses noise and preserves speech, leading to the clean output waveform. You also did not mention that this mask‑prediction block is one of the components additionally trained in the proposed setup (shown in a dashed box).

qa.question: How does the connectivity structure of the DFT differ from that of the FFT?
qa.answer: The DFT has a dense connectivity structure, meaning that each input is connected to every output. In contrast, the FFT has a sparser connectivity structure, meaning that each input is only connected to a few outputs.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the connectivity structure of the DFT and FFT. In the DFT, each input node is connected to every output node. In the FFT, each input node is only connected to a few output nodes. This difference in connectivity structure is due to the fact that the FFT is a more efficient algorithm for computing the DFT.
qa.student: The DFT and FFT have basically the same connectivity, because both transform every input into every output. The only real difference is that the FFT just applies a bit-reversal permutation before doing the same dense connections, so its structure is effectively still a single fully connected layer like the DFT.
qa.feedback: You are right that both transforms relate all inputs to all outputs mathematically, but you are misunderstanding the structural difference. In the DFT diagram, every input node connects directly to every output node in one dense layer: that is a fully connected matrix. In contrast, the FFT diagram shows multiple sparse stages: each stage only connects certain pairs of nodes, and a fixed bit-reversal permutation reorders inputs between stages. So the FFT is not “effectively a single fully connected layer”; it factorizes the DFT into several sparse layers plus a permutation, which is why its connectivity is much sparser at each step.

qa.question: How does the learned window for the analysis window compare to the initialization?
qa.answer: The learned window for the analysis window is more complex than the initialization. The initialization is a smooth curve, while the learned window has a lot of high-frequency variation.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the learned window (solid black line) is much more jagged than the initialization (dashed grey line). This suggests that the learned window is able to capture more of the nuances of the data than the initialization.
qa.student: The learned window for the analysis window is more complex than the initialization. The initialization is a smooth curve, while the learned window has a lot of high-frequency variation.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: What is the purpose of the linear layers in the masking network?
qa.answer: The linear layers are used to transform the input data into a form that is more suitable for the GRU layer.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the linear layers are located before the GRU layer, and that they are used to process the real and imaginary components of the transformed input. This suggests that the linear layers are used to extract features from the input data that are relevant for the GRU layer.
qa.student: The linear layers are used to split the complex FFT input back into separate real and imaginary parts before it is processed by the GRU.
qa.feedback: You misread how the real and imaginary parts are handled. According to the figure and caption, the real and imaginary components are already stacked together before entering the network; the first linear layer does not split them. Instead, the linear layers apply learned linear transformations: the first maps the stacked complex input into a feature space suitable for the GRU, and the second maps the GRU output into the real and imaginary masks stacked as [Mr; Mi].

qa.question: Which front-end setup performed the best in terms of the perceptual metric C_sig?
qa.answer: The front-end setup with a trainable window and a non-trainable FFT performed the best in terms of the perceptual metric C_sig.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the C_sig value for the front-end setup with a trainable window and a non-trainable FFT is 3.624, which is the highest value among all the front-end setups.
qa.student: The setup with both the window and FFT trainable performed best.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified which setup gives the highest C_sig value (3.686), but your answer is incomplete because you did not specify the corresponding condition shown in the table. You need to clearly state that the best C_sig performance occurs in the configuration where both the window and the FFT are trainable (the column with ✓ under Trainable Window and ✓ under Trainable FFT), not just say “both trainable” without tying it explicitly to the C_sig metric from the table.

qa.question: What is the relationship between the matrices $W_m$ and $W_{m-1}$?
qa.answer: The matrix $W_{m-1}$ is obtained from the matrix $W_m$ by removing the first row and column.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the matrix $W_m$ has a dashed line around its first row and column, indicating that these elements are not used in the matrix $W_{m-1}$.
qa.student: The matrix \(W_{m-1}\) is just a scaled-down version of \(W_m\); it has the same diagonal pattern but with fewer rows and columns, so it performs the same operation on a smaller signal.
qa.feedback: You are assuming \(W_{m-1}\) is simply a smaller copy of \(W_m\), but the figure indicates a different structural relationship. \(W_m\) has single diagonal bands of non-zero entries, while \(W_{m-1}\) is organized into block structure (shown with dashed boxes) that contains smaller diagonal patterns. This reflects how the FFT factorization breaks the transform into stages where each \(W_{m-1}\) rearranges and combines outputs from \(W_m\) in a more complex way than just repeating the same operation on a smaller vector. The relationship is hierarchical and block-structured, not just a scaled-down duplicate.

2002.09758v3-Figure1-1.png.caption: Overview: Using unsupervised learning, we decompose a multi-hop question into single-hop subquestions, whose predicted answers are given to a recomposition model to predict the final answer.

2002.09758v3-Table2-1.png.caption: Left: Decompositions improve QA F1 for all 4 HOTPOTQA types. Right (Ablation): QA model F1 when trained with various sub-answers: the sentence of the predicted sub-answer, predicted sub-answer span, or random entity from the context. We also train models with (X) or without (7) sub-questions/sub-answers.

2002.09758v3-Figure3-1.png.caption: Multi-hop QA is better when the single-hop QA model answers with the ground truth “supporting fact” sentences. We plot mean and std. over 5 QA runs.

2002.09758v3-Figure7-1.png.caption: Performance difference between a QA model that does vs. does not use ONUS decompositions, stratified by whether the gold final answer is in a subanswer sentence. We find a larger gain when the subanswer sentence contains the gold, final answer.

2002.09758v3-Figure2-1.png.caption: One-to-N Unsupervised Sequence transduction (ONUS): Step 1: We create a corpus of pseudodecompositions D by finding candidate sub-questions from a simple question corpus S which are similar to a multi-hop question in Q. Step 2: We learn to map multi-hop questions to decompositions using Q and D as training data, via either standard sequence-to-sequence learning (Seq2Seq) or unsupervised sequence-to-sequence learning (for ONUS).

2002.09758v3-Table4-1.png.caption: Analysis of sub-questions produced by our method vs. the supervised+heuristic method of Min et al. (2019b). qa.question: What are the two subquestions that the model decomposes the original question into?
qa.answer: SQ1: What profession does H. L. Mencken have? and SQ2: Who was Albert Camus?
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the original question ("What profession do H. L. Mencken and Albert Camus have in common?") is decomposed into two subquestions, SQ1 and SQ2. These subquestions are then answered by the Single Hop QA Model, and the answers are then combined by the Recomposition Model to answer the original question.
qa.student:
The model decomposes the original question into: 
1) "Who was H. L. Mencken?" and 
2) "Who was Albert Camus?"
qa.feedback: You correctly identified that each subquestion is about one of the two people, but you misread the actual wording shown in the figure. The first subquestion (SQ1) is “What profession does H. L. Mencken have?” not “Who was H. L. Mencken?”. The second subquestion (SQ2) is “Who was Albert Camus?”. The key point is that the first subquestion is specifically about Mencken’s profession, as indicated in the diagram.

qa.question: How does the use of decompositions affect the QA F1 score for different types of HOTPOTQA questions?
qa.answer: The use of decompositions generally improves the QA F1 score for all four types of HOTPOTQA questions.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the QA F1 score is higher for all four types of questions when decompositions are used. This suggests that decompositions are helpful for improving the performance of QA models on HOTPOTQA.
qa.student: Using decompositions increases the QA F1 score for all HOTPOTQA question types. For example, Bridge questions go from about 80.1 to 81.7, and 1-hop questions go from about 73.9 to 76.9.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that decompositions improve QA F1 and give examples for Bridge and 1-hop questions, but you leave out how they affect the other two types. To fully answer the question you need to mention that Comparison and Intersection questions also see gains (their F1 scores increase when using decompositions). The question asks about “all 4 HOTPOTQA types,” so you should describe the effect for each type, not just a subset.

qa.question: Which model performs best when the single-hop QA model answers with the ground truth “supporting fact” sentences?
qa.answer: The Multi-hop Dev model.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the Multi-hop Dev model has the highest F1 score when the single-hop QA model answers with the ground truth “supporting fact” sentences. This means that the Multi-hop Dev model is better at answering questions that require multiple hops of reasoning.
qa.student: The multi-hop dev model performs best, because it’s specifically designed to use supporting fact sentences and its orange curve ends up at the top.
qa.feedback: You are right that the orange curve is the multi-hop dev model, but you are misinterpreting which curve is highest overall. Performance is measured by Answer F1 on the y-axis, and higher values mean better performance. Across most of the dev set, the gray “Original Dev” curve is consistently above both the orange (Multi-hop Dev) and blue (Out-of-Domain Dev) curves. This means the Original Dev model actually performs best when given the ground truth supporting fact sentences, not the multi-hop dev model.

qa.question: Which development set has the largest difference in performance between the model that uses ONUS decompositions and the one that does not?
qa.answer: The Out-of-Domain Dev set.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the difference in F1 score between a QA model that does and does not use ONUS decompositions. The Out-of-Domain Dev set has the largest difference in F1 score, with a difference of approximately 12 points.
qa.student: The Out-of-Domain Dev set.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: What is the purpose of the ONUS model?
qa.answer: The ONUS model aims to learn to map multi-hop questions to decompositions of sub-questions.
qa.explanation: The figure shows how the ONUS model works. First, a corpus of pseudo-decompositions is created by finding candidate sub-questions from a simple question corpus that are similar to a multi-hop question. Then, the model is trained to map multi-hop questions to decompositions using the pseudo-decompositions and the original multi-hop questions as training data.
qa.student: The purpose of the ONUS model is to directly answer hard multi-hop questions without breaking them into smaller parts.
qa.feedback: You are claiming ONUS answers hard questions directly, but the figure and caption show the opposite. ONUS is a “One-to-N Unsupervised Sequence transduction” model that learns to map a single multi-hop (hard) question in Q to a sequence of simpler sub-questions (a decomposition) in D. Step 1 builds a corpus of pseudo-decompositions, and Step 2 trains ONUS (or a Seq2Seq model) to produce those decompositions. Its purpose is decomposition, not direct answering.

qa.question: Which decomposition method resulted in questions that were more similar to the multi-hop questions in terms of length?
qa.answer: DecompRC
qa.explanation: The table shows that the Length Ratio for DecompRC is 1.22, which is higher than the Length Ratio for ONUS (1.08). This indicates that the questions produced by DecompRC were on average longer than the questions produced by ONUS, and therefore more similar in length to the multi-hop questions.
qa.student: The ONUS method produced questions that were more similar in length to the multi-hop questions.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified ONUS as producing questions more similar in length (it has a length ratio of 1.08 vs. 1.22 for DecompRC, and a ratio closer to 1 means more similar length). However, your answer is incomplete because you did not explain this comparison or mention that DecompRC’s larger length ratio indicates its decomposed questions are less similar in length to the original multi-hop questions.

qa.question: Are both Coldplay and Pierre Bouvier from the same country?
qa.answer: No
qa.explanation: The passage states that Coldplay is a British rock band, while Pierre Bouvier is a Canadian singer.
qa.student: Yes, because they are both musicians from English-speaking countries, so they must be from the same country.
qa.feedback: You are confusing language and profession with nationality. The sub-questions explicitly give the countries: Coldplay are described as a British rock band, while Pierre Bouvier is described as Canadian. Even though both are musicians and English-speaking, “British” and “Canadian” are different nationalities, so they are not from the same country.

qa.question: How does the addition of UDs and 1hop-Train affect the QA F1 score of the downstream, recomposition model?
qa.answer: The addition of UDs and 1hop-Train increases the QA F1 score of the downstream, recomposition model.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the QA F1 score is higher for the models that include UDs and 1hop-Train than for the model that does not include these components.
qa.student: Adding UDs alone consistently improves the QA F1 score over the RoBERTa baseline at all data sizes, while adding 1hop-Train alone consistently hurts performance. When both UDs and 1hop-Train are used together, they give the worst F1 scores overall.
qa.feedback: You are correctly focusing on how each condition compares to the RoBERTa baseline, but you misread the curves. The orange “+ UDs” line is slightly below (not above) the blue RoBERTa line at most data fractions, so UDs alone do not consistently improve F1. The green “+ 1hop-Train” line is slightly above the baseline across data sizes, so 1hop-Train alone helps, not hurts. The red “+ 1hop-Train + UDs” line is clearly the highest, giving the best F1 scores, not the worst. Remember that in this plot higher F1 values mean better performance, so the topmost line at each x-position corresponds to the best setting.

qa.question: What is the effect of using decompositions on the performance of the multi-hop QA model?
qa.answer: The use of decompositions generally improves the performance of the multi-hop QA model, as evidenced by the higher F1 scores achieved by the model with decompositions compared to the model without decompositions.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the model with decompositions achieves a higher F1 score than the model without decompositions for all epochs except the first one. This suggests that the use of decompositions helps the model to learn more effectively and achieve better performance.
qa.student: Using decompositions improves the multi-hop QA model’s performance compared to not using decompositions, as shown by the higher QA F1 scores across training epochs.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that decompositions improve performance over the no-decomposition baseline, but you are missing key details about how this effect behaves over training. The figure shows that with decompositions, QA F1 increases over the first few decomposition training epochs, peaks around epochs 4–5, and then slightly declines, while the “without decomps” curve stays flat at a lower score. You should mention both the existence of this peak and that the chosen stopping point (epoch 3) gets close to the best observed performance.

qa.question: How does the performance of the recomposition model vary with the confidence of the sub-answers?
qa.answer: The performance of the recomposition model is higher when the single-hop QA model places high probability on its sub-answer. This is shown in the right panel of the figure, where the F1 score of the recomposition model is higher for sub-answer confidence ranks of 80-100% than for lower confidence ranks.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the F1 score of the recomposition model is higher for sub-answer confidence ranks of 80-100% than for lower confidence ranks. This suggests that the recomposition model performs better when the single-hop QA model is more confident in its sub-answers.
qa.student: The performance of the recomposition model decreases as the confidence of the sub-answers increases. The bars on the right plot show that when the single-hop QA model is more confident (higher confidence ranks), the F1 score goes down for all three datasets.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on the bar plot for sub-answer confidence, but you reversed the relationship. Higher sub-answer confidence ranks (moving from 0–20% to 80–100%) correspond to higher F1 scores, not lower ones. In other words, when the single-hop QA model is more confident in its sub-answers (i.e., assigns them higher probability), the recomposition model performs better. Your mistake is treating higher confidence as if it harmed performance, instead of recognizing that higher bars (higher F1) mean improved performance at higher confidence levels.

qa.question: Which model benefits the most from decomposition?

qa.explanation: The table shows the QA F1 scores for different models with and without decomposition. The ROBERTA_LARGE model has the highest F1 score with decomposition (80.1 ± 2) and the largest increase in F1 score from without to with decomposition (3.1).
qa.student: ROBERTA_LARGE.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: Which decomposition method generated the most sub-questions?
qa.answer: PseudoD + Random
qa.explanation: The table shows that PseudoD + Random generated three sub-questions, while all other methods generated two sub-questions.
qa.student: The ONUS+ FastText method generated the most sub-questions.
qa.feedback: You misread which method produced the largest number of sub-questions. In the table, ONUS+ FastText creates 2 sub-questions (Sub-Q1 and Sub-Q2). The Variable ONUS+ FastText method creates 3 sub-questions (Sub-Q1, Sub-Q2, and Sub-Q3), which is more than any other method listed, so that is the correct answer.

qa.question: Which decomposition method produces the most coherent sub-questions?
qa.answer: This is subjective, but some might argue that DecompRC produces the most coherent sub-questions, as they are both directly related to the original question and are clear and concise.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the sub-questions generated by different decomposition methods. DecompRC generates the sub-questions "Is Coldplay from which country?" and "Is Pierre Bouvier from which country?" These sub-questions are both directly related to the original question and are clear and concise.
qa.student: DecompRC.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified DecompRC, but your answer is incomplete because you do not explain why it is the most coherent. To fully answer, you need to note that DecompRC’s sub-questions (“Is Coldplay from which country?” and “Is Pierre Bouvier from which country?”) stay on-topic and directly address the original question about whether they are from the same country, unlike the other methods whose sub-questions drift off-topic or become nonsensical.

qa.question: What is the difference between the DecompRC and PseudoD + Random decomposition methods?
qa.answer: DecompRC decomposes the question into two sub-questions, while PseudoD + Random decomposes the question into two different sub-questions.
qa.explanation: The DecompRC method decomposes the question into two sub-questions that are both related to the original question. In contrast, the PseudoD + Random method decomposes the question into two sub-questions, one of which is related to the original question and one of which is not.
qa.student: There is no real difference between DecompRC and PseudoD + Random; both methods decompose the original question into two subquestions about birth dates using essentially the same strategy.
qa.feedback: You are misunderstanding how the two methods decompose the question. PseudoD + Random does not reliably generate the correct subquestions for this example: its Sub-Q1 asks “What did Decent Phase I become?” and Sub-Q2 asks “What group can amend the Victorian constitution?”, which are completely unrelated to the original age-comparison query. In contrast, DecompRC produces two focused, relevant subquestions: “Annie Morton is born when?” and “Terry Richardson is born when?”, both directly tied to the needed birth dates. The key difference is that DecompRC yields targeted, semantically appropriate subquestions, whereas PseudoD + Random can produce unrelated, off-topic ones.

qa.question: Which decomposition method and pseudo-decomposition retrieval method combination resulted in the highest QA F1 score on the Dev set?
qa.answer: The combination of SAE decomposition method and HGN pseudo-decomposition retrieval method resulted in the highest QA F1 score of 82.2 on the Dev set.
qa.explanation: The table shows the QA F1 scores for all combinations of decomposition methods and pseudo-decomposition retrieval methods. The highest score for the Dev set is highlighted in bold.
qa.student: The best QA F1 score on the Dev set comes from using the Seq2Seq decomposition method with FastText pseudo-decomposition retrieval, achieving 80.1.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on combinations that use both a decomposition method and a pseudo-decomposition retrieval method, but you misread which one has the highest Dev score. In the Dev column, higher values mean better performance. The maximum Dev F1 is 80.1, but it is obtained by the ONUS decomposition method with FastText retrieval (not Seq2Seq with FastText). Double-check the row labels: both Seq2Seq+FastText and ONUS+FastText are highlighted, but ONUS+FastText is the one with 80.1 on Dev.

qa.question: Which decomposition method achieves the highest F1 score on the HOTPOTQA Dev F1 OOD set?
qa.answer: ONUS with FastText achieves the highest F1 score on the HOTPOTQA Dev F1 OOD set.
qa.explanation: The table shows the F1 scores for different decomposition methods on the HOTPOTQA Dev F1 OOD set. The ONUS with FastText method has the highest F1 score of 77.1.
qa.student: The ONUS method with FastText pseudo-decompositions achieves the highest F1 score on the HOTPOTQA Dev F1 OOD set, with a score of 77.1.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified that ONUS with FastText pseudo-decompositions is the best among the unsupervised decomposition methods in the “Ours” group, and you gave the right F1 value (77.1). However, you did not consider the supervised baselines below. In the HOTPOTQA Dev F1 OOD column, HGN (a supervised method using supporting fact supervision) has a higher F1 score of 77.6, which is the overall best. You need to compare all rows in that column, not just the unsupervised ones, to identify the true top performer.

qa.question: What is the main difference between the FEVER 1.0 and FEVER 2.0 datasets?
qa.answer: The FEVER 1.0 dataset contains simple questions that can be answered by looking up a single fact in a knowledge base. The FEVER 2.0 dataset contains more complex questions that require reasoning over multiple facts.
qa.explanation: The table shows examples of questions from both datasets. The FEVER 1.0 questions are all factual and can be answered with a single piece of information. The FEVER 2.0 questions require more complex reasoning, such as understanding the relationships between different entities or events.
qa.student: FEVER 1.0 contains simple, single-fact questions, while FEVER 2.0 has more complex questions that require multiple facts and reasoning steps.
qa.feedback: You are right that there is a progression in complexity, but you are mischaracterizing FEVER 1.0 as only simple, single-fact questions. The examples in the table show that both FEVER 1.0 and FEVER 2.0 involve factual claims that can need multiple pieces of evidence; what changes is more the style and specificity of the claims. FEVER 1.0 examples include shorter, more direct factual statements (e.g., about Everest or a person’s birth date), whereas FEVER 2.0 has richer, more context-laden claims (co-founding a band before a date, starring in a specific film, geographic and historical details). The main difference is not that FEVER 1.0 is single-fact while FEVER 2.0 is multi-fact, but that FEVER 2.0 questions are more detailed and nuanced, often embedding extra constraints or relations.

qa.question: What does the close proximity of the red and blue dots in the first column of the figure indicate?
qa.answer: The close proximity of the red and blue dots in the first column of the figure indicates that the corresponding interactions are similar in the embedding domain.

You need to connect this to the spatial-domain plots: the figure is showing that when two interactions are close in the embedding space (first column), their corresponding traffic scenes and trajectories (middle and right columns) are also similar. In other words, proximity in the embedding domain reflects similarity of real-world traffic interactions, not just similarity of abstract vectors.

qa.question: How does the model's prediction of pedestrian trajectory compare to the ground truth and other methods?
qa.answer: The model's prediction of pedestrian trajectory is more accurate than SGAN and closer to the ground truth than both SGAN and GT_Pred.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the predicted trajectory of a pedestrian compared to the ground truth and other methods. The green line represents the ground truth observed trajectory, the dashed green line represents the GT_Pred trajectory, the red dashed line represents the model's prediction (Our_Pred), and the blue dashed line represents the SGAN prediction. It can be seen that the model's prediction is closer to the ground truth than both SGAN and GT_Pred.
qa.student: The model’s predicted trajectories (red dashed lines) mostly follow the same general direction as the ground-truth future paths (green dashed), but they are less accurate than SGAN (blue dashed), which tends to match the ground truth more closely in these examples.
qa.feedback: You are correctly comparing the three types of trajectories, but you have reversed the relative performance. In these plots, “Our_Pred” (red dashed) is designed to be the improved method over the SGAN baseline (blue dashed). If you look closely at several panels, the red dashed lines consistently stay closer to the green dashed ground-truth futures than the blue ones do (for example, in the top middle and bottom right plots, SGAN veers away while our model tracks the turn/curvature better). Your conceptual mistake is assuming the baseline is generally more accurate; instead, the figure is illustrating that the new model’s predictions align more closely with the ground truth than SGAN’s.

qa.question: Which of the two metrics, ADE or FDE, is more sensitive to the inclusion of the NMMP module?

qa.student: FDE.
