qa.question: What is the effect of pruning on the quality of video super-resolution?
qa.answer: Pruning can improve the quality of video super-resolution.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the pruned models (BasicVSR-lite, BasicVSR-uni-lite, L1-norm-bi, L1-norm-uni, ASSL-bi, ASSL-uni, SSL-bi, SSL-uni) generally produce sharper and more detailed images than the unpruned models (BasicVSR, BasicVSR-uni). This is because pruning removes redundant or unimportant weights from the model, which can improve its efficiency and accuracy.
qa.student: Pruning generally lowers the quality of video super-resolution. The pruned models (like the “lite” and SSL versions) show more blur and artifacts compared to the unpruned HR and full BasicVSR outputs, so pruning mainly hurts detail reconstruction to save computation.
qa.feedback: You are right that pruning can hurt quality if done naively, but you are misunderstanding what the figure shows. The comparison includes several pruned schemes, and not all of them degrade quality noticeably. In particular, the SSL-based pruned models (ours) visually recover details and edges very close to the unpruned baselines and HR, unlike simpler pruning (e.g., L1-norm) that can cause blur. The key idea is not that pruning inherently lowers VSR quality, but that with an appropriate pruning strategy, you can keep most of the visual quality while reducing the model, and in some cases even produce sharper, cleaner results than other VSR or pruning methods.

qa.feedback: You misread how the curves change with iteration. The y-axis shows average scaling factors, where higher values mean larger scaling. As iterations increase, the pruned filters’ average scaling factors rapidly drop toward zero, but the kept filters’ average scaling factors actually increase above 1.0 and then level off, not decrease toward zero.

qa.student: The VSR network is made up of a residual block network and an upsampling network.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up components inside the networks with the two main networks themselves. From panel (a), the VSR architecture with bidirectional recurrent structure is composed of a Forward Network and a Backward Network. Both of these internally contain residual blocks and connect to an upsampling network, but the question asks specifically for the two types of networks that make up the VSR network, which are the forward and backward networks, not “residual” and “upsampling” networks.

qa.question: What is the difference between the two sentences in the image?
qa.answer: The first sentence describes a physical property of the factory, while the second sentence describes a non-physical property.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the two sentences have the same syntactic structure, but the words used in the predicate of each sentence are different. The first sentence uses the adjective "dusty" to describe a physical property of the factory, while the second sentence uses the adjective "cheerful" to describe a non-physical property.
qa.student: The main difference is in the verb tense: the first sentence uses the present simple plural form “are,” while the second uses the present simple singular form “is.” Otherwise the sentences are the same.
qa.feedback: You are right that the verbs differ (“are” vs. “is”), but the important difference is not just verb tense. The figure highlights that the subject nouns also differ in number: “areas” is plural (tagged NNS) and correctly pairs with the plural verb “are” (VBP), while “news” is treated as singular (tagged NN) and correctly pairs with the singular verb “is” (VBZ). The contrast is about singular vs. plural agreement between the subject and the verb, not only a generic tense change.

qa.question: What is the best performance achieved by the MPoSM model on the 45-tag English Penn WSJ dataset?
qa.answer: The best performance achieved by the MPoSM model on the 45-tag English Penn WSJ dataset is 78.6 (±1.7) for the M-1oR metric and 77.9 (±1.8) for the M-1 metric.
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of different models on the 45-tag English Penn WSJ dataset. The MPoSM model is listed in the first row of the table, and its performance is shown in the second and third columns for the M-1oR and M-1 metrics, respectively.
qa.student: The best performance of the MPoSM model is 78.6 on the M-1OR metric.
qa.feedback: You correctly picked out the highest MPoSM score (78.6 on M-1OR for “MPoSM + emb”), but you ignored that the question is about performance on the dataset in general, not just one metric. To be complete, you should also mention the corresponding M-1 score (77.9) for that same best-performing MPoSM variant, since both metrics summarize performance on the 45-tag WSJ dataset.

qa.question: Which model performs the best on the universal treebank?
qa.answer: Gupta et al. (2022)
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of different models on the universal treebank. The model with the highest performance is Gupta et al. (2022) with a score of 81.7.
qa.student: The MPoSM + mBERT_OR model performs the best on the universal treebank because it uses mBERT and generally has higher numbers than the others.
qa.feedback: You are right that MPoSM + mBERT_OR is strong, but you are assuming that using mBERT automatically makes it the best overall. To decide which model performs best, you must compare the actual scores across languages. If you look carefully, Gupta et al. (2022)_OR has higher accuracies than MPoSM + mBERT_OR on almost every language (for example, de: 81.7 vs 77.5, es: 79.5 vs 77.0, pt-br: 78.9 vs 78.1). The conceptual mistake is relying on the presence of mBERT instead of checking which row actually has the highest values across the table.

qa.student: The sentences generated for D(0) have randomly generated characters in every word, while the sentences generated for MORPH have words with useful morphological features.

qa.question: Which model achieved the highest percentage of perfect runs on the synthetic dataset?

However, you must look across all columns and models to find the highest percentage. The maximum percentage of perfect runs shown is 95% for MPoSM (width=1) on D(0), so you should compare only the percentages in parentheses, not the bold M-1 scores.

qa.question: How do the sizes of the predicted clusters compare to the sizes of the gold clusters?
qa.answer: The predicted clusters are generally larger than the gold clusters.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the bars for the predicted clusters are generally taller than the bars for the gold clusters. This means that the predicted clusters contain more words than the gold clusters.
qa.student: The predicted clusters and gold clusters have very similar sizes overall.
qa.feedback: You only mentioned that the sizes are similar overall, but you did not describe how they differ across the range. You should note that for the largest clusters the gold sizes are slightly larger than the predicted ones, whereas for smaller clusters the predicted sizes become larger than the gold sizes. This trend across cluster ranks is essential to fully answer the question.

qa.question: What is the performance of the MP-OSM model with a width of 2 on the German language dataset?
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of the MP-OSM model on four different languages. The second row of the table shows the performance of the MP-OSM model with a width of 2. The second column of the table shows the performance on the German language dataset.
qa.student: The performance of the MP-OSM model with a width of 2 on the German dataset is about 71.8, the same as the full model, because reducing the width doesn’t affect German.
qa.feedback: You are confusing the full MPoSM model with the width=2 variant. In the table, “de (uni)” is the German dataset. The first row (full) has 71.8, but the second row (width=2) has 68.5. You need to distinguish between these two configurations: reducing the width does change performance on German, and it lowers it from 71.8 to 68.5.

qa.question: What is the maximum number of times the sequence "o1 o2" can appear in a sentence in D(2-4)?
qa.answer: Two.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the "o1 o2" block can appear at most once between "n" and "v" and can also appear at most once between the start symbol and "n".
qa.student: The maximum number of times the sequence "o1 o2" can appear in a sentence in D(2-4) is three times.
qa.feedback: You are correctly looking for the maximum number of repetitions allowed by the diagram, but you miscount them. The diagram shows an initial “o1 o2” block on the left that can appear at most once, and then another “o1 o2” block between the n-tag and the v-tag that can also loop at most once. This means you can have one “o1 o2” before n, and one “o1 o2” between n and v, for a total maximum of two occurrences, not three.

qa.question: Which of the three synthetic subsets has the most specific regular expression?
qa.explanation: The table shows that the regular expression for D(0) is the shortest and most specific of the three, as it only allows for a single occurrence of the pattern "n v". The regular expressions for MORPH and D(2-4) are longer and allow for more variations in the pattern.
qa.student: The D(2-4) subset has the most specific regular expression.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified D(2-4) as having the most specific regex, but you did not explain why. To fully answer, you need to point out that its pattern explicitly constrains the distance between n and v to be between 2 and 4 and has a more detailed structure than D(0) and MORPH, whose regex is simpler and only allows distance 0 (with MORPH just adding morphological features).

qa.question: Which context type performs the best on Korean?
qa.answer: MPoSM (width=2)

For Korean, the best-performing context type is actually “MPoSM-Word (width=2)” with accuracy 62.2, which is higher than the 55.2 for “MPoSM (full)”. qa.question: What is the difference between the local POS prediction module and the masked POS reconstruction module?
qa.answer: The local POS prediction module predicts the POS tags for each word in the sentence, while the masked POS reconstruction module reconstructs the POS tags for masked words.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the local POS prediction module takes the word and character embedding of each word as input and outputs the POS tag for each word. The masked POS reconstruction module takes the POS tags of all words in the sentence as input and outputs the POS tags for masked words.
qa.student: The local POS prediction module predicts the POS tags for each word in the sentence, while the masked POS reconstruction module reconstructs the POS tags for masked words.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

For the context type z_{i-2}, z_{i-1}, the mutual information values are: de 0.56, en 0.86, es 0.65, fr 0.66, id 0.39, it 0.57, ja 0.74, ko 0.27, pt-br 0.59, sv 0.59. Since higher numbers mean higher mutual information, English (0.86) is the highest, not Japanese (0.74).

qa.question: How does the size of the predicted clusters compare to the size of the gold clusters?
qa.answer: The predicted clusters are generally smaller than the gold clusters.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the log-scale sizes of the predicted clusters and the gold clusters. The bars representing the gold clusters are generally taller than the bars representing the predicted clusters, indicating that the gold clusters are larger.
qa.student: The predicted clusters are consistently smaller than the gold clusters, meaning the model systematically underestimates cluster sizes across the whole range.
qa.feedback: You are treating the relationship as one-sided, but the plot shows a more nuanced pattern. On the log-scale y-axis, higher bars mean larger clusters. For large clusters (left side), predicted sizes (blue) are often slightly larger than gold (orange), not smaller. In the middle range they are quite similar, and only for many of the smaller clusters (right side) do gold clusters become smaller than predictions or disappear. Your mistake is assuming a uniform “predicted < gold” relationship instead of recognizing that the comparison varies with cluster size.

MPoSM (full) has the highest value, 95.31%, which is larger than 87.62% for MPoSM (width=2). So the best method on the D(2-4) dataset is MPoSM (full), not MPoSM (width=2).

qa.question: What is the relationship between the amount of TeO2 and the density of the LBPT samples?
qa.answer: There is a positive correlation between the amount of TeO2 and the density of the LBPT samples.
qa.explanation: The table shows that as the amount of TeO2 increases, the density of the LBPT samples also increases.
qa.student: As the amount of TeO2 decreases across the LBPT samples, their density steadily increases, showing that TeO2 makes the glass denser.
qa.feedback: You correctly noticed there is a systematic change in TeO2 and density across the samples, but you reversed the relationship. From LBPT0 to LBPT4, TeO2 content decreases (40 → 20 mol%), and at the same time the density increases (4.97 → 5.31 g·cm⁻³). This means that lower TeO2 corresponds to higher density in these samples, so TeO2 is associated with lower, not higher, density in this series.

qa.question: Which table type has the most tuples in the training set?
qa.answer: NC
qa.explanation: The table shows that the NC table type has 2761 tuples in the training set, which is more than any other table type.
qa.student: NC.

qa.question: What are the different types of embeddings used by TAPAS-ADAPTED to represent table cells?
qa.answer: The different types of embeddings used by TAPAS-ADAPTED to represent table cells are: cell embeddings, column index embeddings, row index embeddings, MatSciBERT embeddings, token embeddings, position embeddings, and segment embeddings.
qa.explanation: The figure shows how TAPAS-ADAPTED uses different types of embeddings to represent table cells. The cell embeddings capture the semantic meaning of the cell content. The column and row index embeddings capture the position of the cell in the table. The MatSciBERT embeddings capture the contextual information of the cell content. The token embeddings capture the individual words in the cell content. The position embeddings capture the position of each word in the cell content. The segment embeddings capture whether the word belongs to the table caption or the table content.
qa.student: TAPAS-ADAPTED represents table cells using only column index embeddings, row index embeddings, and token embeddings.
qa.feedback: You left out one of the embedding types and misread another. The figure shows that each table cell embedding is formed by summing four components: column index embeddings (CID), row index embeddings (RID), MatSciBERT embeddings (MSB), and cell embeddings (CELL). Token, position, and segment embeddings at the bottom are for the text sequence going into MatSciBERT, not the final table cell representation itself.

qa.question: What is the relationship between the amount of bromine and the percentage of selenium in the (GeSe2)1−x(GeBr4)x and (GeSe2)1−xBrx samples?
qa.answer: The percentage of selenium decreases as the amount of bromine increases.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the percentage of selenium (ρSe) decreases as the value of x, which represents the amount of bromine, increases. This trend is observed in both the (GeSe2)1−x(GeBr4)x and (GeSe2)1−xBrx samples.
qa.student: As the amount of bromine increases, the percentage of selenium decreases.
qa.feedback: You correctly noticed that increasing bromine is associated with a decrease in selenium, but your answer is incomplete because it ignores that this trend is shown separately for two kinds of samples: (GeSe2)1−x(GeBr4)x and (GeSe2)1−xBrx. You should explicitly mention that in both of these sample types, higher bromine content corresponds to lower selenium percentage, as indicated by the pSe(%) and pBr(%) rows in the table.

qa.question: What is the difference between the glass compositions of GLSC10 and GLSC40?
qa.answer: The glass composition of GLSC10 is (80GeSe2-20Ga2Se3)90-(CsCl)10, while the glass composition of GLSC40 is (72GeSe2-28Ga2Se3)90-(Li2S)10.
qa.explanation: The table in the image shows the glass compositions of different samples. The composition of each sample is given in terms of the mole percentages of the different components.
qa.student: GLSC10 and GLSC40 have the same base glass composition; the only difference is that they have different refractive index values, with GLSC40 having a slightly higher index than GLSC10.
qa.feedback: You are focusing on the refractive index n instead of the compositional variable that defines these glasses. In this table, the composition of the glass is expressed as 20La₂S₃ – (80 – x)Ga₂S₃ – xCsCl, so changing from GLSC10 to GLSC40 changes x, i.e., the CsCl mol% in the glass. GLSC10 contains 10 mol% CsCl, whereas GLSC40 contains 40 mol% CsCl; that is the primary compositional difference. The change in refractive index is a consequence, not the defining distinction in composition.

qa.question: What are the two possible outcomes of the SCC Predictor?
qa.answer: The two possible outcomes of the SCC Predictor are "Yes" and "No."
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the SCC Predictor is a diamond-shaped decision point in the process. This means that it is used to make a decision based on some criteria. The two possible outcomes of this decision are "Yes" and "No," as indicated by the arrows leaving the diamond.
qa.student: The SCC Predictor can output either “Yes,” meaning the table is a PI table, or “No,” meaning the table is not a PI table.
qa.feedback: You are confusing the roles of the SCC Predictor and the PI Table Predictor. From the diagram, the SCC Predictor only decides whether the table should go directly to the rule-based composition parser (“Yes”) or continue through GNN₂ and the PI Table Predictor (“No”). It does not label tables as PI or non‑PI; that is the job of the PI Table Predictor later in the pipeline.

qa.question: Which hyper-parameter has the biggest difference between GNN1 and GNN2?
qa.answer: Peak LR for LM.
qa.explanation: The table shows the values of different hyper-parameters for two different GNNs. The Peak LR for LM is 1e-5 for GNN1 and 2e-5 for GNN2, which is the biggest difference between the two models.
qa.student: The GAT attention heads have the biggest difference, because GNN1 uses [4, 4, 4] while GNN2 uses [6, 4, 4].
qa.feedback: You correctly noticed a difference in the GAT attention heads, but you did not compare all the hyper-parameters. Several others also differ between GNN1 and GNN2: GAT hidden layer sizes, peak LR for LM, RegEx feature embedding size, max-frequency feature embedding size, constraint penalty, and the edge loss weight (which is only present for GNN2). To answer the question fully, you need to identify that multiple hyper-parameters differ and then explain which one changes the most in magnitude or structure, rather than focusing on just one.

qa.question: What is the most common type of table that is misclassified by the model?
qa.answer: MCC-CI
qa.explanation: The confusion matrix shows that 11 MCC-CI tables were misclassified as SCC tables. This is the highest number of misclassifications for any table type.
qa.student: The most commonly misclassified table type is NC, because it has the largest number in its row (361), so the model is often wrong about that class.
qa.feedback: You are confusing total instances of a class with misclassifications. In a confusion matrix, the diagonal entries (like 98 for SCC, 121 for MCC-CI, 81 for MCC-PI, and 361 for NC) are correct classifications. Misclassifications are the off-diagonal values in each row, so you need to sum those for each true label and compare. When you do that, SCC has 15 misclassifications (2+2+11), MCC-CI has 11, MCC-PI has 27, and NC has 19. Therefore, MCC-PI is the most commonly misclassified table type, not NC.

qa.question: What is the difference between the learning process for few-shot image classification using pretext tasks in previous works and the work presented in this paper?
qa.answer: Previous works use all images indiscriminately, while the work presented in this paper exploits the hierarchical tree structure to adaptively select useful feature representations.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the learning process for previous works (b, c) and the work presented in this paper (d, e). In (b) and (c), all images are used indiscriminately, while in (d) and (e), the hierarchical tree structure is used to select useful feature representations.
qa.student: Previous works use all images indiscriminately, while the work presented in this paper exploits the hierarchical tree structure to adaptively select useful feature representations.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: What is the role of the subscript and superscript in the figure?
qa.answer: The subscript represents the number of child nodes, and the superscript represents the number of levels.
qa.explanation: This is explicitly stated in the caption of the figure.
qa.student: The subscript indicates the level of the TreeLSTM, and the superscript denotes the index of the child node.
qa.feedback: You have swapped the meanings. The caption states that in this figure the subscript represents the number (index) of the child nodes, while the superscript represents the level in the hierarchy. So subscripts label which child a node is, and superscripts show at which level that node lies.

qa.question: How does the number of child nodes affect the accuracy of the ProtoNet and Rotation methods?
qa.answer: The accuracy of the ProtoNet and Rotation methods increases as the number of child nodes increases.
qa.explanation: This can be seen in panels (c) and (d) of the figure. Panel (c) shows the accuracy of the ProtoNet method for different numbers of child nodes, while panel (d) shows the accuracy of the Rotation method for different numbers of child nodes. In both cases, the accuracy increases as the number of child nodes increases.
qa.student: For both ProtoNet and Rotation, increasing the number of child nodes always leads to higher accuracy; the more child nodes you add, the better both methods perform, with no decline.
qa.feedback: You are overgeneralizing the effect of child nodes. The rotation method (panel c) does improve as you increase child nodes from 0 to 3, but when you go from 3 to 4, its accuracy slightly drops, so it is not “always” better with more nodes. ProtoNet’s accuracy is not shown as varying with the number of child nodes in panels (c) and (d); only Rotation and Color Permutation are plotted there. This means you cannot conclude that ProtoNet’s accuracy keeps increasing with more child nodes based on these subfigures.

qa.question: Which dataset shows the most improvement in accuracy when the number of levels increases from 1 to 4 in the 5-way-1-shot setting?
qa.answer: CIFAR-FS
qa.explanation: In the 5-way-1-shot setting (panel c), the accuracy for CIFAR-FS increases from around 45% at level 1 to almost 70% at level 4, which is the largest improvement among all the datasets.
qa.student: The CUB-200-2011 dataset shows the most improvement in accuracy when the number of levels increases from 1 to 4 in the 5-way-1-shot setting.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on the 5-way-1-shot plot (panel c), but you misidentified which curve improves the most from level 1 to level 4. Higher points on the vertical axis mean higher accuracy. As the number of levels increases from 1 to 4, all datasets first go up and then drop, and the CUB-200-2011 (orange) and CIFAR-FS (red) curves end up with only a small net increase or even a slight decrease by level 4. In contrast, the miniImageNet (blue) curve shows the largest net gain in accuracy between level 1 and level 4, so miniImageNet is the dataset with the most improvement across that range.

qa.question: Which method appears to be the best at separating the five classes of data?
qa.answer: TreeLSTM.
qa.explanation: The TreeLSTM plot shows the clearest separation between the five classes of data. The other methods, ProtoNet and Backbone, show some overlap between the classes.
qa.student: ProtoNet appears to be the best at separating the five classes, because the colored clusters are more spread out across the space, so they must be more distinguishable from one another than in the other two methods.
qa.feedback: You are focusing on how “spread out” the points are in the plot, but for t-SNE the key is how well points of the same class cluster together and how little they overlap with other classes. In (a) ProtoNet, many colors overlap in the central region, indicating poorer separation. In (b) Backbone and especially (c) TreeLSTM, each color forms a tighter cluster with clearer gaps between different colors, meaning better class separation. So you should base your judgment on cluster compactness and inter-class gaps, not just how widely the points are spread over the plot.

qa.feedback: You have reversed the meaning of the colors. According to the caption and the legend, higher values on the color key (red) indicate unobservable traffic information, while lower values (blue) indicate observable traffic information where it is entirely recorded. The red areas are where traffic data cannot be seen, and the blue areas are where it is fully available.

To fully answer the question, you need to mention both the layout (size and number of intersections) and this difference in traffic directionality.

qa.feedback: You are misinterpreting how to read the bars and what RMSE means. The figure states “The lower, the better,” so a smaller RMSE indicates better performance. The ASTGCN w/ action (red bar) is shorter and positioned at a lower RMSE value than the original ASTGCN (blue bar). This means adding traffic management actions improves, not worsens, the model’s performance.

STGCN has MAE 0.0563, but Ours (WaveNet) has a lower MAE of 0.0378, which is the best performance. Carefully compare all MAE values and pick the minimum one.

qa.answer: DTIGNN

The table shows three metrics (MSE, RMSE, MAPE), and lower values are better for all of them. “Ours” has the lowest value in each metric, not just MSE. To fully justify why it performs best, you should refer to all three metrics instead of focusing on only one.

qa.question: What is the predict time slice of DTIGNN?
qa.explanation: The predict time slice is listed in the table under the parameter column.
qa.student: The predict time slice of DTIGNN is 30, since that’s the time window the model looks at when making predictions.
qa.feedback: You’re confusing two different time-related parameters. The table lists “Historical time slice T = 30” and “Predict time slice T′ = 1.” The historical time slice (30) is the past window the model uses as input, while the predict time slice is how far ahead or how many future steps it predicts. Since the question asks specifically for the predict time slice, you should use T′, which is 1, not the historical window of 30.

qa.question: What is the difference between the static adjacency matrix and the phase-activated adjacency matrix?
qa.answer: The static adjacency matrix represents the connectivity between road segments based on the road network structure, while the phase-activated adjacency matrix represents the connectivity between road segments based on the current state of the traffic signals.
qa.explanation: The static adjacency matrix in (d) shows which road segments are connected to each other, regardless of the state of the traffic signals. For example, road segment 1 is connected to road segments 2, 4, and 8. The phase-activated adjacency matrix in (e) shows which road segments are connected to each other when the traffic signals are activated. For example, when the traffic signals are activated as shown in (b) and (c), road segment 1 is connected to road segments 2 and 8, but not to road segment 4.
qa.student: The static adjacency matrix and the phase-activated adjacency matrix are the same; in both, all upstream segments that intersect are connected to all possible downstream segments regardless of the signal status.
qa.feedback: You are misreading the matrices. In (d), the static adjacency matrix has grey off-diagonal cells showing all potential connections between upstream and downstream road segments that are physically possible at the intersection, independent of signal status. In (e), the phase-activated adjacency matrix only turns on a subset of those potential links (colored and grey cells) that are actually allowed under the currently activated signal phase; other possible connections in the static matrix become white (not connected) when their signal is red.

qa.question: Which method performs better in terms of queue length when using MaxPressure, baseline or DTIGNN?
qa.answer: DTIGNN performs better than baseline in terms of queue length when using MaxPressure.
qa.explanation: Figure (b) shows the queue length of MaxPressure using predictions from baseline (left) and DTIGNN (right). The color bar indicates that lower values are better, and DTIGNN has lower values overall than baseline.
qa.student: DTIGNN performs better than the baseline in terms of queue length when using MaxPressure.
qa.feedback: You identified which method performs better but did not mention the key detail that “better” here means achieving a lower average queue length across intersections. The figure shows that, under MaxPressure, DTIGNN consistently yields smaller queue lengths than the baseline at almost all observed intersections, reflecting its advantage in this task.

qa.question: How does DTIGNN perform compared to the baseline models in terms of MAE, RMSE, and MAPE?
qa.answer: DTIGNN achieves better performance than the baseline models in terms of MAE, RMSE, and MAPE.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the bars representing DTIGNN are consistently lower than the bars representing the baseline models for all three metrics. This indicates that DTIGNN has lower error values, which implies better performance.
qa.student: DTIGNN performs worse than the baseline models because its MAE, RMSE, and MAPE bars are generally higher than those of the base models across WaveNet, ASTGNN, and ASTGCN.
qa.feedback: You are misinterpreting what the bar heights mean for these metrics. MAE, RMSE, and MAPE are error measures where lower values indicate better performance. In all three plots, the red bars (DTIGNN + base model) are consistently lower than the blue bars (base model alone) for each architecture. This means DTIGNN actually reduces the errors and thus performs better than the baseline models, not worse.

qa.question: How does the performance of the DTIGNN model compare to the ASTGNN model under different levels of sparsity on DHZ?
qa.answer: The DTIGNN model consistently outperforms the ASTGNN model under different levels of sparsity on DHZ.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the MAE, RMSE, and MAPE of the DTIGNN and ASTGNN models under different levels of sparsity on DHZ. The DTIGNN model has lower MAE, RMSE, and MAPE than the ASTGNN model at all levels of sparsity, indicating that it performs better.
qa.student: DTIGNN performs worse than ASTGNN at low sparsity (6.25%) but gradually overtakes it as sparsity increases, and by 75% sparsity DTIGNN has lower MAE, RMSE, and MAPE than ASTGNN.
qa.feedback: You misread the curves for low sparsity. In all three plots (MAE, RMSE, MAPE), the red line (DTIGNN) is below the blue line (ASTGNN) at every sparsity level, including 6.25%. Since lower values mean better performance here, DTIGNN is consistently better than ASTGNN across all sparsity levels; it does not start worse and then overtake it. Be careful to compare the y‑axis values correctly: higher on the y‑axis means larger error, so the model with the lower line is better.

qa.question: What is the role of the Attention Matrix in the DTIGNN network?
qa.answer: The Attention Matrix is used to learn the importance of different spatial and temporal dependencies between nodes in the graph. This allows the network to focus on the most relevant information for making predictions.
qa.explanation: The Attention Matrix is shown in Figure 3(a) as a block within the Base GNN Module. The Base GNN Module is used to learn spatial and temporal dependencies between nodes in the graph. The Attention Matrix allows the network to learn which dependencies are most important for making predictions.
qa.student: The Attention Matrix in the DTIGNN network is used inside the Attention-based Spatial-Temporal GNN layer to capture relationships among nodes. It helps the model focus on the most relevant node interactions when making predictions.
qa.feedback: You correctly noted that the Attention Matrix captures node relationships, but you left out how it is actually used in the full framework. In DTIGNN, the Attention Matrix is not only part of the spatial-temporal GNN layer, it is also passed into the Neural Transition Layer (together with the phase-activated adjacency and state tensor) to predict the next-step state tensor. This means its role extends beyond just “focusing on relevant nodes” in a GNN block; it explicitly informs the transition dynamics used for forecasting and imputation across time.

qa.student: The DTIGNN with \( \mathcal{L}_{N-N} \) performs the best on the DHZ dataset because it has the highest values in the table across MSE, RMSE, and MAPE.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified one specific method from the table, but you misunderstood how to interpret the metrics. The caption states “The lower, the better,” meaning smaller MSE, RMSE, and MAPE indicate better performance, not higher values. When you compare the numbers, the “Ours” method has the lowest values for all three metrics, so it actually performs best on the DHZ dataset.

qa.question: Which similarity measure is the most sensitive to small changes in similarity?
qa.answer: The cosine similarity measure.
qa.explanation: The cosine similarity measure has the sharpest peak of all the measures shown in the figure, which means that it is the most sensitive to small changes in similarity. This can be seen in the bottom panel of the figure, where the cosine similarity measure is shown in green.
qa.student: The bag of words TF-IDF similarity is the most sensitive to small changes in similarity.
qa.feedback: You are misinterpreting what the histograms show. A measure that is highly sensitive to small changes will spread sentence pairs over a wider range of similarity scores instead of clustering them all near one value. In the top plot, the bag-of-words TF-IDF scores are mostly concentrated very close to 0, while the character 4‑gram TF-IDF scores are more spread out between 0 and 1. This indicates that the char 4‑gram measure, not bag of words, is more sensitive to small differences in similarity.

qa.question: Which algorithm variant performed the best in terms of precision, recall, and F1-score?
qa.answer: The MST-LIS algorithm variant performed the best in terms of precision, recall, and F1-score.
qa.explanation: The top plot in the figure shows the precision, recall, and F1-score for all algorithm variants evaluated on the ground truth. The MST-LIS algorithm variant has the highest values for all three metrics.
qa.student: The max MST-LIS variant performed the best.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified that max MST-LIS is the best variant, but your answer is incomplete because you did not state that it is best across all three metrics: it has the highest precision, the highest recall, and therefore the highest F1-score among all algorithm variants. When a question asks “in terms of precision, recall, and F1-score,” you should explicitly mention that the same variant is best on all three measures.

qa.question: How many sentences are there in the German dataset?
qa.explanation: The table shows that there are 5,942 sentences in the German dataset. This is the number of unique sentences in German with n corresponding Simple German sentences.
qa.student: There are 89,411 sentences in the German dataset, since that’s the largest total number shown in the table.
qa.feedback: You are confusing sentences with tokens. In this table, the first row labeled “Sentences” gives the counts of sentences, and the second row labeled “Tokens” gives the counts of word tokens. The value 89,411 is under “Tokens,” not “Sentences.” To find the number of sentences in the German dataset, you should look at the “Total” column in the “Sentences” row, which is 5,942.

qa.question: Which alignment strategy and similarity measure combination yields the highest average similarity?
qa.answer: MST-LIS and maximum.
qa.explanation: The table shows the average similarity values for different combinations of alignment strategy and similarity measure. The highest average similarity value is 0.71, which is achieved by the combination of MST-LIS and maximum.
qa.student: The highest average similarity is achieved by the MST alignment with cosine similarity at threshold 1.5, with a value of 0.78.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on the Average Similarity columns and looked for the largest value, since higher is better there. However, you misread the table values: for cosine similarity under MST with threshold 1.5, the average similarity is 0.77, not 0.78. That 0.77 is indeed the highest value in the Average Similarity columns, but your numeric value must match the table exactly.

qa.question: Which website in the table offers information about different social services?
qa.answer: There are three websites that offer information about different social services: (beb), (lmt) and (soz).
qa.explanation: The table shows the content of each website. The websites (beb), (lmt) and (soz) are all listed as offering information about different social services.
qa.student: The website behindertenbeauftragter.de (beb) offers information about different social services.
qa.feedback: You correctly picked a site that relates to social services (beb is an official office for disabled people), but the question asks which website in the table offers information about different social services, and there are three such sites: behindertenbeauftragter.de (beb), lebenshilfe-main-taunus.de (lmt), and sozialpolitik.com (soz). You need to mention all of them to fully answer the question.

qa.question: Which combination of similarity measure and matching algorithm results in the highest number of matched sentences?
qa.answer: The combination of "maximum" similarity measure and "MST" matching algorithm results in the highest number of matched sentences (32,499).
qa.explanation: The table shows the number of matched sentences for all combinations of similarity measure and matching algorithm. The highest number of matched sentences is highlighted in bold.
qa.student: The combination of SBERT similarity with the MST-LIS matching algorithm and a threshold of 1.5 gives the highest number of matched sentences, because SBERT is the most powerful model and MST-LIS tends to outperform MST.
qa.feedback: You correctly point to SBERT as part of the best combination, but you misunderstand which specific setting yields the maximum. The highest number of matched sentences is with SBERT and MST without any threshold (32,899). Adding MST-LIS or a threshold actually reduces the count. Your error comes from assuming the more complex algorithm (MST-LIS and thresholding) must perform best, instead of reading off the maximum value directly from the table.

qa.question: Which of the following sentences is an example of a partially aligned sentence pair?
qa.answer: "Diverse öffentliche Verkehrsmittel bieten eine optimale Anbindung an die Hamburger Innenstadt, die Autobahn sowie den Flughafen." and "They go all the way to the highway and the airport."
qa.explanation: The first sentence is in German and talks about various public transport options offering an optimal connection to Hamburg's city center, the highway, and the airport. The second sentence is in English and talks about how "they" go all the way to the highway and the airport. The two sentences are partially aligned because they both talk about transportation to the highway and the airport, but the first sentence also talks about the city center.
qa.student: "Diverse öffentliche Verkehrsmittel bieten eine optimale Anbindung an die Hamburger Innenstadt, die Autobahn sowie den Flughafen." and "They go all the way to the highway and the airport.".
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: What is the main reason for the increase in the price of waste paper?
qa.answer: The main reason for the increase in the price of waste paper is the increased demand for packaging materials due to the rise in online shopping during the Corona crisis.
qa.explanation: The figure shows a simple GUI used to create the ground truth of sentence alignments. The text on the left is a German article, and the text on the right is a simplified German article. The article discusses the increase in the price of waste paper, and it states that the main reason for this increase is the increased demand for packaging materials due to the rise in online shopping during the Corona crisis.
qa.student: The main reason is that fewer people are reading newspapers, so less waste paper is being produced.
qa.feedback: You misread the text. The GUI sentences state that prices increased mainly because the demand for packaging material and cardboard rose sharply during the Corona pandemic and with the growth of online shopping, not because there is less waste paper from newspapers. You need to base your answer on the explicitly mentioned cause: increased demand for packaging and cardboard.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the GUI shown in the figure?
qa.answer: The GUI is designed to allow users to evaluate the results of sentence matching.
qa.explanation: The figure shows a GUI with several examples of sentence matches. The user is asked to evaluate whether the sentences are similar or not. This suggests that the GUI is used to evaluate the performance of a sentence matching algorithm.
qa.student: It is used to evaluate sentence matches.
qa.feedback: You’ve only captured part of the purpose. The GUI is not just for “evaluating sentence matches” in general; it is specifically designed for the second manual evaluation to let annotators judge how well pairs of sentences match by choosing among “Similar,” “Not similar,” or “Undefined.” You need to mention that it is for manual assessment of match quality between example sentence pairs.

qa.question: Which source has the highest average number of tokens per sentence?
qa.answer: bra
qa.explanation: The "t/s" column in the table shows the average number of tokens per sentence for each source. The highest value in this column is 18.9, which corresponds to the source "bra".
qa.student: The source “koe” has the highest average number of tokens per sentence, because in both Simple German and German it has the largest total number of tokens.
qa.feedback: You are confusing total tokens with tokens per sentence. The column t/s gives the average tokens per sentence; higher values there mean longer sentences on average. For German, “beb” has the largest t/s (18.7), not “koe” (14.3). For Simple German, “taz” has the largest t/s (9.4), again not “koe”. You need to compare the t/s values, not the overall token counts t.

qa.question: Which matching strategy achieved the highest alignment classification accuracy with the MST-LIS method?
qa.answer: sbert
qa.explanation: The table shows the alignment classification accuracy for different matching strategies with both the MST and MST-LIS methods. The highest accuracy for MST-LIS is 0.56, which is achieved by the sbert strategy.
qa.student: With the MST-LIS method, the sbert matching strategy achieved the highest alignment classification accuracy.
qa.feedback: You correctly looked at the MST-LIS column, but you misidentified which value is the highest. For MST-LIS, higher accuracy values are better. The sbert strategy has an accuracy of 0.55, while the maximum strategy has 0.56, which is slightly higher and therefore the best. Carefully compare all the numbers in the MST-LIS column to find the largest one.

qa.question: Which similarity measure performed the best overall?
qa.answer: The average precision, recall, and F1-score for each similarity measure are shown in the last row of the table. The bipartite similarity measure has the highest average precision and F1-score, while the 4-gram cosine similarity measure has the highest average recall. Therefore, the bipartite similarity measure performed the best overall.
qa.explanation: The table shows the precision, recall, and F1-score for each similarity measure for each website. The average values for each measure are shown in the last row of the table. By comparing these values, we can determine which similarity measure performed the best overall.
qa.student: The best overall similarity measure is sbert because it has the highest average F1-score.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified sbert as the best overall measure, but your justification is incomplete. The table shows that sbert not only has the highest average F1-score, but also the highest average precision and recall among all similarity measures. To fully answer the question, you should mention that sbert outperforms the others across all three averaged metrics, not just F1.

Our proposed GENREAD with the InstructGPT reader (named GENREAD (InstructGPT)) can significantly outperform the original InstructGPT, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on three open-domain QA benchmarks (previous SoTA: 1GLaM (Du et al., 2022), 2FLAN (Wei et al., 2021)) under this setting without using any external document. Our GENREAD can achieve comparable or even better performance than zero-shot retrieve-then-read models that use a retriever or search engine to first obtain contextual documents. To ensure reproducibility, we use greedy search in decoding. All prompts used are shown in the §B.1. qa.question: Which model performed best on the TriviaQA dataset in the zero-shot open-domain QA setting?
qa.answer: DPR + InstructGPT
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of different models on various open-domain QA datasets in the zero-shot setting. The DPR + InstructGPT* model achieved the highest score of 53.8 on the TriviaQA dataset.
qa.student: The best-performing model on the TriviaQA dataset is Google + InstructGPT, since it has the highest overall capabilities as a search-based model even though its TriviaQA score is slightly lower than some others.
qa.feedback: You correctly focus on the TriviaQA column, but your conclusion is based on an assumption about “overall capabilities” instead of the actual metric being asked about. The question is which model scored highest on TriviaQA specifically, regardless of whether it uses a retriever or external documents. In the table, higher values mean better accuracy, and the highest value in the TriviaQA column is 59.0 for GENREAD (InstructGPT), which is higher than Google + InstructGPT’s 58.8. You should pick the model with the numerically highest score in that column, not the one you think is strongest in general.

qa.question: What is the most common answer to the query "What is the collection of the districts to the east of the Jordan River?"
qa.answer: Jordan
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the answer "Jordan" is given by the DPR model, the GPT nucleus model, and the GPT cluster model. The GPT cluster model also gives the answer "West Bank".
qa.student: Jordan.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: Which dataset has the largest number of training examples?
qa.answer: TriviaQA
qa.explanation: The table shows the number of training examples for each dataset. TriviaQA has the largest number of training examples, with 78,785.
qa.student: The NQ (Natural Questions) dataset has the largest number of training examples, with 79,168 training instances.
qa.feedback: You misread the training counts in the table. Higher values in the “Train” column mean more training examples. NQ has 79,168, but FEVER has 104,966 training examples, which is larger than any other dataset listed.

qa.question: How does the recall of the proposed clustering-based prompting method compare to DPR-Multi and Google search on the TriviaQA and WebQ test sets?
qa.answer: The proposed clustering-based prompting method has higher recall than DPR-Multi and Google search on both the TriviaQA and WebQ test sets.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the Recall@K for different methods on the TriviaQA and WebQ test sets. The proposed clustering-based prompting method is represented by the red line, DPR-Multi by the blue line, and Google search by the yellow line. The red line is consistently higher than the blue and yellow lines, indicating that the proposed method has higher recall.
qa.student: On both TriviaQA and WebQ, the clustering-based prompting method generally achieves higher recall than DPR-Multi and Google search.
qa.feedback: You correctly state that the clustering-based method has higher recall, but your answer is too vague. You need to mention that this improvement holds across different values of K (1, 5, 10, 15, 20) on both TriviaQA and WebQ, and that it outperforms both DPR-Multi and Google search at each of those K values. Including these details shows you understood how performance compares over the full range of top-K documents.

qa.question: Which type of music did Mozart compose the most, according to the GPT models?
qa.answer: Chamber music.
qa.explanation: The GPT models mention chamber music more than any other type of music. For example, GPT nucleus #1 mentions chamber music once, GPT nucleus #2 mentions it once, and GPT nucleus #3 mentions it once. Additionally, GPT cluster #1 mentions chamber music twice, GPT cluster #2 mentions it once, and GPT cluster #3 mentions it seven times. This suggests that the GPT models believe that Mozart composed more chamber music than any other type of music.
qa.student: According to the GPT models, Mozart composed opera the most.
qa.feedback: You are confusing which category appears most often in the GPT outputs with which category the models emphasize overall. In the figure, the summaries under both “GPT nucleus” and “GPT cluster” explicitly count how often each answer type appears in the top-10 generated documents. For GPT nucleus, “chamber music” appears 9 times, and for GPT cluster, “chamber music” appears 7 times, more than opera or classical music. Since higher counts mean that type is mentioned more frequently by the models, the GPT models consider chamber music as the type Mozart composed the most.

qa.question: What are the different ways in which the image depicts the relationship between Monsanto and agriculture?
qa.answer: The image shows that Monsanto is a major player in the agricultural industry, both through its production of agricultural chemicals and seeds, and through its ownership of agricultural businesses. The image also shows that Monsanto is a controversial company, with some people believing that it is an "evil corporation" that is responsible for producing harmful GMOs.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the results of a query about what Monsanto owns, and the answers include agricultural chemicals, seeds, agriculture, and the chemical industry. The figure also shows that Monsanto is a leading producer of genetically engineered seeds and Roundup, a glyphosate-based herbicide. Additionally, the figure mentions that Monsanto has been accused of suppressing data showing that GMOs cause harm.
qa.student: The image shows Monsanto mainly as a seed company in agriculture, because “seed” is highlighted and repeated most often. It also suggests Monsanto has only a minor role in agricultural chemicals and almost no broader agricultural involvement.
qa.feedback: You are correct that the figure emphasizes Monsanto’s role in seeds, but you misread how agriculture and agricultural chemicals are represented. The highlights and counts at the bottom show that Monsanto is repeatedly linked not only to seeds but also to agriculture and agricultural chemicals. For example, in the generated clusters, “agriculture” appears multiple times and “agricultural chemicals” is explicitly mentioned, indicating a substantial role in both areas, not just a minor or negligible one.

qa.question: Which model performs best on the FEVER dataset?
qa.answer: The model that performs best on the FEVER dataset is FiD.
qa.explanation: The table shows the accuracy of different models on the FEVER dataset. FiD has the highest accuracy of 90.2.
qa.student: The GenRead (FiD-xl) clustering model performs best on the FEVER dataset because it has the highest accuracy, 89.6.
qa.feedback: You correctly looked at the FEVER accuracy column, but you stopped at the clustering variant. The table shows one more row, “merge two source docs.”, with an even higher FEVER accuracy of 91.8. To answer fully, you need to scan all rows in the FEVER Acc. column and pick the highest value, which corresponds to the “merge two source docs.” model.

qa.question: Which model performed the best on the TriviaQA open test?
qa.answer: GENREAD (FiD-xl) (clustering)
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of different models on the TriviaQA open test. The model with the highest score is GENREAD (FiD-xl) (clustering), with a score of 54.4.
qa.student: The best-performing model on the TriviaQA open test is GENREAD (FiD-xl) with clustering, since it has the highest average score across all datasets.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up “best on TriviaQA” with “best on average across all datasets.” The question asks specifically which model did best on the TriviaQA open test, so you must look only at that column, not the Avg. column. The highest TriviaQA score (74.3) is from the model that merges retrieved documents with generated documents, not from GENREAD (FiD-xl) with clustering. Be careful to align the column you read with exactly what the question asks about.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the clustering-based prompting method?
qa.answer: The clustering-based prompting method is used to generate diverse documents that can be used to answer a question.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the method first clusters question-document pairs based on their embeddings. Then, it samples a pair from each cluster and uses it as a prompt to generate a new document. The new document is then read to predict an answer to the question.
qa.student: The clustering-based prompting method is used to generate diverse documents that can be used to answer a question.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: What are the different types of errors that GENREAD makes?
qa.answer:
The different types of errors that GENREAD makes are: 
- Data collection and annotation errors (12%)
- Model prediction errors (39%)
qa.explanation: The figure shows that out of 100 GENREAD predictions, 49 were correct and 51 were incorrect. The incorrect predictions were further categorized into data collection and annotation errors and model prediction errors.
qa.student: GENREAD makes three main types of errors: hallucinations, wrong retrieval, and temporal question errors.
qa.feedback: You listed only some of the error types and also misread the table. The figure divides incorrect predictions into “Good Q&A” and “Bad Q&A” categories, and under these it shows several specific error types. For GENREAD there are five distinct error types: hallucinations, wrong retrieval, no hit answers, wrong reading, temporal questions, and incomplete answers. You need to name all of these categories from the table.

qa.question: Based on the figure, what is the effect of adding large language model generated documents (LLM) to the DPR retrieved documents?
qa.answer: Adding large language model generated documents (LLM) to the DPR retrieved documents significantly improves the retrieval performance, as measured by Recall@k.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the Recall@k for DPR + LLM (red line) is consistently higher than the Recall@k for DPR only (blue line) for all three datasets (NQ-test, TriviaQA-test, and WebQ-test). This indicates that adding LLM documents to the DPR retrieved documents helps to improve the retrieval performance.
qa.student: Adding large language model generated documents to the DPR retrieved documents changes the recall performance across different datasets and top-k settings.
qa.feedback: You state that performance changes but you do not explain how. From the figure, you should say that adding LLM-generated documents consistently increases recall at all tested top‑k values and on all three datasets compared with using DPR documents alone, meaning retrieval becomes significantly better when the two sources are merged.

qa.question: How does sampling documents from each cluster affect the performance of the model compared to sampling documents from the entire data?
qa.answer: Sampling documents from each cluster generally leads to better performance than sampling documents from the entire data.
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of the model on different datasets, with different sampling strategies. For all datasets, the model performs better when documents are sampled from each cluster than when documents are sampled from the entire data. This suggests that sampling documents from each cluster is a more effective strategy for providing in-context demonstrations to the model.
qa.student: Sampling documents from each cluster makes the model perform worse because it restricts diversity; the R@10 and EM scores drop across all three datasets compared to sampling from the entire data.
qa.feedback: You are interpreting the effect in the wrong direction. Higher R@10 and EM values mean better performance. In the table, sampling 5 documents from each cluster slightly increases both R@10 and EM for all three datasets compared with sampling 5 from the entire data (for example, TriviaQA R@10 goes from 81.5 to 82.7, EM from 70.9 to 71.8). This shows that cluster-based sampling actually improves performance a bit, rather than harming it.

qa.question: Which model in the table achieves the highest performance on the WebQ open test set?
qa.answer: GENREAD.
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of different language models on different question-answering tasks. The WebQ open test set is one of the tasks, and GENREAD has the highest EM score of 24.8.
qa.student: The FLAN model achieves the highest performance on the WebQ open test set.
qa.feedback: You correctly looked at the WebQ open test column, but you misread which value is the highest. FLAN’s WebQ score is 0 (a dash indicates no reported value), not the top score. The highest WebQ open test score in the table is 24.8, achieved by GENREAD (InstructGPT). Always compare the numeric values in the specific column: here, higher numbers mean better performance.

qa.question: Which model performed the best on the TriviaQA dataset according to Recall@20?
qa.answer: DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) performed the best on the TriviaQA dataset according to Recall@20 with a score of 79.0.
qa.explanation: The table shows the retrieval performance of different models on various datasets. The Recall@K metric measures the percentage of relevant documents retrieved in the top K results. The highest Recall@20 score for TriviaQA is 79.0, which corresponds to the DPR model.
qa.student: The GENREAD (clustering prompts) model performed the best because it has the highest Recall@20 value on TriviaQA.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified GENREAD (clustering prompts) as the best model and noted that it has the highest Recall@20 on TriviaQA. However, you did not mention the actual Recall@20 value (85.1) or that this is higher than all other listed models’ Recall@20 scores on that dataset. When explaining which model performs best, you should state both the model name and the specific number that shows it outperforms the others.

qa.question: Which prompt achieved the highest validation score?
qa.answer: Prompt #1: "Generate a background document from Wikipedia to answer the given question."
qa.explanation: The table in the figure shows the top 10 human prompts and their corresponding validation scores. The validation score is a measure of how well the prompt performs on a given task. In this case, the task is to generate a background document to answer a given question. Prompt #1 achieved the highest validation score of 66.0.
qa.student: Prompt #4, “Generate a Wikipedia document to support the given question,” achieved the highest validation score because it is more specific about using Wikipedia than the others.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on the prompts and their wording, but you misunderstood which score is highest. The table shows numerical validation scores, where larger numbers mean better performance. Prompt #1 has a validation score of 66.0, which is higher than Prompt #4’s 63.5 and all others. The answer should be based on comparing these numbers, not on how specific the prompt sounds.

qa.question: Which model performed the best on the NQ dataset when using a retriever and trained directly on the dataset?

qa.question: Which prompt resulted in the highest average performance across all three test sets?
qa.answer: Prompt #1 (Generate ...)

qa.question: Which city is mentioned the most in the retrieved and generated documents as being the city that Zeus was the patron god of?

The figure explicitly states “Among top-10 retrieved documents: Olympia: 1 time” and “Among top-10 generated documents: Olympia: 9 times Elis: 1 time,” so you need to mention that Olympia is the most frequently mentioned city in both the retrieved and generated documents.

qa.question: Which method obtains the highest answer coverage for NQ, TriviaQA, and WebQ?
qa.answer: DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) obtains the highest answer coverage for NQ (67.9%), TriviaQA (76.5%), and WebQ (62.1%).
qa.explanation: The table shows the answer coverage (%) for each method and dataset. DPR has the highest values in each column.
qa.student: DPR has the highest answer coverage for all three datasets: NQ, TriviaQA, and WebQ.
qa.feedback: You are assuming one method dominates across all datasets, but the table shows different methods win on different columns. Higher numbers mean better answer coverage. DPR is best only for NQ (67.9). For TriviaQA, GenRead with clustering prompts is highest (20.4), and for WebQ, GenRead with clustering prompts is again highest (62.1). You need to compare each column separately instead of expecting the same method to be best everywhere.

qa.question: Which document retrieval method achieves the highest accuracy on WebQ?
qa.answer: GENREAD (clustering prompts) achieves the highest accuracy on WebQ with a score of 66.7.
qa.explanation: The table shows the accuracy of different document retrieval methods on three different datasets: NQ, TriviaQA, and WebQ. The accuracy of GENREAD (clustering prompts) on WebQ is higher than the accuracy of the other two methods.
qa.student: The DPR retrieval method achieves the highest accuracy on WebQ, with an accuracy of 63.3.
qa.feedback: You misread the WebQ column. Higher accuracy values are better, and the highest number in the WebQ column is 66.7, not 63.3. This value corresponds to GENREAD (clustering prompts), not DPR. DPR has 63.3 accuracy, which is lower than both GENREAD variants. Carefully compare all numbers in the WebQ column to identify the maximum.

qa.question: What is the effect of combining DPR retrieved documents and LLM generated documents on the EM score?
qa.answer: Combining DPR retrieved documents and LLM generated documents can achieve significantly better performance than using DPR retrieved documents only.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the EM score is higher for the "DPR (K=10) + LLM (10)" condition than for the "DPR only (K documents)" condition for all three datasets. This indicates that combining DPR retrieved documents and LLM generated documents improves performance.
qa.student: Combining DPR retrieved documents with LLM generated documents increases the EM score compared to using DPR documents alone.
qa.feedback: You correctly state that adding LLM-generated documents improves EM over DPR-only, but you leave out important details shown in the plots. You should mention that this improvement holds consistently across all three datasets (NQ, TriviaQA, WebQ) and for all tested TopK values (20, 40, 70, 100). You also do not note that, at every K, the red curve (DPR+LLM) is clearly above the blue curve (DPR only), indicating a substantial and consistent gain rather than a small or occasional one.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the table?
qa.answer: The table shows the results of a study on the temporality issues of the NQ dataset. The study compared the answers generated by different models (DPR and GENREAD) to the correct answers.
qa.explanation: The table lists several questions and the corresponding answers generated by DPR and GENREAD, as well as the correct answers.
qa.student: The purpose of the table is to compare how different QA systems (DPR, Google search, and GENREAD) perform on a set of time-dependent questions and to show that GENREAD generally gives the most accurate answers.
qa.feedback: You are right that the table compares answers from different systems on time-dependent questions, but you are misunderstanding its main purpose. The focus is not to prove that GENREAD is best; instead, the table is used to illustrate temporality problems in the NQ dataset itself. It contrasts the original question, the existing NQ labels, and the correct (time-aware) labels, then shows how the systems respond with respect to those. The key point is that the NQ labels can be outdated or incomplete for time-sensitive questions, which can mislead evaluation, not primarily that one model outperforms the others.

qa.question: Why is it easier for DPR-FiD to predict correct answers on the NQ dataset than for other models?
qa.answer: Because the labels in NQ are spans identified from Wikipedia passages, and DPR-FiD is specifically designed to retrieve relevant passages from Wikipedia.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that DPR-FiD is able to correctly predict the answer to all three questions, while GENREAD and GPT are only able to correctly predict the answer to one question each. This suggests that DPR-FiD is better at retrieving relevant information from Wikipedia than the other models.
qa.student: Because the labels in NQ are spans identified from Wikipedia passages, and DPR-FiD is specifically designed to retrieve relevant passages from Wikipedia.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: Which model performs better on WebQ when using InstructGPT as a context generator?
qa.answer: DPR-FiD

qa.student: TriviaQA performs better than WebQ when using InstructGPT as a context generator.
qa.feedback: You mixed up the datasets. The question is specifically about WebQ, which is shown by the blue line with circle markers. For every GPT‑3 model size on the x‑axis, the WebQ EM score is below the TriviaQA score at the same size. Since higher EM scores on the y‑axis mean better performance, TriviaQA performs better than WebQ, not the other way around.

qa.question: Which dataset required the highest total training steps for GENREAD (FiD-l)?

qa.feedback: You correctly identified WoW and its 20,000 training steps, but you left out that FM2 also used 20,000 total training steps for GENREAD (FiD-l). Because the question asks which dataset required the highest total training steps, you need to mention all datasets that share this maximum value, not just one of them.

qa.question: Which open-source large language model achieved the highest EM score on TriviaQA?
qa.answer: Codex
qa.explanation: The table shows the EM scores for different open-source large language models on TriviaQA and WebQ. Codex achieved the highest EM score of 72.6 on TriviaQA.
qa.student: Codex achieved the highest EM score on TriviaQA among the open-source large language models.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up “open-source” with “all models in the table.” Codex (and InstructGPT) are not open-source models; only OPT is. The question asks for the best EM score among open-source large language models, so you must compare only the open-source ones. From the table, OPT is the only open-source LLM listed, so it is the one with the highest EM score among open-source models, even though Codex has a higher overall score.

qa.question: Which model performs best in terms of accuracy for activity classification?
qa.answer: CMAP

qa.student: The best model for activity classification accuracy is SAP – AP 4, since it has the highest MCC and strong accuracy, so it should be considered the top performer overall.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up “best overall” with “best accuracy.” The question asks specifically which model has the highest accuracy value for activity classification, not which has the highest MCC or a balance of metrics. Looking only at the Activity Acc column, CMAP has the highest accuracy (91.4%), slightly better than AMAP (91.1%) and clearly higher than all SAP variants. qa.question: What is the difference between the CMAP and AMAP models?
qa.answer: The CMAP model concatenates the features extracted from each access point, while the AMAP model aggregates them.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the CMAP model has a single "Aggregation" block after the feature extractors, while the AMAP model has two "Aggregation" blocks, one after the activity classifiers and one after the orientation classifiers.
qa.student: CMAP concatenates the activity and orientation outputs from each AP, while AMAP concatenates the feature vectors from each AP before sending them to a single shared classifier.
qa.feedback: You have swapped what is being concatenated and what is being aggregated in each model. In the CMAP model, the feature extractors from multiple APs feed their feature vectors into a concatenation block, and then one shared activity and one shared orientation classifier operate on this combined feature vector. In the AMAP model, each AP’s features go through its own activity and orientation classifiers first, and then the resulting prediction outputs (not the features) are aggregated to form the final activity and orientation decisions.

2210.07670v1-Figure3-1.png.caption: Comparison of MVPS reconstructions on DiLiGenT-MV benchmark [29]. We report F-score metric results for numerical comparison. We can observe that our method recovers fine details and provides high-quality reconstructions of challenging objects.

2210.07670v1-Figure1-1.png.caption: (a) The classical MVPS setup as outlined in Hernández et al. [16] work. (b) The advantage of our method over current state-ofthe-art deep-MVPS method i.e., UA-MVPS [23]. It can be observed that our method is able to correctly recover the fine object’s details for anisotropic and glossy material object. The 3D model used for the above illustration is taken from [33] dataset.

2210.07670v1-Figure4-1.png.caption: (a) Chamfer L2 comparison of our method with UA-MVPS [23] on our synthetic dataset (lower is better). (b) We show depth and surface normal predictions on texture-less object. Pixels marked with yellow color indicate confident MVS or PS predictions (cmvs i and cps i ). Note that MVS cannot predict depth reliably on texture-less surface, which leads to inferior results in UA-MVPS [23]. On the other hand, our uncertainty-aware volume rendering approach can recover missing surface information, and therefore, provides better reconstructions. (c) Comparison of our method with TSDF Fusion algorithm [7]. We report F-score metric for numerical comparison.

2210.07670v1-Figure5-1.png.caption: Surface profile of our reconstructions on a randomly chosen path. Clearly, our surface profile overlaps with the groundtruth(GT), which indicates the high quality of our reconstructions.

2210.07670v1-Table3-1.png.caption: Contribution of MVS, PS, rendering loss terms and uncertainty modeling to our reconstruction quality. We report Chamfer L2 metric for comparison (lower is better). Clearly, our proposed loss in Eq:(8) produces best results on average.

2210.07670v1-Figure2-1.png.caption: Method overview (Left to Right): We obtain highly confident 3D position and surface normal predictions of the object via uncertainty-aware deep-MVS and deep-PS networks, respectively. Then, we learn the signed distance function representation of the object surface. Finally, our optimization uses the volume rendering technique to recover the missing details of the surface, providing high-quality 3D reconstructions of challenging material types.

qa.question: Which method produced the most accurate reconstruction of the Buddha statue?
qa.answer: The "Ours" method produced the most accurate reconstruction of the Buddha statue, as indicated by the F-score of 0.993.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the reconstructions of the Buddha statue produced by different methods, along with the ground-truth reconstruction. The F-score is a measure of accuracy, and the "Ours" method has the highest F-score for the Buddha statue.
qa.student: The B-MVPS method produced the most accurate reconstruction of the Buddha statue.
qa.feedback: You correctly focused on one of the compared methods, but you did not consider all of them. The figure shows several reconstructions with F-scores under each: R-MVPS, B-MVPS, NR-MVPS, UA-MVPS, and Ours. To find the most accurate, you must identify which has the highest F-score for the Buddha statue; that is “Ours,” not B-MVPS. You are omitting the comparison with the “Ours” column, which leads you to the wrong choice.

qa.question: Which method category performs the best on average across all datasets?
qa.answer: View-Synthesis
qa.explanation: The average F-score for View-Synthesis methods is 0.784, which is higher than the average F-scores for the other method categories.
qa.student: The photometric stereo methods perform the best on average, because they specifically estimate surface normals which should directly translate into higher F-scores across all datasets.
qa.feedback: You are assuming that the algorithm type that seems most suitable conceptually (photometric stereo) must also achieve the best empirical performance. However, the table shows averaged F-scores for each method, and higher values mean better performance. The “Ours” method (a separate category) has the highest average F-score (0.982), followed by view-synthesis methods (e.g., VolSDF with 0.873), while photometric stereo averages are lower (up to 0.461). You need to base your conclusion on these reported averages, not on what you expect from the method’s underlying idea.

qa.question: Which method performs best on the COW dataset?
qa.answer: Ours.
qa.explanation: The table shows that our method has the highest F-score of 0.987 on the COW dataset.
qa.student: On the COW dataset, the B-MVPS method performs best because it has the highest F-score of 0.989.
qa.feedback: You correctly read that B-MVPS has a high F‑score on the COW dataset (0.989), but it is not the highest. In this table, higher F‑scores mean better performance. The “Ours” method has an F‑score of 0.987 on COW, which is slightly lower than 0.989. So B‑MVPS, not “Ours,” actually performs best on COW. Double-check which number is largest in that row when identifying the best method.

qa.question: What are the advantages of the proposed method over the UA-MVPS method?
qa.answer: The proposed method is able to correctly recover the fine object’s details for anisotropic and glossy material object.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the results of the proposed method and the UA-MVPS method on two different materials: anisotropic and glossy. The proposed method is able to correctly recover the fine details of the object, while the UA-MVPS method produces a blurry and inaccurate reconstruction.
qa.student: The proposed method produces more accurate 3D reconstructions than UA-MVPS, giving a cleaner final shape.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that the proposed method gives better reconstructions, but your answer is too general and misses what kind of improvement is shown. From the figure and caption, you should state that the main advantage is recovering fine object details specifically for challenging surfaces: anisotropic material and glossy material. UA-MVPS fails to capture these fine details on such materials, while the proposed method reconstructs them more faithfully.

qa.question: How does the performance of the proposed method compare to that of UA-MVPS on texture-less objects?
qa.answer: The proposed method performs better than UA-MVPS on texture-less objects.
qa.explanation: This can be seen in Figure (b), which shows the depth and surface normal predictions on a texture-less object. The MVS network in UA-MVPS is unable to predict depth reliably on texture-less surfaces, which leads to inferior reconstructions. In contrast, the proposed method is able to recover missing surface information and provide better reconstructions.
qa.student: The proposed method performs similarly to UA-MVPS on texture-less objects, since both methods struggle with the lack of texture. The figure mainly shows that texture-less regions are hard for any algorithm, and the uncertainties only help visualize this difficulty rather than significantly improving reconstruction quality.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that texture-less regions are challenging, but you misunderstand how much the proposed method improves over UA-MVPS. The caption and visuals state that MVS (and thus UA-MVPS) cannot predict depth reliably on texture-less surfaces, leading to inferior results, while the uncertainty-aware volume rendering “can recover missing surface information” and “provides better reconstructions.” The images of the texture-less object clearly show UA-MVPS producing incomplete/incorrect geometry, whereas the proposed method yields a more complete and accurate shape. So the key point is not that both methods perform similarly badly; rather, the proposed method substantially outperforms UA-MVPS on texture-less objects by leveraging uncertainty to fill in missing information.

This indicates that the reconstruction of the Buddha is more accurate.
qa.student: The reconstruction of the Buddha is more accurate than the reconstruction of the READING figure.

qa.answer: Ours

For BEAR, the lowest Chamfer L2 value (best performance, since lower is better) is 0.154, which corresponds to the “w/o Rendering Loss” setting, not “Ours” (which has 0.213).
