For MRPC, you need to compare the numerical scores across the layers (#1–#12) in the MRPC row: higher values mean better transfer. The highest score is 89.0±1.1 at layer #5 (shaded), not at layer #1. So, the middle layer #5 has the highest transferability for MRPC.

qa.question: Which modules of BERT have the most consistent weight distributions?
qa.answer: The Feed-Forward and Multi-Head modules have the most consistent weight distributions.
qa.explanation: The violin plots for these modules are narrower and have less variance than the plots for the LayerNorms modules. This indicates that the weights in these modules are more tightly clustered around the mean.
qa.student: The LayerNorm modules have the most consistent weight distributions, since their violin plots are the narrowest and most peaked for both RTE and STS-B, especially compared with the Feed-Forward and Multi-Head modules.
qa.feedback: You are misreading which violins are narrow and concentrated. In these plots, more consistent weights correspond to distributions that are tightly clustered (narrow horizontally) around a central value. The Feed-Forward and Multi-Head modules actually show the narrowest, most compact violins, indicating their weights are the most consistent. The LayerNorm-related modules (LayerNorms, LayerNorms_A, LayerNorms_F) have broader, often bimodal violins, meaning their weights vary more and are therefore less consistent.

qa.question: Which tuning method consistently outperforms the other across all tasks and sample sizes?
qa.answer: Outlier tuning consistently outperforms random selection across all tasks and sample sizes.
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of fine-tuned BERT with n trainable parameters in every LayerNorm module on four different target tasks. For each task and sample size, the performance of outlier tuning is compared to that of random selection. In all cases, outlier tuning either outperforms or matches the performance of random selection.
qa.student: The outlier tuning method performs better, since for each value of n it has higher scores than random tuning.
qa.feedback: You correctly saw that outlier tuning generally gives higher scores than random tuning, but you overgeneralized without checking all cells. To say one method “consistently” outperforms the other, you need to verify there are no exceptions. In the RTE task at n = 64, random tuning is slightly higher than outlier tuning, so outlier tuning is not strictly better in every case, only in most of them.

2302.00993v2-Figure5-1.png.caption: Compact versions of two 2-domain graphs. In both graphs, both domains have two latent parents. In setup (a) there is a shared latent parent while in setup (b) there is not.

2302.00993v2-Figure3-1.png.caption: Results. Logarithmic scale on the x-axis. Error bars in (a) are one standard deviation of the mean and in (b) and (c) they are the interquartile range.

2302.00993v2-Figure1-1.png.caption: Setup. A latent causal representation where multiple domains Xe provide different “views” on subsets of the latent variables Zi. The domains may correspond to different data modalities such as images, text or numerical data. Crucially, the observations across domains are unpaired, i.e., they arise from different states of the latent causal model.

2302.00993v2-Figure8-1.png.caption: Simulation results where assumptions are not satisfied. Logarithmic scale on the x-axis. Error bars in (a) are one standard deviation of the mean and in (b) and (c) they are the interquartile range.

2302.00993v2-Figure6-1.png.caption: Histograms showing the frequency of 25000 values sampled from the random variables εi with distribution as specified in Appendix E. Each distribution has mean zero and variance one. The first row shows the empirical distributions from ε1 to ε5 and the second row from ε6 to ε9.

2302.00993v2-Figure2-1.png.caption: Compact version of a 2-domain graph G2 with five latent nodes and two domains V1 and V2. All observed nodes in each domain are represented by a single grey node. We draw a dashed blue edge from latent node h ∈ H to domain Ve ⊆ V if h ∈ Se = pa(Ve). The random vectors associated to the two domains are uncoupled, that is, we do not observe their joint distribution.

qa.question: What is the difference between the two graphs shown in the figure?
qa.answer: The two graphs differ in whether they have a shared latent parent. In graph (a), there is a shared latent parent, while in graph (b), there is not.
qa.explanation: The figure shows two graphs, each with two domains. The domains are represented by the gray circles labeled V1 and V2. The latent parents are represented by the white circles labeled 1, 2, 3, and 4. In graph (a), the latent parents 1 and 2 are shared by both domains, while in graph (b), each domain has its own set of latent parents.
qa.student: In both graphs, each observed variable V1 and V2 just has two independent latent parents, so the main difference is only in how the nodes are drawn. The structure of dependencies between V1 and V2 is essentially the same in (a) and (b).
qa.feedback: You correctly noticed that V1 and V2 each have two latent parents, but you are missing the key structural difference. In graph (a), V1 and V2 share a latent parent (node 2), so there is a common hidden cause that links the two domains. In graph (b), V1 and V2 have completely separate latent parents (1–2 for V1, 3–4 for V2), so there is no shared hidden cause between them. This changes the dependency structure: (a) allows cross-domain correlation via the shared parent, while (b) does not.

qa.question: What does the figure represent?

qa.explanation: The figure shows three domains, X1, X2, and X3, which correspond to different data modalities such as images, text, or numerical data. Each domain provides a different "view" on a subset of the latent variables Z1-Z7.

The figure shows different domains (X¹ images, X² tables, X³ text) each observing different subsets of the latent variables Z, but not as matched tuples from the same underlying sample.

From the plot, you should note that as sample size increases, the median score for the “Same errors” condition first increases from the smallest to medium sample sizes, then slightly decreases at the largest sample size. You need to describe this non‑monotonic trend instead of just saying it stays the same.

qa.question: What is the difference between the latent nodes and the observed nodes in the figure?
qa.answer: The latent nodes are the nodes that are not directly observed, while the observed nodes are the nodes that are directly observed. In the figure, the latent nodes are represented by the circles with numbers, while the observed nodes are represented by the circles with the letter "v" and a superscript.
qa.explanation: The figure shows a 2-domain graph, with 5 latent nodes and 2 observed domains. The latent nodes are connected to each other and to the observed nodes. The observed nodes are not connected to each other. This indicates that the latent nodes are not directly observed, but they can be inferred from the observed nodes.
qa.student: The latent nodes are the gray circles and represent real measured variables in each domain, while the observed nodes are the white circles and represent hidden or unmeasured variables that are inferred from the data.
qa.feedback: You have the roles reversed. In this graph, the white numbered circles are the latent nodes: they are hidden variables that are not directly observed and sit between the two observed domains. The gray nodes with superscripts (v₁¹,…,v₄¹ and v₁²,…,v₅²) are the observed nodes in the two domains; their superscripts indicate which domain they belong to. Latent nodes explain the relationships among the observed variables, while the observed nodes correspond to actual measured features in each domain.

qa.question: Which of the random variables has the largest variance?

Although ε5 appears to extend further on the x-axis, all random variables are constructed to have the same variance; visual differences are due to their different distribution shapes, not different variances.

qa.question: What is the difference between the conventional EDA and the proposed non-autoregressive attractor extraction?
qa.answer: The conventional EDA uses an LSTM autoregressive process to extract attractors, while the proposed non-autoregressive attractor extraction uses an attention mechanism.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the conventional EDA uses an LSTM encoder and decoder to extract attractors, while the proposed non-autoregressive attractor extraction uses an attention mechanism that takes as input the attractor queries, the embeddings, and the speaker labels.
qa.student: The main difference is that the proposed non-autoregressive attractor extraction uses a larger sigmoid layer instead of the LSTM encoder-decoder used in the conventional EDA.
qa.feedback: You misidentified where the change occurs. The sigmoid part that outputs speaker labels is the same in both systems. The factual difference shown in the figure is how attractors A are obtained: conventional EDA uses an autoregressive LSTM encoder–decoder to generate A, while the proposed method replaces this with a non-autoregressive attention module Attn(Q, E_L, E_L) driven by attractor queries Q.

In the Tp column, the highest value is 5.20, which corresponds to the “EEND-NA +SelfCond” model, not the InterLoss variant. Always identify the maximum by checking the numeric entries, not by guessing which design should be faster.

qa.question: What is the role of the EDA block in the EEND-EDA model?
qa.answer: The EDA block is responsible for estimating the data distribution and generating new samples.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the EDA block is used in each layer of the EEND-EDA model. The EDA block takes the input from the previous layer and outputs a new sample. This suggests that the EDA block is responsible for estimating the data distribution and generating new samples.
qa.student: The EDA block in the EEND-EDA model generates the frame-wise output labels Y directly from each encoder layer’s representation, which are then used to compute the PIT loss at every layer.
qa.feedback: You are correct that the EDA block is used at each encoder layer, but you misread how it connects to the outputs. From the diagram, the EDA block produces attractors A₁, A₂, A₃, A₄ from the encoder outputs E₁–E₄. These attractors are then multiplied with the encoder representations (AᵀE) and passed through a sigmoid to give the frame-wise speaker activity predictions Y₁–Y₄, which are used in the PIT loss. So the EDA block itself does not output Y; it outputs the attractors that condition the prediction of Y at each layer.

qa.question: What is the main difference between the local upsampling method and the proposed PGCU method?
qa.answer: The local upsampling method only utilizes the local information of LRMS for upsampling, while the proposed PGCU method can fully exploit the rich global information of LRMS and the cross-modal global information of PAN.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the local upsampling method only considers the information within a small receptive field around each pixel, while the PGCU method considers the information from the entire image. This allows the PGCU method to produce more accurate and detailed results.
qa.student: The main difference is that the local upsampling method only uses a small local patch from the LRMS image to compute weights for upsampling, while the PGCU method uses information from the whole image, giving it a global receptive field.
qa.feedback: You correctly noted that the local method relies on a limited local receptive field and that PGCU has a global receptive field. However, you omitted that PGCU is specifically a probability-based cross-modal method: it uses PAN guidance to derive pixel distribution probabilities and also uses global distribution values from LRMS. This cross-modal use of both PAN and LRMS global information is the key difference beyond just “local vs. global” context.

Higher PSNR means better performance, and among all listed values in the WorldView3 PSNR column, SFIIN* has the highest PSNR (30.399), not GPPNN (29.950). qa.question: How does the PGCU module extract information from the input images?
qa.answer: The PGCU module extracts information from the input images by using three different branches. The first branch extracts value information (G) from the LRMS image, the second branch extracts pixel information (F) from the PAN image, and the third branch extracts channel distribution information (V) from the MS image.
qa.explanation: This can be seen in Figure (c), which shows the overall flow of the PGCU module.
qa.student: The PGCU module takes the LRMS and PAN images, runs them through a single D2S block to get a combined feature map, and then directly upsamples it with a convolution layer to produce the high‑resolution MS image.
qa.feedback: You correctly mention that LRMS and PAN go into the PGCU pipeline and that a convolution is used before producing the upsampled MS image, but you misread how information is actually extracted. The diagram shows two separate D2S blocks (one per input) inside the EA part, not a single combined block. Their outputs are then split into two kinds of information: value information (G) and pixel information (F). These are further processed in the DEE block via channel projection, and only after that does the FA block with a convolution generate the upsampled MS. So information is not extracted by one fused D2S block and direct upsampling; it is extracted via separate D2S feature extraction, decomposition into value and pixel information, and channel projections before the final convolution.

qa.question: Which dataset has the most training samples?
qa.answer: GaoFen2
qa.explanation: The table shows that the GaoFen2 dataset has 27720 training samples, which is more than the other two datasets.
qa.student: The GaoFen2 dataset has the most training samples.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified GaoFen2 as having the most training samples, but you did not explain how you know. You should point out that the Train/Test row shows 2720/208 for GaoFen2, and 768/80 and 2160/208 for WordView2 and WordView3, respectively, so 2720 is the largest training number. Including this comparison makes your reasoning clear.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the PGCU module?
qa.answer: The PGCU module is used to generate a high-resolution multispectral image from a low-resolution multispectral image and a high-resolution panchromatic image.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the detailed structure of the PGCU module. The module consists of three main parts: information extraction, distribution and expectation estimation, and fine adjustment. The information extraction part extracts features from the low-resolution multispectral image and the high-resolution panchromatic image. The distribution and expectation estimation part estimates the distribution and expectation of the high-resolution multispectral image. The fine adjustment part uses the estimated distribution and expectation to generate the final high-resolution multispectral image.
qa.student: The PGCU module is designed to extract low-level spatial features from the LRMS and PAN images using convolution and pooling, so that more detailed textures can be captured before fusion.
qa.feedback: You are focusing only on the front “information extraction” part of the figure and treating PGCU like a basic feature extractor. However, the PGCU’s main purpose is not just to get low‑level textures, but to model and use a probabilistic distribution over channel features (F, G, V) and then compute their expectation to guide pansharpening. The middle “Distribution and Expectation Estimation” block shows that PGCU projects features into distributions, derives distribution values and probabilities, and then uses an expectation operation before the final adjustment. You should emphasize that PGCU estimates and exploits feature distributions (and their expectations) to refine the fused image, rather than simply extracting features.

qa.question: How does the general AdaTT model differ from the AdaTT-sp model?
qa.answer: The general AdaTT model includes an optional shared fusion unit, which is not present in the AdaTT-sp model.

So the main difference is that general AdaTT is a deeper version of AdaTT-sp.
qa.feedback: You correctly notice that both models have task‑specific parts for A and B, but you misunderstand what is new in general AdaTT. The key difference is not simply “more depth” or more repetitions of the same units. General AdaTT introduces optional shared modules (shown in blue and with dotted outlines) that can be used by both tasks. These include shared experts and a shared fusion unit, allowing the model to learn representations that are common across tasks. When these shared modules are removed, general AdaTT reduces to AdaTT-sp, which has only task‑specific fusion units and no shared components.

The plot shows how the different data points are related to each other in a two-dimensional space. Data points that are close together in the plot are similar to each other, while data points that are far apart are dissimilar.

In the figure, many crosses and dots of the same colors occupy overlapping regions rather than forming completely separate clusters. This indicates that text and speech from the same dataset are mapped to similar areas by the shared encoder, not to distinct, non-overlapping regions.

On the left, text (crosses) and speech (dots) embeddings form more separated clusters, indicating modality-specific representations. qa.question: What is the role of the shared encoder in the Maestro architecture?
qa.answer: The shared encoder takes the speech encoder's output and the text encoder's output and combines them to create a representation of the input data that is shared by both the ASR decoder and the auxiliary phone decoder.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the shared encoder is connected to both the speech encoder and the text encoder, and its output is fed to both the ASR decoder and the auxiliary phone decoder. This suggests that the shared encoder is responsible for combining the information from both the speech and text modalities to create a single representation that can be used by both decoders.
qa.student: The shared encoder just forwards the representations from the speech encoder directly to the ASR decoder, acting like a pass-through layer that doesn’t really interact with the text side. Its main role is to prepare features only for speech recognition, while the auxiliary phone decoder and text encoder work independently.
qa.feedback: You are right that the shared encoder feeds representations to the ASR decoder, but you are missing its core function. The shared encoder is not just a pass-through speech-only layer. It is where information from the speech encoder and the text-side module (upsampler/duration/text encoder) is brought into a common representation that both the ASR decoder and the auxiliary phone decoder use. The consistency loss is applied on its outputs to align speech and text representations. So its role is to learn a shared, modality-consistent encoding, not to operate independently of the text side.

qa.question: What is the role of the non-label party in the vertical federated learning framework?
qa.answer: The non-label party, in this case, the online publisher, provides the collected user feedback data, such as browsing behavior and clicks, to collaboratively train the conversion rate estimation model.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the non-label party (blue box) provides the input features (xN) and the hidden representations (hN) to the label party (orange box). These are used to train the model, which then predicts the conversion rate.
qa.student: The non-label party is responsible for providing the labels (like conversions or purchases) and making the final prediction in the model.
qa.feedback: You are confusing the roles of the two parties. In the figure, the non-label party (online publisher) does not have labels or make final predictions. Its role is to take its own user features x_N and transform them via f_N into an intermediate representation h_N, which is then sent forward to the label party. The label party (advertising platform) combines h_N with its own features x_L, uses the labels y to compute the loss, and sends gradients back so the non-label party can update f_N. So the non-label party contributes feature embeddings, not labels or final predictions.

qa.question: What is the difference between the "Projection" and "Mixup and Projection" operations shown in the figure?
qa.answer: The "Projection" operation projects the mixed gradient onto the unit sphere, while the "Mixup and Projection" operation first mixes the gradient with a random vector and then projects the result onto the unit sphere.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the "Projection" operation simply projects the mixed gradient (g_mixed,i) onto the unit sphere, resulting in the projected gradient (g_projected,i). In contrast, the "Mixup and Projection" operation first mixes the gradient with a random vector (g_r) and then projects the resulting vector onto the unit sphere. This additional mixing step helps to prevent the model from overfitting to the training data.
qa.student: In the “Projection” operation, the mixed gradient \(g_{mixed,i}\) is directly projected onto the target direction \(\bar{g}\), giving \(g_{projected,i}\). In the “Mixup and Projection” operation, the original gradient \(g_i\) is first projected to be closer to the reference gradient \(g_r\), and then this result is used as the final gradient without any projection onto \(\bar{g}\).
qa.feedback: You are correctly distinguishing that both operations involve modifying gradients, but you have the directions and sequence wrong. In the Projection case (top), the already mixed gradient \(g_{mixed,i}\) is checked against the goal angle with \(\bar{g}\); if it does not meet the goal, it is projected onto a feasible cone around \(\bar{g}\), producing \(g_{projected,i}\). In the Mixup and Projection case (bottom), the original gradient \(g_i\) is first mixed with a reference gradient \(g_r\) to form \(g_{mixed,i}\) (red). This mixed gradient is then also projected relative to \(\bar{g}\) (blue cone) to get \(g_{projected,i}\). The figure does not show any projection of \(g_i\) toward \(g_r\), and in both operations the final step is a projection with respect to \(\bar{g}\), not skipping projection in the second case.

qa.feedback: You are naming entities shown in the diagram rather than the main procedural components the question asks about. The figure visually groups the process into three labeled stages: “Ranking System” on the left, “Publisher Page” in the middle, and “Ad Landing Page” on the right. These three labeled blocks are the main components of the ad delivery procedure, not the individual actors like user, publisher, or advertising platform.

qa.question: Which defense approach provides the best trade-off between privacy and utility?
qa.answer: Marvell.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that Marvell achieves the highest reduction in LeakAUC (24.7%) while maintaining a similar level of utility (AUC) compared to other approaches.
qa.student: MixPro (Ours) provides the best trade-off between privacy and utility.
qa.feedback: You only named a method without explaining the trade-off using both privacy and utility values. To show a trade-off, you need to mention that Marvell has the best privacy improvement (largest drop in Leak AUC: -24.7%) but suffers the biggest utility loss (AUC 0.601 vs 0.620 with no defense), while MixPro and DP keep utility almost unchanged (0.602) but offer smaller privacy gains (-11.3% and -5.7%). Your answer omits this comparison, so it doesn’t justify why one method is the “best” trade-off.

qa.question: How does the Diffu-AT framework exploit unaligned samples in vFL training?
qa.answer: The Diffu-AT framework exploits unaligned samples in vFL training by learning a conditional diffusion model for generating federated embeddings of the label party's unaligned samples. This is done by first training a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) on the label party's aligned samples. Then, the DDPM is used to generate federated embeddings of the unaligned samples. These embeddings are then used to train the vFL model.
qa.explanation: The figure shows the Diffu-AT framework, which consists of two parts: (a) a conditional diffusion model for generating federated embeddings of unaligned samples, and (b) the vFL training process. In (a), the DDPM is trained on the label party's aligned samples. Then, the DDPM is used to generate federated embeddings of the unaligned samples. In (b), the vFL model is trained using the federated embeddings of both the aligned and unaligned samples.
qa.student: Diffu-AT uses the unaligned samples by directly training them with the same supervised loss as the aligned samples, treating them as if their features from both parties were already matched.
qa.feedback: You are assuming unaligned samples are treated like aligned ones, but the figure shows a different mechanism. In vFL, unaligned samples do not have matching features from both parties, so Diffu-AT first generates federated embeddings for the label party’s unaligned samples using the conditional diffusion model (panel a). Then, in panel b, these generated embeddings from the non‑label party are combined with the label party’s real features in the lower branch and used with an “unaligned” loss in an alternative training scheme (the orange dashed cycle). The key idea is not to pretend they are aligned, but to synthesize the missing side’s representation and alternate training between aligned and unaligned branches.

2305.10727v1-Figure5-1.png.caption: GPUSQ-ViT scheme with two sub-workflows. For the 2:4 structured sparse pruning workflow, the dense floating-point model MDF is compressed as the sparse floating-point model MSF . Hard label, soft logits and feature-based distillation losses are accumulated as the overall sparse pruning loss. The sparse floating-point model MSF is quantized as the sparse quantized model MSQ for the sparsedistillation-aware QAT workflow. Hard label and soft logits calibration losses are obtained in a similar manner. Each feature maps calibration result is multiplied with a weight factor to indicate this layer’s probability of having a real influence on MSQ model’s final accuracy. Three calibration losses are accumulated as the overall quantization calibration loss.

Dense matrix A of size M ×K in left side becomes M × K 2 in right side after compressing with 2:4 fine-grained structured sparse pattern. Sparse Tensor Core automatically picks only the elements from B according to the nonzero elements in A. Comparing the dense and sparse GEMMs, B and C are the same dense K ×N and M ×N matrices, respectively. By skipping the unnecessary multiplications of redundant zeros, sparse GEMM accelerate the dense GEMM with 2×.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the weight factors in the sparse floating-point model?
qa.answer: The weight factors indicate the probability that a layer has a real influence on the final accuracy of the sparse quantized model.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the weight factors are used to multiply the feature maps calibration results. This suggests that the weight factors are used to determine how important each layer is to the final accuracy of the model.
qa.student: The weight factors in the sparse floating-point model are used to equally scale all of the feature-map calibration losses so that every layer contributes the same amount to the overall quantization calibration loss.
qa.feedback: You are correct that the weight factors act on the feature-map calibration results for each layer, but they do not make all layers contribute equally. The figure and caption state that each feature map’s calibration result is multiplied by a weight factor “to indicate this layer’s probability of having a real influence on MSQ model’s final accuracy.” That means the weights differentiate layers by importance: layers judged more influential on the final accuracy are given larger weights, and less influential layers are given smaller weights.

qa.question: How does the sparse GEMM operation on the Tensor Core differ from the dense GEMM operation?
qa.answer: The sparse GEMM operation only multiplies the non-zero elements of matrix A with the corresponding elements of matrix B, while the dense GEMM operation multiplies all elements of matrix A with the corresponding elements of matrix B.
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the sparse GEMM operation uses a "Select" operation to choose only the non-zero elements of matrix A, while the dense GEMM operation does not. This allows the sparse GEMM operation to skip unnecessary multiplications, which can improve performance.
qa.student: In dense GEMM, the Tensor Core multiplies all elements of matrix A (M×K) with matrix B (K×N). In sparse GEMM, matrix A is compressed using a 2:4 sparsity pattern so only part of A is multiplied with B. This skips many multiplications and makes the sparse GEMM about 2× faster than the dense GEMM.
qa.feedback: You correctly noted that A is compressed with a 2:4 sparsity pattern and that skipping zero multiplications makes sparse GEMM faster, but you left out key structural details. In the sparse case, A is stored as nonzero values plus 2‑bit indices, and its K dimension is effectively halved (M×K becomes M×K/2). You also did not mention that B and C remain dense and unchanged between dense and sparse GEMM; only A’s representation and how the Tensor Core selects matching elements of B differ. Including these details clarifies exactly what changes between dense and sparse operations.

qa.question: Which model seems to be focusing on the most relevant parts of the image for each example?
qa.answer: The Swin Transformer baseline dense models seem to be focusing on the most relevant parts of the image for each example.
qa.explanation: The CAM visualizations for the Swin Transformer baseline dense models show that the models are focusing on the horse, the cat and dog, and the car, which are the main subjects of the images. The GPUSQ-ViT compressed INT8 and INT4 models, on the other hand, seem to be focusing on less relevant parts of the images, such as the background or the edges of the objects.
qa.student: The Swin Transformer Tiny and Swin Transformer Base baselines are always the best because they are full-precision models, so they consistently focus most on the relevant objects. The INT8 and INT4 compressed versions lose too much information, so their attention maps are always worse and more spread out than the baselines across all three examples.
qa.feedback: You are assuming that higher-precision baseline models must always attend better, but the CAMs show that this is not consistently true. For the horse and car examples, the INT8 and even INT4 models concentrate strongly on the main object, often as well as or more tightly than the baselines. In the cat–dog image, some compressed models actually highlight both animals more clearly than the corresponding baseline. Your misconception is treating compression level as a direct indicator of “worse” attention, instead of comparing where the red/yellow regions actually fall relative to the important objects in each specific example.

qa.question: How does the performance of GPUSQ-ViT compare to other state-of-the-art compression methods on the classification task?
qa.answer: GPUSQ-ViT achieves similar or slightly better accuracy than other state-of-the-art compression methods, while requiring significantly fewer parameters and FLOPs.
qa.explanation: The table shows the accuracy, model size, and FLOPs for GPUSQ-ViT and other state-of-the-art compression methods. We can see that GPUSQ-ViT consistently achieves similar or slightly better accuracy than other methods, while having a smaller model size and requiring fewer FLOPs. For example, GPUSQ-ViT (INT4) achieves 81.3% top-1 accuracy with only 1.73M parameters and 0.07G FLOPs, while PTQ4-ViT requires 21.64M parameters and 17.48G FLOPs to achieve 81.2% top-1 accuracy.
qa.student: GPUSQ-ViT achieves similar or slightly better accuracy than other state-of-the-art compression methods, while requiring significantly fewer parameters and FLOPs.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: Which model and method combination achieves the highest throughput on the NVIDIA A100 GPU with a batch size of 256?
qa.answer: The Swin-Base model with the GPUSQ-ViT method achieves the highest throughput on the NVIDIA A100 GPU with a batch size of 256.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the Swin-Base model with the GPUSQ-ViT method achieves a throughput of 1747 FPS (1.29x) on the NVIDIA A100 GPU with a batch size of 256. This is the highest throughput achieved by any model and method combination in the table.
qa.student: The Swin-Base model with GPUSQ-ViT at INT4 achieves the highest throughput on the A100 with a batch size of 256, reaching 49,993 FPS.
qa.feedback: You misread the throughput values in the A100 (BS=256) column. Higher numbers in this column mean higher FPS (throughput). The largest value is 70,860 FPS, which corresponds to the DeiT-Tiny model using GPUSQ-ViT with INT2 format, not the Swin-Base INT4 model. Always scan the entire column and compare all rows before deciding which is the maximum.

qa.question: How many bits are used to store the non-zero elements in the Sparse INT4 data format?
qa.answer: 4 bits
qa.explanation: The Sparse INT4 data format uses 4 bits to store each non-zero element. This can be seen in the figure, where the non-zero elements are labeled as "z", "y", "x", and "w", and each element is shown to occupy 4 bits.
qa.student: It uses 4 bits because it is the INT4 data format.
qa.feedback: You only mentioned the bit-width of a single INT4 value and not the total bits used to store all the non-zero elements. In the Sparse INT4 format, there are four non-zero elements (z, y, x, w), each stored with 4 bits. You need to multiply 4 bits by 4 elements to get the total number of bits used for the non-zero elements.

qa.question: How does the performance of the GPUSQ-ViT models compare to the baseline models in terms of accuracy?
qa.answer: The GPUSQ-ViT models generally perform slightly worse than the baseline models in terms of accuracy, as measured by both Mean IoU and Pixel Acc. However, the difference in accuracy is small, typically less than 0.5%.
qa.explanation: The table shows the Mean IoU and Pixel Acc for the GPUSQ-ViT models and the baseline models. The differences in accuracy are shown in parentheses and are typically less than 0.5%.
qa.student: The GPUSQ-ViT models have clearly worse accuracy than the baseline models. Because they are quantized (INT8/INT4), they sacrifice a large amount of Mean IoU and Pixel Accuracy in order to reduce parameters and FLOPs.
qa.feedback: You are overestimating the accuracy loss from quantization. The numbers in parentheses next to Mean IoU and Pixel Accuracy are small negative values (or even slightly positive in one case), indicating only minor differences from the FP32 baselines, not a “large amount” of degradation. The key idea is that GPUSQ-ViT greatly reduces parameters and FLOPs while keeping accuracy almost the same as the baselines, showing that quantization here preserves performance rather than severely harming it.

qa.question: What is the effect of enabling QAT on the Top-1 and Top-5 accuracy of GPUSQ-ViT (INT8) and GPUSQ-ViT (INT4) models?

For example, for the DeiT-Base (224^2) model with factor α = 10, factor β = 5, and factor γ = 5, the Top-1 accuracy is 82.9% when QAT is enabled and 82.4% when QAT is disabled. From the table, when QAT is enabled, both Top-1 and Top-5 accuracies increase for GPUSQ-ViT (INT8) and GPUSQ-ViT (INT4) in all listed settings, compared to their corresponding rows without QAT. To fully answer the question, you should explicitly state that for both bit-widths (INT8 and INT4), enabling QAT consistently raises Top-1 and Top-5 accuracy across the different model backbones.

qa.question: How does the Cascade Mask R-CNN model with Swin-Tiny backbone compare to the DETR model with ResNet50 backbone in terms of bbox mAP and FLOPs?
qa.answer: The Cascade Mask R-CNN model with Swin-Tiny backbone achieves a higher bbox mAP of 51.9 compared to 42.0 for the DETR model with ResNet50 backbone. However, the Cascade Mask R-CNN model also requires more FLOPs, 27.5 G compared to 2.8 G for the DETR model.
qa.explanation: The table shows the bbox mAP and FLOPs for different models and backbones. We can compare the values for the two models in question to answer the question.
qa.student: Cascade Mask R-CNN with Swin-Tiny is clearly better than DETR with ResNet50 because it has higher bbox mAP (around 47–48 vs about 43) while also using fewer FLOPs thanks to GPUSQ-ViT quantization.
qa.feedback: You are right that Cascade Mask R-CNN with Swin-Tiny achieves higher bbox mAP than DETR with ResNet50, but your reasoning about FLOPs is off. When you compare models, you must compare at the same precision/format. At FP32, Cascade Mask R-CNN (Swin-Tiny) uses 325 G FLOPs versus 86 G for DETR (ResNet50), so it is much more computationally expensive despite better mAP. Under INT8 with GPUSQ-ViT, Cascade Mask R-CNN still uses more FLOPs (about 122 G vs 43 G). Quantization reduces FLOPs for both models, but it doesn’t reverse which one is more costly.

qa.question: Which method resulted in the lowest perplexity for GPT-Neo 2.7B when quantized into 4 bits?
qa.answer: PEQA

qa.student: QAT.

The question asks “which method resulted in the lowest perplexity,” so you also need to mention that this is lower than both LoRA + OPTQ (12.09) and PEQA (11.38). By not comparing QAT to the other two methods, your answer leaves out an essential part of the requested information.

qa.question: Which quantization method resulted in the lowest perplexity for LLaMA 7B?
qa.answer: Both zero-points and quantization scales
qa.explanation: The table shows that the perplexity for LLaMA 7B is lowest when both zero-points and quantization scales are used.
qa.student: The method using both zero-points and quantization scales resulted in the lowest perplexity for LLaMA 7B.
qa.feedback: You are misunderstanding how to interpret the perplexity values: lower perplexity means better performance. For LLaMA 7B, the table shows 11.56 for “Zero-points only,” 5.84 for “Quantization scales only (PEQA),” and 5.86 for “Both zero-points and quantization scales.” Since 5.84 is the smallest value, the “Quantization scales only (PEQA)” method, not the combined method, achieved the lowest perplexity.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the decomposition (quantization) step in the PEQA scheme?
qa.answer: The decomposition (quantization) step is used to convert the full-precision weights of the model into sub-4-bit integers. This reduces the memory footprint of the model, which makes it more efficient to fine-tune and deploy.
qa.explanation: The figure shows how the full-precision weights are converted into sub-4-bit integers. The decomposition step is shown in the leftmost box of the figure. The full-precision weights are represented by the matrix W0. The decomposition step converts W0 into a matrix of sub-4-bit integers, which is represented by S0.
qa.student: The decomposition (quantization) step in the PEQA scheme converts the full-precision weights into low-bit integer weights and then fine-tunes both the resulting integer matrix and the quantization scale, so that the model can adapt to new tasks while using less memory.
qa.feedback: You are right that decomposition converts full‑precision weights into low‑bit (sub‑4‑bit) integers to reduce memory. However, the figure and caption state that in PEQA the integer matrix is frozen after quantization and only the quantization scale is updated during fine‑tuning. Saying that both the integer matrix and the scale are fine‑tuned contradicts the diagram, where only the red scale vectors change across tasks while the blue integer matrix remains fixed. The main purpose of the decomposition step is to separate the weights into a fixed integer matrix and a learnable scale so that only the scale needs to be updated.

qa.question: How does the performance of PEQA compare to LoRA on the Wikitext2 and PTB datasets?
qa.answer: PEQA performs better than LoRA on both the Wikitext2 and PTB datasets.
qa.explanation: The table shows the perplexity (PPL) of different methods on the Wikitext2 and PTB datasets. A lower PPL value indicates better performance. PEQA has a lower PPL than LoRA for all model sizes and bit widths on both datasets.
qa.student: PEQA generally performs better than LoRA because it has lower perplexity values on both Wikitext2 and PTB.
qa.feedback: You correctly note that PEQA tends to outperform LoRA, but your answer is too general and misses important details. You need to mention that the comparison is specifically at low precision (3‑bit and 4‑bit weights) and that PEQA is being compared against the quantized LoRA+OPTQ baselines, not the full‑precision LoRA rows. You also should point out that PEQA consistently achieves lower perplexity than LoRA+OPTQ across nearly all model sizes on both Wikitext2 and PTB, which is how we tell it performs better.

qa.question: Which model and quantization method combination achieved the highest average MMLU score?
qa.answer: LLaMA2 with PEQA quantization achieved the highest average MMLU score of 67.5.
qa.explanation: The table shows the MMLU scores for different model and quantization method combinations. The average score is shown in the last column. LLaMA2 with PEQA quantization has the highest average score of 67.5.
qa.student: The highest average MMLU score is achieved by the largest LLaMA2 model quantized with PEQA, because PEQA is a more accurate quantization method than RTN and therefore always improves performance over the full-precision model.
qa.feedback: You correctly identified that the best average score comes from the 70B LLaMA2 model with PEQA quantization (67.5). However, your explanation is conceptually off. PEQA does not “always” improve over full precision or “always” beat RTN; its effect depends on the specific model and size. For example, some smaller models with PEQA do not surpass their full-precision counterparts in every subject area, and RTN sometimes gives similar or better scores than PEQA for certain sizes. Be careful not to generalize from one best-performing configuration to a universal rule about the method.

qa.question: Which method performs better according to the perplexity (PPL) values in the table?
qa.answer: LoRA(QV4) performs better than PEQA(Ours) according to the PPL values in the table.
qa.explanation: The table shows the PPL values for two different methods, LoRA(QV4) and PEQA(Ours), on the Wikitext2 dataset. A lower PPL value indicates better performance. The PPL values for LoRA(QV4) are lower than the PPL values for PEQA(Ours) across all OPT model sizes, indicating that LoRA(QV4) performs better.
qa.student: PEQA (Ours) performs better because it uses 4-bit weights and generally has higher perplexity values across all OPT model sizes.
qa.feedback: You misinterpreted what the perplexity values mean. The caption states that a lower PPL value indicates better performance. When you look at the table, LoRA(QV4) has lower PPL than PEQA for every OPT model size (for example, 11.58 vs 12.40 on OPT 1.3B, 7.93 vs 8.11 on OPT 30B). Since higher PPL is worse, LoRA(QV4) actually performs better than PEQA in this comparison.

qa.question: Which quantization method is more efficient in terms of model size and perplexity trade-off for 3-bit quantization?
qa.answer: PEQA (Ours) is more efficient.
qa.explanation: The plot shows that for a model size of approximately 10 GB, the 3-bit PEQA model achieves a lower perplexity than the 3-bit LoRA+OPTQ model. This indicates that PEQA is able to achieve better performance with a smaller model size.
qa.student: PEQA is more efficient for 3-bit quantization.
qa.feedback: You only state that PEQA is more efficient without explaining the trade-off shown in the plot. To fully answer, you need to describe that for 3-bit quantization PEQA consistently achieves lower perplexity than LoRA+OPTQ at similar model sizes (i.e., better performance for the same or smaller size), which is why it is more efficient in terms of the model size–perplexity trade-off.

qa.question: How does the number of learnable parameters in LLaMA models change as the model size increases?
qa.answer: The number of learnable parameters in LLaMA models increases as the model size increases. This is shown in the table, where the number of learnable parameters for LLaMA models ranges from 2.10 million for the 7B model to 10.49 million for the 65B model.
qa.explanation: The table shows the number of learnable parameters for different LLaMA models. The number of learnable parameters is a measure of the complexity of a model, and generally, larger models have more learnable parameters.
qa.student: The number of learnable parameters in LLaMA stays roughly constant as the model size increases, because PEQA compresses the models so that parameter count does not scale with model size.
qa.feedback: You are confusing compression with how parameter counts scale. PEQA reduces the absolute number of learnable parameters, but within the LLaMA family those parameters still increase as model size grows. Looking at the PEQA row for LLaMA, the learnable parameters go from 1.36M (7B) to 2.13M (13B), 4.15M (30B), and 6.80M (65B). So, even under the same PEQA setting, larger LLaMA models require more learnable parameters; they do not stay roughly constant.

qa.question: Which model performs better in terms of perplexity when W Bits is set to 3?
qa.answer: LLaMA 13B performs better in terms of perplexity when W Bits is set to 3.
qa.explanation: The table shows that the perplexity for LLaMA 13B is lower than the perplexity for LLaMA 7B when W Bits is set to 3. This is true for all group sizes (g256, g128, and g64).
qa.student: When W Bits is set to 3, the LLaMA 7B model performs better in terms of perplexity, because its values (around 5.9–6.2) are lower than those of LLaMA 13B (around 5.3–5.5).
qa.feedback: You have the correct models and settings, but you misinterpreted which perplexity values are better. For perplexity, lower values mean better performance. At W Bits = 3, LLaMA 13B has perplexities of 5.54 (Channel-Wise), 5.40 (g256), 5.37 (g128), and 5.34 (g64), all of which are lower (better) than LLaMA 7B’s corresponding values of 6.19, 5.96, 5.91, and 5.89. Therefore, LLaMA 13B performs better than LLaMA 7B when W Bits is 3.

qa.question: How does the number of trainable parameters in the LLaMA-65B model with LoRA compare to the number of trainable parameters in the LLaMA-65B model with 4-bit PEQA?
qa.answer: The LLaMA-65B model with LoRA has 10.49M trainable parameters, while the LLaMA-65B model with 4-bit PEQA has 6.8M trainable parameters. This means that the LLaMA-65B model with LoRA has almost 4 million more trainable parameters than the LLaMA-65B model with 4-bit PEQA.
qa.explanation: The number of trainable parameters for each model is indicated by the size of the circle in the plot in part (b) of the figure. The larger the circle, the more trainable parameters the model has. We can see that the circle for the LLaMA-65B model with LoRA is larger than the circle for the LLaMA-65B model with 4-bit PEQA, which indicates that the LLaMA-65B model with LoRA has more trainable parameters.
qa.student: The LLaMA-65B model with LoRA has more trainable parameters than the LLaMA-65B model with 4-bit PEQA.
qa.feedback: You correctly recognized which method has more trainable parameters, but you left out the specific values shown in the figure. The question asks for a comparison, which here requires numbers: LoRA uses 10.49M trainable parameters, while 4-bit PEQA uses 6.8M. Including these values makes your answer precise and fully supported by the figure.

qa.question: Which LLaMA model has the highest epoch value?
qa.answer: LLaMA 30B
qa.explanation: The table in the figure shows the hyperparameters for each LLaMA model. The epoch value for LLaMA 30B is 5, which is the highest value among all the models.

From the table, some models (LLaMA 30B and LLaMA2 70B) are trained for 5 epochs, which is higher than 3. So 7B does not have the highest epoch value; instead, you should compare all the epoch entries and see that 5 is the maximum.

qa.question: Which method performs the best on the PIQA dataset in the zero-shot setting?
qa.answer: LLaMA + PEQA with 30 billion parameters.
qa.explanation: The table shows the performance of different methods on the PIQA dataset in both zero-shot and five-shot settings. In the zero-shot setting, LLaMA + PEQA with 30 billion parameters achieves the highest score of 80.3.
qa.student: The PEQA method with 30B parameters performs the best on PIQA in the zero-shot setting.
qa.feedback: You are mixing up “method” with “model size.” The table separates methods (LLaMA, +LoRA, +PEQA) from parameter counts (7B, 13B, 30B). For PIQA in the zero-shot block, you should compare across the three methods for models of the same size, then pick which method achieves the highest score overall. The best PIQA score in zero-shot is achieved by LLaMA with LoRA at 30B, not by PEQA. Your confusion comes from treating “30B PEQA” as a separate method rather than as the same method (PEQA) at a particular size.

2305.17225v2-Figure5-1.png.caption: Typical cases in the four definitions of CauCA in Defn. E.2. Each row corresponds to a dataset in which there is one perfect intervention target: Cloud (C), Sprinkler (S), Rain (R), and Wet grass (W). We consider two latent CBN models with their nodes corresponding to the same nodes in V (G), with intervention targets (τi)i∈[5] and (τ ′i)i∈[5]. Suppose we are given G and (τi)i∈[5]. The mapping V (G′) −→ V (G) are unknown in all settings except with known intervention targets. We give one example of mapping V (G′) −→ V (G) for each of the 4 settings in Defn. E.2.

2305.17225v2-Figure1-1.png.caption: Causal Component Analysis (CauCA). We posit that observed variables X are generated through a nonlinear mapping f , applied to unobserved latent variables Z which are causally related. The causal structure G of the latent variables is assumed to be known, while the causal mechanisms Pi(Zi | Zpa(i)) and the nonlinear mixing function are unknown and to be estimated. (Known or observed quantities are highlighted in red.) CauCA assumes access to multiple datasets Dk that result from stochastic interventions on the latent variables.

2305.17225v2-Table1-1.png.caption: Overview of identifiability results. For the DAG Z1 −→ Z2 −→ Z3 from Fig. 1, we summarise the guarantees provided by our theoretical analysis in § 4.1 for representations learnt by maximizing the likelihoods Pk θ(X) for different sets of interventional regimes.

2305.17225v2-Figure3-1.png.caption: We use the “ ” symbol together with a “times” symbol to represent how many interventions are required by the two assumptions. (Left) (Thm. 4.5) For Asm. 4.4, we need nk interventions to get block-identification of zτk . (Right) (Prop. 4.9) For the block-variability assumption, we need 2nk to get to elementwise identification up to scaling and permutation.

2305.17225v2-Table2-1.png.caption: Overview of ICA identifiability results. For the trivial DAG over Z1, Z2, Z3 (i.e., no edges), we summarise our identifiability results (§ 4.2) for representations learnt by maximizing the likelihoods Pk θ(X) based on multiple interventional regimes. π[·] denotes a permutation.

2305.17225v2-Figure6-1.png.caption: Experimental results with nonparametric model. The figure shows the mean correlation coefficients (MCC) between true and learned latents for Causal Component Analysis (CauCA) experiments. The first two violin plots show the fully nonparametric model when the ground truth latent CBN is generated by a locationscale model and for the case with a linear SCM generating the ground truth CBN. Misspecified models assuming a linear encoder function class and a naive (single-environment) unidentifiable normalizing flow with an independent Gaussian base distribution (labelled i.i.d.) are compared. The misspecified models are trained on a location-scale CBN. All violin plots show the distribution of outcomes for 10 pairs of CBNs and mixing functions.

2305.17225v2-Figure4-1.png.caption: Experimental results. Figures (a) and (e) present the mean correlation coefficients (MCC) between true and learned latents and log-probability differences between the model and ground truth (∆ log prob.) for CauCA experiments. Misspecified models assuming a trivial graph (E(G)=∅) and a linear encoder function class are compared. All violin plots show the distribution of outcomes for 10 pairs of CBNs and mixing functions. Figures (c) and (d) display CauCA results with varying numbers of nonlinearities in the mixing function and latent dimension. For the ICA setting, MCC values and log probability differences are illustrated in (b) and (f). Baselines include a misspecified model (linear mixing) and a naive (single-environment) unidentifiable normalizing flow with an independent Gaussian base distribution (labelled i.i.d.). The naive baseline is trained on pooled data without using information about interventions and their targets. Figure (g) shows the median MCC for CauCA and the misspecified baseline (E(G)=∅) as the strength of the linear parameters relative to the exogenous noise in the structural causal model generating the CBN increases. The shaded areas show the range between minimum and maximum values.

2305.17225v2-Figure2-1.png.caption: Violation of the Interventional Discrepancy Assumption. The shown distributions constitute a counterexample to identifiability that violates Asm. 4.1 and thus allows for spurious solutions, see App. C for technical details. (Left) Visualisation of the joint distributions of two independent latent components z1 and z2 after no intervention (red), and interventions on z1 (green) and z2 (blue). As can be seen, each distribution reaches the same plateau on some rectangular interval of the domain, coinciding within the red square. (Center/Right) Within the red square where all distributions agree, it is possible to apply a measure preserving automorphism which leaves all distributions unchanged, but non-trivially mixes the latents. The right plot shows a distance-dependent rotation around the centre of the black circle, whereas the middle plot show a reference identity transformation.

qa.question: Which intervention target results in the same outcome for both latent CBN models in all datasets?
qa.answer: Wet grass (W)
qa.explanation: The table shows that for all datasets, the outcome for both latent CBN models is the same when the intervention target is Wet grass (W).
qa.student: The intervention target “Cloud (C)” results in the same outcome for both latent CBN models in all datasets.
qa.feedback: You misread the table of intervention targets. To see which target is the same for both models across all datasets, you need to look for a column where τ and the corresponding τ′ entry match in every row. In the table, Sprinkler (S) and Rain (R) differ between τ and τ′ in some datasets, and Cloud (C) also differs (for example, in dataset D4 τ = R while τ′(ii) = C). Only Wet grass (W) matches in every dataset where it appears (D5: τ = W and all τ′ columns = W). Therefore, Wet grass, not Cloud, is the intervention target that results in the same outcome for both latent CBN models in all datasets.

qa.question: Which dataset in the figure is generated from the causal structure where Z1 and Z3 are intervened upon?
qa.answer: Dataset D3.
qa.explanation: The figure shows four datasets, each with a different causal structure. In dataset D3, Z1 and Z3 are intervened upon, as indicated by the hammers.
qa.student: It is generated from dataset D3.
qa.feedback: You correctly picked one intervened variable (Z3 in D3), but you ignored that the question asks for the dataset where both Z1 and Z3 are intervened upon. In the figure, each dataset panel shows which Zi have a hammer icon on them: D1 intervenes on Z1, D2 on Z2, and D3 on Z3 only. There is no dataset shown where interventions occur on both Z1 and Z3 together, and you needed to note this explicitly.

qa.question: How many interventions are required per node for the representation [h_1(z_1), h_2(z_2), h_3(z_2, z_3)] to be identifiable according to Proposition D.1?
qa.answer: One intervention per node for z_1 and z_2, plus 2 x 3 = 6 imperfect interventions on z_3.
qa.explanation: The table in the figure shows that for the representation [h_1(z_1), h_2(z_2), h_3(z_2, z_3)] to be identifiable, we need one intervention per node for z_1 and z_2, plus 2 x 3 imperfect interventions on z_3 with the "variability" assumption. This is stated in Proposition D.1.
qa.student: According to Proposition D.1, you only need one intervention per node to identify the representation [h_1(z_1), h_2(z_2), h_3(z_2, z_3)].
qa.feedback: You are mixing up the different intervention requirements listed in the table. The row labeled “Prop. D.1” does not say “1 intervention per node”; it specifies “1 intervention per node for z1 and z2, plus |pa(3)|(|pa(3)|+1) = 2×3 imperfect interventions on z3 with ‘variability’ assumption.” For node z3, this works out to 6 interventions, not 1 per node. Your error is conceptual: you assumed the identifiability condition in Proposition D.1 was the same as in the first row, instead of recognizing that z3 needs a larger, specific number of interventions due to its parents in the DAG.

qa.question: What is the difference between the two assumptions shown in the figure?
qa.answer: The left assumption (Asm. 4.4) requires fewer interventions to achieve block-identification of zτk than the right assumption (Prop. 4.9), which requires twice as many interventions to achieve elementwise identification up to scaling and permutation.
qa.explanation: The figure shows two different assumptions about the relationship between the variables zτk and z'τk. The left assumption (Asm. 4.4) requires nk interventions to achieve block-identification of zτk, while the right assumption (Prop. 4.9) requires 2nk interventions to achieve elementwise identification up to scaling and permutation. This difference is shown in the figure by the number of hammers next to each arrow.
qa.student: The first assumption (left) needs twice as many interventions overall compared to the second assumption (right). Under the first assumption you only get elementwise identification of each variable, while under the second, stronger assumption you only get block-level identification.
qa.feedback: You reversed what each assumption achieves and misread the intervention counts. The left panel (Asm. 4.4) requires nk interventions and yields only block-identification of zτk, not elementwise identification. The right panel (block-variability assumption) actually needs more interventions, 2nk, but in return it gives finer, elementwise identification (up to scaling and permutation), not just block-level. So the stronger assumption is on the right, and it both uses more interventions and gives more detailed identifiability.

qa.question: What is the minimum number of interventions needed to identify the learned representation  if we assume the "variability" assumption?
qa.answer: 6 fat-hand interventions.
qa.explanation: The table shows that if we assume the "variability" assumption, we need 6 fat-hand interventions on  to identify the learned representation.
qa.student: The minimum number of interventions is 6 fat-hand interventions on (z1, z2, z3) with the variability assumption.
qa.feedback: You correctly noticed a setting with the variability assumption, but you missed that there is another row that also assumes “variability” and uses fewer interventions. The table shows that with the variability assumption, only 1 intervention on z1 and 4 fat-hand interventions on (z2, z3) are needed (total 5 interventions), which is fewer than 6. You need to scan all rows that mention the same assumption and then pick the one with the minimum number of interventions.

qa.question: Which model performed the best in terms of the mean correlation coefficient (MCC)?
qa.answer: CauCA with a local scale model.
qa.explanation: The violin plot for CauCA with a local scale model has the highest median MCC value, and its distribution is also shifted towards higher values compared to the other models.
qa.student: The linear model performed the best because its violin plot is the tallest compared to the others.
qa.feedback: You are misinterpreting what “tallest” means in a violin plot. The vertical axis is MCC, so higher positions on this axis mean better performance, not the vertical spread/height of the violin itself. The spread only shows variability. The black bar/marker inside each violin indicates the mean MCC. For both CauCA variants (loc. scale and lin. SCM), this mean is near the top of the axis (around 0.9–0.95), whereas the linear and i.i.d. models have means around 0.5–0.6. Thus, CauCA (especially with linear SCM) performs best, not the linear model.

qa.answer: CauCA performs better when the signal-to-noise ratio in the structural causal model is low.

qa.student: CauCA performs better when the signal-to-noise ratio in the structural causal model is low.
qa.feedback: Your answer is correct. Great job!

qa.question: How does the figure illustrate the violation of the Interventional Discrepancy Assumption?
qa.answer: The figure shows that it is possible for two different latent variable models to have the same observed distribution, even after interventions are performed. This violates the Interventional Discrepancy Assumption, which states that different latent variable models should have different observed distributions after interventions.
qa.explanation: The left panel of the figure shows the joint distribution of two independent latent variables, z1 and z2, after no intervention (red), and interventions on z1 (green) and z2 (blue). All three distributions reach the same plateau on some rectangular interval of the domain, coinciding within the red square. The center and right panels of the figure show two different latent variable models that have the same observed distribution within the red square. This demonstrates that it is possible for different latent variable models to have the same observed distribution, even after interventions are performed.
qa.student: The figure shows that the distributions under no intervention and under interventions on z1 and z2 are completely different everywhere, so any automorphism would change the distributions and thus the Interventional Discrepancy Assumption is satisfied rather than violated.
qa.feedback: You are misreading how the distributions overlap. In the left panel, the red (no intervention), green (intervention on z1), and blue (intervention on z2) surfaces all reach the same plateau over a shared rectangular region (the red square). This means the three interventional distributions are identical there, not “completely different everywhere.” Because they coincide on that region, the center/right panels show that a nontrivial, distance‑dependent rotation (a measure‑preserving automorphism) can mix z1 and z2 inside the circle while leaving all these distributions unchanged. That invariance under mixing is exactly what violates the Interventional Discrepancy Assumption and leads to non‑identifiability.

qa.question: Why did the model over-correct the second test input?
qa.answer: The model over-corrected the second test input because it was trained on data that included the correction of "stiff" to "sound".
qa.explanation: The figure shows that the model was trained on a pair of sentences, one of which included the correction of "stiff" to "sound". When the model encountered the test input "The bird I bought sounds stiff", it incorrectly predicted that the sentence should be corrected to "The bird I bought sounds sound". This is because the model had learned to associate the word "stiff" with the correction "sound".
qa.student: Because the model thought the word “stiff” was wrong and changed it to “sound.”
qa.feedback: You only mention that the model changed “stiff” to “sound,” but you do not explain why this happened. The key point is that in training, BERT repeatedly saw corrections where “stiff” in Chinese (生硬) was changed to “sound” (声音), so at test time it wrongly treated the correct sentence with “stiff” as if it contained an error of the same type and “corrected” it again. Your answer misses this connection between the training examples and the over-correction behavior.

qa.question: How did adding MFT affect the performance of the different models on the CAR dataset?
qa.answer: Adding MFT improved the performance of all the models on the CAR dataset. For example, the F1 score for BERT increased from 47.6 to 52.3, and the F1 score for Soft-Masked increased from 47.7 to 52.0.
qa.explanation: The table shows the F1 scores for different models on the CAR dataset, both with and without MFT. The values in the "w/ MFT" rows are all higher than the corresponding values in the rows without MFT, indicating that adding MFT improved the performance of all the models.
qa.student: Adding MFT generally hurt the performance on the CAR dataset, since the CAR scores with MFT are mostly lower than without MFT for all models.
qa.feedback: You are reversing the direction of the effect. For CAR, higher F1 means better performance. In the table, every model’s CAR score increases when MFT is added (e.g., BERT from 47.6 to 52.3, Soft-Masked from 47.7 to 52.0, etc.). qa.question: What does the gradient tell us about the importance of each token?
qa.answer: The gradient indicates how much the model's prediction changes when the embedding of a token is changed. A higher gradient indicates that the token is more important for the model's prediction.
qa.explanation: The table shows the gradient for each token embedding. The tokens with the highest gradients are "年纪" (age) and "谢顶" (shedding of hair), which suggests that these tokens are the most important for the model's prediction.
qa.student: The gradient values show that all tokens are equally important, since they all have small magnitudes close to zero in both FT and MFT, so the model is not focusing on any specific character.
qa.feedback: You misread how importance is reflected in the table. Higher gradient magnitude means the model is more sensitive to that token’s embedding and thus considers it more important. In MFT, “谢” (1.16) and “顶” (0.92) have much larger gradients than other tokens, so they are the most important. In FT, “轻” (0.19) has the highest gradient. The values are not all “equally small”; there are clear differences that indicate varying token importance.

qa.question: How does the performance of the fine-tuned model compare to that of the vanilla BERT model on unseen edit pairs?

For EXC, the fine-tuned model’s precision, recall, and F1 (10.7, 4.4, 6.3) are dramatically worse than vanilla BERT’s (46.3, 45.0, 45.6). Fine-tuning has overfit to seen edits and crashed on unseen ones, so you should conclude that the fine-tuned model performs much worse than vanilla BERT on unseen edit pairs.

qa.question: Which fine-tuning method leads to faster convergence of the model?

qa.feedback: You are focusing on the gradient norm, but the question is about convergence, which is better read from the F1 plot (panel b). Higher gradient norm values (y‑axis in panel a) do not directly mean faster convergence. In panel b, the dashed red curve (fine-tune) reaches its plateau F1 earlier in terms of steps than the solid blue curve (masked-fine-tune). This means the standard fine-tune method actually converges faster, even though its gradient norms are generally lower.

qa.question: What are the differences between the outputs generated by the source, target, and Masked-FT models?

However, you misunderstand how closely Masked-FT matches the target. In the first example, the source and Masked-FT both use the same incorrect character (marked in red), whereas the target uses a different, corrected character (in blue). This shows Masked-FT fails to correct that error and instead copies the source. In the second example, Masked-FT matches the target’s corrected character (blue), differing from the source’s wrong one (red). So Masked-FT does not always perfectly fix errors; sometimes it leaves them unchanged and sometimes it corrects them.

qa.question: What are the second-level relations of PCC?

In PDTB 3.0, Contingency.Pragmatic cause is replaced by Contingency.Cause+Belief, and there are additional Expansion types that are not in PDTB 2.0: Expansion.Purpose, Expansion.Equivalence, Expansion.Level-of-detail, Expansion.Manner, and Expansion.Substitution. Carefully compare both L2 lists column by column instead of assuming they are identical.

qa.question: What are the differences between the four benchmark datasets shown in the figure?
qa.answer: The four benchmark datasets shown in the figure are Extended Yale B, ORL, Umist, and COIL20. They differ in terms of the number of images, the number of subjects, and the lighting conditions. For example, Extended Yale B has more images and subjects than ORL, and the images in Extended Yale B are taken under more varied lighting conditions than the images in ORL.
qa.explanation: The figure shows example images from each of the four benchmark datasets. By comparing the images, we can see the differences in the number of images, the number of subjects, and the lighting conditions.

qa.feedback: You correctly notice that image appearance varies, but you are misunderstanding the fundamental nature of the datasets. Three of them (Extended Yale B, ORL, Umist) are human face datasets with different pose and illumination variations (e.g., Yale B has strong lighting changes; ORL shows mostly frontal to slight pose variations; Umist covers a broad range of head poses). COIL20, however, is not a face dataset at all; it contains images of objects (like the toy duck shown) captured from different viewpoints. The key differences are in what is being imaged (faces vs. objects) and the type of variations (illumination, pose, viewpoint), not just resolution or image quality.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the self-supervised module in the proposed network?

It then uses this information to learn a representation of the data that is invariant to certain transformations. This representation can then be used for downstream tasks, such as classification or regression.

2307.07512v1-Figure2-1.png.caption: Our monotonic architecture (green) and an unconstrained network (red) trained on two realizations (purple data points) of a one dimensional dataset. The shaded regions are where training data were absent. Each model is trained using 10 random initialization seeds. The dark lines are averages over the seeds, which are each shown as light lines. The unconstrained models exhibit overfitting of the noise, non-monotonic behavior, and highly undesirable and unpredictable results when extrapolating beyond the region occupied by the training data. Conversely, the monotonic Lipschitz models are always monotonic, even in scenarios where the noise is strongly suggestive of non-monotonic behavior. In addition, the Lipschitz constraint produces much smoother models.

2307.07512v1-Figure4-1.png.caption: From Kitouni et al. (2021): True positive rate (efficiency) of each model shown in Figure 3 versus the proper lifetime of the decaying heavy-quark particle selected. The monotonic models produce a nearly uniform efficiency above a few picoseconds at the expense of a few percent lifetime-integrated efficiency. Such a trade off is desirable as explained in the text.

2307.07512v1-Figure3-1.png.caption: From Kitouni et al. (2021): Simplified version of the heavy-quark selection problem using only two inputs, which permits displaying the response everywhere in the feature space; shown here as a heat map with more signal-like (background-like) regions colored blue (red). The dark solid line shows the decision boundary (upper right regions are selected). Shown are (left) a standard fully connected neural network, (middle) a monotonic BDT, and (right) our architecture. The quantities shown on the horiztonal and vertical axes are related to how long the particle lived before decaying and how massive the particle was, respectively.

2307.07512v1-Figure1-1.png.caption: p-norm constrained gradients showing (red) p = 2 and (green) p = 1. The gradient of a function g(x) that is Lipp=2 resides within the dashed red line. For a Lipp=1 function, the boundary is the green dashed line. Note that x is taken to be a row vector. The residual connection (in blue) effectively shifts the possible gradients to strictly positive values and thus enforces monotonicity. Note how the red solid circle does not include all possible gradient configurations. For instance, it does not allow for very small gradients in both inputs, whereas the green square includes all configurations, up to an element-wise maximum of 2λ.
